- For non-dynamic sections, `_canonicalize_section` (lines 159-167) normalizes each value with `_normalize_value(v)` at line 164.
- `_normalize_value` (lines 20-40) only handles SCALARS: bool/int/float (with int-collapse `v==int(v)->int(v)`, round to 10 dp, and repr() for non-finite), and str. For any other type — including list/dict — it falls through to the final bare `return v` at line 40. It does NOT recurse.
- A sibling recursive helper `_normalize_nested` (lines 43-51) exists and IS used on the dynamic-section path (line 154), but NOT on the non-dynamic path (line 164).

Live reproduction (perturb 'speed' section; default `amplitude_quantile: [1.0, 99.0]` at src/odas_tpw/perturb/config.py:147):
- canonical(default) = {"speed":{"amplitude_quantile":[1.0,99.0],"aoa_deg":3,...}}
- canonical(user [1,99]) = {"speed":{"amplitude_quantile":[1,99],"aoa_deg":3,...}}
- compute_hash equal: False.
Note the sibling scalar `aoa_deg` IS collapsed 3.0->3, while the list elements are not — directly demonstrating the asymmetry.

Helper behavior confirms the fix: `_normalize_value([1,99])`->[1,99] vs `_normalize_value([1.0,99.0])`->[1.0,99.0] (different), whereas `_normalize_nested(...)` collapses both to [1,99] (equal) and converts non-finite list elements ([inf,nan]) to ['inf','nan'] strings (avoiding non-standard JSON Infinity/NaN tokens).

Contract violation: tests/test_config_shared.py::test_float_int_normalization (lines 40-43) asserts that scalar 256 and 256.0 hash equal. The same int/float-collapse contract is silently broken for elements inside list/dict-valued keys.

Impact / severity = minor: No data or units corruption, no crash, no incorrect physics. The only consequence is a spurious cache miss in resolve_output_dir (config_base.py:199-235): a user writing `amplitude_quantile: [1, 99]` instead of `[1.0, 99.0]` gets a different output-dir hash than the numerically identical default, so a new sequential versioned directory is created and a needless recompute runs. Results are still correct. The non-finite-in-list case (Infinity/NaN JSON tokens) is a latent secondary issue but unlikely in practice for quantile-style lists.

Suggested fix is correct: replace `_normalize_value(v)` with `_normalize_nested(v)` at config_base.py:164 so list/dict elements receive the same int/float/round/non-finite normalization as scalars.

title: binned/combo directories re-versioned by unrelated binning sub-keys (chi_width churns diss_binned)
description: profiles_binned, diss_binned, and chi_binned are all resolved with the full merged binning section (merge_config('binning', binning_cfg)) as their hashed params. The binning section contains diss_width/diss_aggregation/chi_width/chi_aggregation, which only affect a single downstream product each. Consequently, changing chi_width alone re-hashes (and re-creates) the diss_binned and profiles_binned directories even though the dissipation/profile binning is byte-identical, proliferating versioned dirs and forcing needless rebinning.
evidence: Live: compute_hash('binning', merge_config('binning', {...chi_width:5...})) != compute_hash('binning', merge_config('binning', {...chi_width:9...})) -> True, while only chi_width changed. diss_binned uses this same merged-binning hash (line 1944).
suggested_fix: Hash each binned stage on only the sub-keys it actually consumes (e.g. diss_binned on the resolved diss_width/diss_aggregation/method, chi_binned on chi_width/chi_aggregation/method), not the entire binning section.
verify_reasoning:
VERIFIED REAL against current code on this branch. The three binned-output dirs are each resolved with section="binning" and the ENTIRE merged binning section as the hashed params: profiles_binned (pipeline.py:1927-1934 via merge_config("binning", binning_cfg)), diss_binned (pipeline.py:1940-1946, merge_config("binning", binning_cfg)), and chi_binned (pipeline.py:1958-1964, merge_config("binning", binning_cfg)).

Root cause confirmed in config_base.py:_canonicalize_section (lines 150-167): "binning" is NOT in dynamic_key_sections, so canonicalization takes the base=dict(self.defaults["binning"]) branch which includes every binning key (method, width, aggregation, diss_width, diss_aggregation, chi_width, chi_aggregation), excluding only "diagnostics" (the sole hash_exclude_key, perturb/config.py:245). The upstream chains (_upstream_for, pipeline.py:166-167) also do NOT scope these — diss_binned upstream omits all chi knobs and vice versa. So the section hash itself is the only thing carrying the binning sub-keys, and it carries ALL of them into every binned dir.

Empirically reproduced via the actual _mgr.compute_hash:
- chi_width 5 vs 9, all else equal -> diss_binned/profiles_binned hash DIFFERS (True). Canonical JSON shows chi_width inlined into the diss_binned signature.
- diss_width 2 vs 3 -> chi_binned hash DIFFERS (True).
- chi_aggregation median vs unset -> profiles_binned hash DIFFERS (True).

Consequence: changing chi_width alone re-hashes and re-creates diss_binned_NN and profiles_binned_NN even though the dissipation/profile binning inputs and the consumed sub-keys (diss_width/diss_aggregation, falling back to width/aggregation, used at pipeline.py:1937-1938,1991-1994) are byte-identical. This is a spurious cache miss / versioned-dir proliferation + needless rebin. The same defect is replicated in the combo path (cli.py:342,357 resolve diss_combo/chi_combo/combo with the full merged binning_p) and the CLI binning path (cli.py:277,302).

NOT already_fixed: current code exhibits it. Severity = MINOR: this is purely an output-dir-versioning/caching inefficiency. The binning computations themselves use the correct sub-keys (diss_width/diss_agg, chi_width/chi_agg), so results are numerically correct — there is no data corruption, no wrong physics, no crash, no QC-gate breakage. The suggested fix (hash each binned/combo stage on only the sub-keys it consumes, e.g. by canonicalizing a reduced param dict per product) is sound. || CONFIRMED REAL and NOT fixed on the current branch.

Code trace (src/odas_tpw/perturb/pipeline.py):
- profiles_binned dir is resolved with `merge_config("binning", binning_cfg)` (the full merged binning section) at line 1927-1934.
- diss_binned dir is resolved with `merge_config("binning", binning_cfg)` at line 1944.
- chi_binned dir is resolved with `merge_config("binning", binning_cfg)` at line 1962.
- All three combo dirs (combo, diss_combo, chi_combo) are resolved with the same full `binning_p = merge_config("binning", ...)` at _run_combo lines 2072-2076 and 2097.

The binning section defaults (src/odas_tpw/perturb/config.py:166-175) contain method, width, aggregation, diss_width, diss_aggregation, chi_width, chi_aggregation, diagnostics. Only `diagnostics` is in `hash_exclude_keys` (config.py:245). So chi_width/chi_aggregation/diss_width/diss_aggregation are all part of every binned/combo hash.

But each stage only actually consumes a subset:
- profiles binning (pipeline.py:1971-1985) uses generic `bin_width`/`aggregation` only.
- diss binning (1989-1999) uses `diss_width`/`diss_agg` only (lines 1937-1938).
- chi binning (2003-2013) uses `chi_width`/`chi_agg` only (lines 1949-1954).

Reproduction (run against the repo venv): compute_hash for the binning section with chi_width=5 differs from chi_width=9 (returned True), and changing chi_aggregation alone changes the binning hash (returned True). Because diss_binned/profiles_binned/diss_combo/combo all key on that same whole-section hash, changing chi_width (or chi_aggregation) alone re-hashes and re-creates the dissipation and profile binned/combo directories even though their binned output is byte-identical. Note: `_canonicalize_section` re-overlays params on full defaults, so every hash always reflects all non-excluded keys regardless of merge_config's None-dropping — confirming the cross-contamination is unavoidable with the current whole-section keying.

Impact: This is an output-directory-proliferation / spurious-cache-miss inefficiency, NOT data corruption — the binned/combo data written is still correct; it just lands in unnecessary new versioned dirs and forces redundant re-binning of identical inputs, leaving stale dirs behind. Hence severity is minor, matching the report.

Suggested fix is sound: key each binned/combo stage on only the sub-keys it consumes (profiles on method/width/aggregation; diss on method/diss_width(or width)/diss_aggregation(or aggregation); chi on method/chi_width(or fallbacks)/chi_aggregation(or fallbacks)) rather than the entire merged binning section.

title: Malformed config section (list or scalar) raises opaque TypeError instead of a clear validation error
description: load_config builds config via `{str(k): (dict(v) if v is not None else {}) ...}`. If a YAML section is written as a list or scalar rather than a mapping (e.g. `epsilon: 42` or an accidental list under a section), dict(v) raises `TypeError: 'int' object is not iterable` / `object is not iterable` before validate_config runs, giving the user no indication of which section is malformed.
evidence: Repro with epsilon: 42 -> TypeError 'int' object is not iterable; with a list-valued section -> TypeError object is not iterable (both raised from the dict(v) comprehension at line 95).
suggested_fix: Check isinstance(v, dict|Mapping) per section before dict(v) and raise a ValueError naming the offending section, consistent with the other validation messages.
verify_reasoning:
Verified against current code at /Users/pat/tpw/turbulence/src/odas_tpw/config_base.py:95. The line reads: `config = {str(k): (dict(v) if v is not None else {}) for k, v in raw.items()}`, and validate_config() (which raises clear ValueErrors naming the section) only runs on line 96, AFTER this comprehension. So a malformed section raises a low-level error from dict(v) before any user-friendly validation.

End-to-end reproduction through the real public load_config (rsi/config.py exports `load_config = _mgr.load_config`; perturb/config.py wraps it):
- `epsilon: 42` -> TypeError: 'int' object is not iterable (matches the finding exactly).
- A list-valued section (`epsilon: [foo, bar]`) -> ValueError: 'dictionary update sequence element #0 has length 3; 2 is required'. Note: the finding's evidence claims this raises `TypeError: object is not iterable`. That specific message only occurs for a list of single-element/non-iterable items like `[1,2,3]` (dict([1,2,3]) -> 'object is not iterable'); a list of strings gives a ValueError instead. So the finding's exact list-case error string is slightly over-specified, but the core claim holds: an opaque Python-level error is raised with no indication of which section is malformed.

This is a genuine, provable error-handling/UX gap in current code, not a style preference and not already fixed. However it is correctly the lowest severity (nit): it only triggers on operator-malformed YAML, never on valid input; there is no data/units corruption, no silent wrong result, and no crash on valid data — the exception is still raised promptly at load time so processing never proceeds with bad config. The only deficiency is the unhelpful message. The suggested fix (isinstance(v, Mapping) check per section raising a ValueError naming the offending section) is appropriate and consistent with the existing validation messages. || CONFIRMED against current code on this branch.

At /Users/pat/tpw/turbulence/src/odas_tpw/config_base.py:95, load_config builds the config via `{str(k): (dict(v) if v is not None else {}) for k, v in raw.items()}`. There is no isinstance/Mapping guard before dict(v), and validate_config (which produces the clear section-naming error messages at lines 103-114) only runs AFTER this comprehension at line 96.

Reproduced with the actual manager (odas_tpw.rsi.config._mgr):
- `epsilon: 42` (scalar section) -> TypeError: 'int' object is not iterable, raised from config_base.py:95
- `epsilon: [1, 2]` (list section) -> TypeError: object is not iterable, raised from config_base.py:95
Neither message names the offending section.

Not guarded elsewhere: the only nearby isinstance(dict) checks are in _normalize_value (line 49, unrelated) and perturb/config.py:264 (_validate_instruments), which validates only inner per-instrument settings. The perturb entry point (_load_config_with_instruments_check, line 284) calls _mgr.load_config FIRST, so a top-level non-mapping section in a perturb config fails at config_base.py:95 too, before _validate_instruments runs.

Impact is low and matches the nit classification: it degrades the error message for a hand-edited/malformed YAML section (no section named in the TypeError) but does not corrupt data, alter physics, or crash an otherwise-valid pipeline. The fix is trivial: add `isinstance(v, Mapping)` per-section check and raise a ValueError naming the section, consistent with the existing validation messages.

title: fft_length: null in config crashes diss_length_seconds computation with TypeError
description: diss_length_samples = float(eps_cfg.get('diss_length') or 4 * eps_cfg.get('fft_length', 256)). If the user explicitly writes epsilon.fft_length: null in YAML, load_config keeps the key with value None (it only substitutes {} for None at the section level, not for inner keys), so eps_cfg.get('fft_length', 256) returns None (key present), and 4 * None raises TypeError, aborting the whole file's processing inside process_file. The chi path (line 1164-1165) has the same exposure.
evidence: Repro: eps_cfg={'fft_length': None}; float(eps_cfg.get('diss_length') or 4*eps_cfg.get('fft_length',256)) -> TypeError: unsupported operand type(s) for *: 'int' and 'NoneType'.
suggested_fix: Resolve via merge_config (which drops None values, restoring the default) or coerce: fft_length = eps_cfg.get('fft_length') or 256.
verify_reasoning:
VERIFIED REAL and not already fixed on this branch.

Code trace (current code on fix/audit-pr73):
- src/odas_tpw/perturb/pipeline.py:1138 sets `eps_cfg = config.get("epsilon", {})` — the RAW config section, NOT routed through merge_config.
- Line 1162: `diss_length_samples = float(eps_cfg.get("diss_length") or 4 * eps_cfg.get("fft_length", 256))`. Lines 1164-1165 are the identical chi expression with default 512.
- These lines sit at process_file's top-level body (verified indentation: nothing between line 1136 and 1168 is inside a try/except), so they run unconditionally after profile extraction — even when only the profiles stage is requested.

Why None survives to this point:
- config_base.py:95 `load_config` does `dict(v)` per section; it strips a None *section* to `{}` but does NOT strip inner None *values*. So YAML `epsilon: {fft_length: null}` yields `config["epsilon"] = {"fft_length": None}` with the key present.
- validate_config (config_base.py:99-115) accepts it: confirmed `fft_length` and `diss_length` are valid keys in both the `epsilon` and `chi` defaults, so the config loads without error.
- cli.py:28-33 `_load_and_merge` returns `load_config(...)` directly and passes it straight to run_pipeline -> process_file with no merge_config on the epsilon/chi sections. (The merge_config calls at pipeline.py:122-123 are only used inside _upstream_for for hashing, not for the eps_cfg actually consumed at 1162.)

Failure mechanism: `eps_cfg.get("diss_length")` is None (falsy) -> RHS evaluated; `eps_cfg.get("fft_length", 256)` returns None because the key is present -> `4 * None` -> `TypeError: unsupported operand type(s) for *: 'int' and 'NoneType'`.

Reproduced end-to-end with the real loader for BOTH paths:
- epsilon (cfg `epsilon: {fft_length: null}`): TypeError unsupported operand type(s) for *: 'int' and 'NoneType'.
- chi (cfg `chi: {fft_length: null}`): same TypeError.

Impact: The TypeError is uncaught within process_file. In single-job mode (jobs==1, pipeline.py:1843-1856 — no try/except around _process_file_timed) it propagates out of run_pipeline and aborts the entire run. In multiprocess mode (jobs>1, 1883-1889) it is caught per-future and logged, so only that file silently produces no output. Either way the documented contract (config_base.py:127-129: "None values ... treated as not specified") is violated for these two lines because they bypass merge_config.

Severity minor: triggering requires the user to explicitly write `fft_length: null` (with `diss_length` absent/null), which is an unusual but legitimate way to request the default; the consequence is a cryptic crash / aborted run rather than data corruption. Suggested fix is sound: coerce e.g. `eps_cfg.get("fft_length") or 256` (and `chi_cfg.get("fft_length") or 512`), or route the section through merge_config which drops None values.

Relevant files: /Users/pat/tpw/turbulence/src/odas_tpw/perturb/pipeline.py:1162 and :1164-1165; /Users/pat/tpw/turbulence/src/odas_tpw/config_base.py:95; /Users/pat/tpw/turbulence/src/odas_tpw/perturb/cli.py:28-33. || VERIFIED REAL, NOT FIXED on this branch.

Trace through the actual current code:

1. CLI builds config via _load_and_merge (src/odas_tpw/perturb/cli.py:28-34), which calls load_config and returns its result directly. There is NO per-section merge_config pass that would strip None inner keys.

2. load_config (src/odas_tpw/config_base.py:84-97) at line 95 does `{str(k): (dict(v) if v is not None else {}) for k, v in raw.items()}`. The `if v is not None else {}` substitution is at the SECTION level only. Inner keys (e.g. fft_length) keep their loaded value, so `epsilon.fft_length: null` in YAML yields config["epsilon"]["fft_length"] == None with the key PRESENT.

3. validate_config (config_base.py:99-115) only rejects unknown keys; fft_length and diss_length are both valid keys in the epsilon and chi defaults (src/odas_tpw/perturb/config.py:84-85, 108-109), so `fft_length: null` passes validation. None values are not rejected.

4. In process_file, eps_cfg = config.get("epsilon", {}) (pipeline.py:1138) and chi_cfg = config.get("chi", {}) (:1142) are these RAW dicts.

5. pipeline.py:1162: `diss_length_samples = float(eps_cfg.get("diss_length") or 4 * eps_cfg.get("fft_length", 256))`. The default diss_length is None (config.py:85), so eps_cfg.get("diss_length") is None (falsy) and the `or` evaluates the right operand `4 * eps_cfg.get("fft_length", 256)`. Because the key is present with value None, .get returns None, giving `4 * None` -> TypeError. The chi path at pipeline.py:1164-1165 has identical exposure (chi default diss_length is also None, config.py:109).

Reproduced exactly:
  eps_cfg={'fft_length': None}
  float(eps_cfg.get('diss_length') or 4*eps_cfg.get('fft_length',256))
  -> TypeError: unsupported operand type(s) for *: 'int' and 'NoneType'

6. Lines 1162-1167 sit at process_file's function body level, OUTSIDE any try/except (the preceding try block closed at pipeline.py:1133-1135 with its own `return result`). The per-profile try/except (line 1172) is reached only AFTER line 1162. So the TypeError propagates out of process_file. In jobs==1 mode (pipeline.py:1846) process_file is called with no surrounding try/except, so the exception aborts the ENTIRE run_pipeline, not just the one file. In multi-job mode it surfaces via future.result().

Suggested fix is sound: coerce with `fft_length = eps_cfg.get("fft_length") or 256` (and likewise for chi with 512), or route the epsilon/chi sections through merge_config which strips None (config_base.py:146).

Severity: minor. The crash requires the user to explicitly write `fft_length: null` in YAML, which is unusual — the template (config.py:390,413) documents fft_length as a number and only documents `null` for diss_length/overlap. Real edge-case config crash, but not on the normal path.

Relevant files: /Users/pat/tpw/turbulence/src/odas_tpw/perturb/pipeline.py:1162,1164-1165; /Users/pat/tpw/turbulence/src/odas_tpw/config_base.py:95; /Users/pat/tpw/turbulence/src/odas_tpw/perturb/cli.py:28-34; /Users/pat/tpw/turbulence/src/odas_tpw/perturb/config.py:84-85,108-109.

title: _apply_fom_cut / _nan_excluded_probes e_N and chi_N masking is dead code (companions don't exist yet)
description: _apply_fom_cut (called at line 1205 BEFORE mk_epsilon_mean at 1206) and _nan_excluded_probes (called at 1203, also before mk_epsilon_mean) both attempt to NaN per-probe companion variables e_{i+1} / chi_{i+1}. But those 1-D companions are first created inside mk_epsilon_mean/mk_chi_mean (epsilon_combine.py:57-60 splits the 2-D epsilon(probe,time) into e_N), so at the call sites they are absent and the `if name in ds.data_vars` / `if e_name in ds.data_vars` branches never fire. The result is still correct because mk_*_mean derives e_N from the already-NaN'd 2-D epsilon, but the companion-masking code is dead and its docstring claims an effect it does not have in this path.
evidence: Call order in process_file: _nan_excluded_probes (1203) -> _apply_fom_cut (1205) -> mk_epsilon_mean (1206). epsilon_combine.py:53-60 shows e_1/e_2 are created only inside mk_epsilon_mean. So at 1203/1205 ds has no e_N vars.
suggested_fix: Either drop the companion-masking branches (rely on the 2-D epsilon mask propagating through mk_*_mean) or move the FOM/exclusion cuts to after mk_*_mean if independent companion masking is genuinely desired.
verify_reasoning:
VERIFIED as a real (but cosmetic) issue. The companion-masking branches in _apply_fom_cut (pipeline.py:443-449) and _nan_excluded_probes (pipeline.py:803-807) are dead code in the only call path.

Trace:
1. Call order in process_file confirmed: epsilon path runs _nan_excluded_probes (1203) -> _apply_fom_cut (1205) -> mk_epsilon_mean (1206); chi path runs _apply_fom_cut (1303) -> mk_chi_mean (1306). All FOM/exclusion cuts run BEFORE mk_*_mean.
2. The datasets passed in are built by _compute_epsilon/_compute_chi via _build_result_dataset (helpers.py:517), which only creates the variables in the explicit spec list. For epsilon that is epsilon/K_max/mad/fom/FM/K_max_ratio/method/speed/nu/P_mean/T_mean/spec_shear/spec_nasmyth/K/F (dissipation.py:426-576) — no e_N. For chi it is chi/epsilon_T/kB/... (chi_io.py:479+) — no chi_N.
3. The 1-D e_N/chi_N companions are FIRST created inside mk_epsilon_mean (epsilon_combine.py:57-60) and mk_chi_mean (chi_combine.py:73-77) by splitting the 2-D epsilon/chi(probe,time). Since those run AFTER the cut functions, at lines 446 (`if name in ds.data_vars`) and 806 (`if e_name in ds.data_vars`) the dataset has no e_N/chi_N, so the inner branches never fire. Each profile iteration builds a fresh dataset, so there is no stale-state path that pre-populates them.

Impact is nil for data correctness: both functions correctly NaN the 2-D epsilon/chi (pipeline.py:436-440, 802), and mk_*_mean derives e_N/chi_N from the already-masked 2-D array via .isel(probe=i), so the mask still propagates into the geometric mean. The companion-masking code is unreachable, and the docstrings (416-418, 783-784, 803-804) describe an effect that does not occur in this path. The branches are defensively guarded with membership checks, so they are harmless, just dead. Correctly classified nit (dead code + slightly misleading docstrings, no functional/units/data impact). Present in current code; not already fixed on this branch. || Confirmed against current code. Call order in process_file: _nan_excluded_probes (pipeline.py:1203) -> _apply_fom_cut (1205) -> mk_epsilon_mean (1206); and for chi, _apply_fom_cut (1303) -> mk_chi_mean (1306). At both FOM/exclusion call sites the dataset contains only the 2-D epsilon(probe,time)/chi(probe,time) built in rsi/dissipation.py (which defines NO e_N/chi_N vars) and the chi compute path. The 1-D companions e_N are first created inside mk_epsilon_mean (epsilon_combine.py:55-60: `for i in range(...): ds[f"e_{i+1}"] = ds["epsilon"].isel(probe=i)`) and chi_N inside mk_chi_mean (chi_combine.py:71-76). Therefore the companion-masking branches in _apply_fom_cut (pipeline.py:443-449, the `if name in ds.data_vars` loop over e_/chi_ prefixes) and in _nan_excluded_probes (pipeline.py:805-807, `if e_name in ds.data_vars`) never fire in the actual pipeline path — confirmed dead code.

However, this is correctly classified as a nit and is NOT a behavioral bug: the 2-D epsilon/chi masking at pipeline.py:436-440 and 802 DOES execute (shape matches fom and the probe index is valid), and mk_*_mean re-derives e_N/chi_N by isel-splitting the already-NaN'd 2-D array, so the NaN mask propagates correctly to the geometric mean. Output is correct. The defect is purely (a) dead/inert code and (b) a misleading docstring at pipeline.py:780-785 ("any pre-existing 1-D e_N companion variables are masked") and 442 ("1-D companions used by mk_*_mean") that claim an effect not realized in this call path. The branches are defensively-but-needlessly guarded by `in ds.data_vars`, so they cause no crash. is_real=true (the dead-code/misleading-invariant condition exists as described); already_fixed=false (the inert branches and docstrings are still present in the current code).

title: trim_p_file destroys the source file when the trim output resolves to the source path (dest == source)
description: When the computed trim destination equals the source file (e.g. files already located under <output_root>/trimmed/, or root=None with a basename collision), trim_p_file opens the SAME path for read and write: `with open(source,'rb') as src_f, open(dest,'wb') as dst_f: dst_f.write(src_f.read(trimmed_size))`. open(dest,'wb') truncates the file to 0 bytes BEFORE src_f.read() executes, so the read returns empty and the file is wiped. There is no dest==source / os.path.samefile guard, and _check_unique_outputs in pipeline.py only checks destination-vs-destination uniqueness, not destination-vs-source.
evidence: Reproduced on a synthetic .p file (header_size=128, config_size=64, record_size=256, 2 full records + 100-byte fragment = 804 bytes). Calling trim_p_file(Path('x.p'), Path('.'), root=None) so dest==source: result.action='trimmed', bytes_removed=100, but x.p went from 804 bytes (md5 16e8888...) to 0 bytes (md5 d41d8cd... = empty file). open('wb') truncates before the read.
suggested_fix: Before the read/write block, raise or no-op if dest.resolve()==source.resolve() (or os.path.samefile). Safer: write to a temp file in dest.parent then os.replace() it onto dest, so even an aliased dest is produced from a separate fd and the source is never truncated mid-read.
verify_reasoning:
CONFIRMED real. In src/odas_tpw/perturb/trim.py:171-172 the write block is `with open(source,"rb") as src_f, open(dest,"wb") as dst_f: dst_f.write(src_f.read(trimmed_size))`. open(dest,"wb") truncates dest to 0 bytes; when dest and source are the same physical file, src_f.read() then returns empty and the file is wiped, while TrimResult falsely reports action="trimmed", bytes_removed=N. There is NO dest==source / os.path.samefile guard in trim.py (verified by reading the full file and grepping), and _check_unique_outputs (pipeline.py:1371-1382) only deduplicates destinations against each other, never dest-vs-source.

Reproduced two ways: (1) direct API trim_p_file(src, src.parent, root=None) -> 804B file became 0B (md5 d41d8cd... = empty); (2) through the real run_trim entry point with files.p_file_root set to the trim dir (output_root=results/, p_file_root=results/trimmed/) -> source went 804B -> 0B, run_trim returned the path as if success. trim_destination with root==trim_dir yields rel=name so dest = trim_dir/name == source.

Scope/severity bounding: the write block is only reachable when remainder!=0 (incomplete file); complete files return action="referenced" at line 150 and never write, so only an incomplete (data-bearing) file can be destroyed. It is NOT hit on the default/documented pipeline (VMP/ -> results/trimmed/, distinct paths) nor on ordinary idempotent re-runs (source!=dest; the size+mtime skip guard at 162-168 handles those). It triggers only under an atypical-but-legitimate config where p_file_root resolves onto the trim output dir (e.g. repointing input at a prior run's results/trimmed/), or direct API use with root=None and output_dir==source.parent. That makes it a latent data-loss footgun rather than a default-path blocker -> major.

Fix: write to a temp file in dest.parent then os.replace() onto dest (atomic, source fd independent), or no-op/raise when dest.resolve()==source.resolve() / os.path.samefile before the read/write block. || CONFIRMED real and NOT fixed on branch fix/audit-pr73 (HEAD 1f15fff).

Code trace (current code):
- src/odas_tpw/perturb/trim.py:112 computes dest = trim_destination(source, output_dir, root). trim_destination (lines 49-64) returns `output_dir / source.name` when root is None, or the relative-preserved path under output_dir when root is set. Both can equal the source path.
- The skip-guard at trim.py:162-168 requires `dest.stat().st_size == trimmed_size`; when dest==source the source size (full, e.g. 804) != trimmed_size (e.g. 708), so the guard does NOT short-circuit and execution falls through.
- trim.py:171-172: `with open(source,'rb') as src_f, open(dest,'wb') as dst_f: dst_f.write(src_f.read(trimmed_size))`. open(dest,'wb') truncates the file to 0 bytes; when dest==source this truncates the source BEFORE src_f.read() runs, so read returns empty and the file is wiped. There is no dest==source / os.path.samefile guard anywhere on the path (grep confirms none in trim.py).
- pipeline.py:1371-1382 _check_unique_outputs only detects two DISTINCT inputs mapping to one output (dest-vs-dest), not a source colliding with its own output (source-vs-dest), so it does not catch this.

Reproductions (against current src):
1. Direct API: trim_p_file(Path('x.p'), Path('.'), root=None) on a synthetic 804-byte .p (2 full 256-B records + 100-B fragment): result action='trimmed', bytes_removed=100, but x.p went 804 B (md5 16e8888...) -> 0 B (md5 d41d8cd... = empty).
2. End-to-end via run_trim with config files.p_file_root='results/trimmed', output_root='results' on results/trimmed/foo.p: run_trim returned the path successfully, file went 804 B -> 0 B (empty md5). trim_destination maps source results/trimmed/foo.p to the identical dest, _check_unique_outputs passes (single file), and trim_p_file truncates the source.

Impact: silent, irrecoverable destruction of the raw .p source while reporting action='trimmed' (success). 

Severity major rather than blocker because the default config (p_file_root='VMP/', output_root='results/') has disjoint input/output trees, so the normal happy-path pipeline run is safe; triggering requires the input root/discovery to point at (or include) the results/trimmed/ output directory — i.e. reprocessing already-trimmed files or a non-default input root — or direct API use with root=None and output_dir set to the file's own directory.

Suggested fix is sound: guard `if dest.resolve() == source.resolve(): return TrimResult(source,'referenced',...)` (or os.path.samefile), or write to a temp file in dest.parent and os.replace() onto dest so the source is never opened 'wb'. Cited locations: src/odas_tpw/perturb/trim.py:49-64, 162-168, 171-172; src/odas_tpw/perturb/pipeline.py:1371-1382, 1481-1482.

title: merge_p_files truncates and corrupts the base file when the merge output resolves to chain[0] (dest == chain[0])
description: merge_destination(chain, output_dir, root=None) returns output_dir/chain[0].name, which equals chain[0] when output_dir is the chain's own directory. merge_p_files then does `with open(dest,'wb') as out_f: with open(chain[0],'rb') as in_f: shutil.copyfileobj(in_f,out_f)`. open(dest,'wb') truncates chain[0] to 0 bytes first, so copyfileobj copies nothing from the base file; only the continuation files' data records get appended. The result is a headerless, config-less, unparseable file AND the original base file is destroyed. No dest==source guard exists.
evidence: Reproduced: built a.p (file_number=0, header+config+2 records = 704 bytes) and b.p (file_number=1). merge_p_files([a.p,b.p], Path('VMP'), root=None) with output dir == source dir. Result file VMP/a.p is 512 bytes and its first 8 bytes are b'DDDDDDDD' with set(bytes)=={ord('D')} — i.e. pure data records, no 128-byte header and no config string. Original 704-byte a.p with its header/config is gone. Downstream PFile parsing will mis-slice or reject this.
suggested_fix: Guard against dest.resolve()==chain[0].resolve() (raise or skip), or build the merged file in a temp file in dest.parent and os.replace() it into place after all source reads complete.
verify_reasoning:
VERIFIED REAL mechanism, but severity overstated; downgraded major -> minor due to non-pipeline reachability.

Confirmed by reading current code (src/odas_tpw/perturb/merge.py:132-226) and reproducing empirically. merge_destination(chain, output_dir, root=None) returns output_dir / chain[0].name (line 153). When output_dir == the chain's own directory, dest.resolve() == chain[0].resolve() (reproduced: True). merge_p_files (lines 213-216) does `with open(dest,"wb") as out_f:` then `with open(chain[0],"rb") as in_f: shutil.copyfileobj(...)`. Because Python opens dest in "wb" mode (truncate) BEFORE the nested open of chain[0], and dest IS chain[0], the base file is truncated to 0 bytes first; copyfileobj copies nothing from it. Reproduction: built a.p (file_number=0, header+config+2 records, 465 bytes) and b.p (file_number=1); find_mergeable_files chained them; merge_p_files([a,b], <same dir>, root=None) returned dest==a.p and produced a 320-byte file whose leading bytes are b.p's first data-record header (file_number=1), NOT a record-0 header+config. Original a.p with its header/config is destroyed. (My leading bytes differ from the finding's b'DDDDDDDD' only because my synthetic data payload differs; the structural corruption -- base file destroyed, no record-0 header/config, only continuation data -- is identical.) There is NO dest==source guard in merge_p_files, merge_destination, or plan_merge_outputs. So is_real=true, already_fixed=false.

Severity downgrade rationale: The production caller run_merge (pipeline.py:1517-1568) always passes merge_dir = output_root / "merged" (line 1537/1561), a dedicated subdirectory disjoint from the input p_file_root. With the default/any-reasonable config (output_root="results/", p_file_root="VMP/") the destination always lands under .../merged/... and cannot coincide with a source .p file, so this data-loss path is NOT reachable from the pipeline. All four test usages (tests/test_perturb_merge.py:125,159,177,184) pass a distinct out_dir (e.g. tmp_path/"merged"), never the source dir; docs/PERTURB_PLAN.md describes the same out-dir contract. The bug is only triggered by directly calling the public merge_p_files/merge_destination API with output_dir pointed at the source directory and root=None (or a contrived root mapping the rel path back onto a source file). It is a genuine defensive-guard gap and public-API footgun (in-place merge silently destroys the input with no dest==source check), but the real-world blast radius is small because no shipped code path hits it. The finding's "major / high-confidence data-loss" framing implies normal-operation impact, which is not the case; minor is the correct severity for a real-but-conditionally-reachable, non-pipeline footgun. Suggested fix is sound: guard against dest.resolve()==chain[0].resolve() (raise/skip), or build into a temp file in dest.parent and os.replace() after all source reads complete. || CONFIRMED REAL and NOT fixed. In src/odas_tpw/perturb/merge.py:213-216, merge_p_files opens dest with mode "wb" (which truncates) BEFORE reading chain[0]: `with open(dest,"wb") as out_f: with open(chain[0],"rb") as in_f: shutil.copyfileobj(in_f,out_f)`. merge_destination (lines 132-153) returns `output_dir/chain[0].name` when root=None, which equals chain[0] whenever output_dir is the chain's own directory. There is NO dest==chain[0] guard anywhere in merge.py (verified by grep: no resolve()/samefile comparison between dest and chain[0]). The only collision check in the codebase, _check_unique_outputs (pipeline.py:1371), detects distinct-inputs->same-output, not dest==source, so it does not protect against this.\n\nI reproduced the corruption exactly: built a.p (file_number=0, header+config+2x256-byte records = 657 bytes) and b.p (file_number=1). find_mergeable_files chained them; merge_destination([a,b], VMP, root=None).resolve() == a.resolve() (True). After merge_p_files([a,b], VMP, root=None), VMP/a.p was 512 bytes (only b.p's two 256-byte data records), first bytes b'DDDDDDDD', byte-set {68}='D' only — i.e. pure data, no 128-byte header, no config string. The original 657-byte base file with header/config was destroyed. Downstream PFile parsing would mis-slice or reject it.\n\nReachability caveat (why major, not blocker): the normal pipeline caller run_merge (pipeline.py:1561) uses merge_dir=output_root/'merged' and root=input_root, so with default config (p_file_root=VMP/, output_root=results/) dest=results/merged/... != chain[0] and the bug does NOT fire on the default path. It is reachable via (1) the documented public API merge_p_files(chain, output_dir, root=None) called with output_dir equal to the chain's directory, or (2) misconfiguration where p_file_root points at <output_root>/merged (e.g. re-running on already-merged files). Impact is irreversible in-place destruction of the source .p file with no guard. Suggested fix: guard dest.resolve()==chain[0].resolve() (raise/skip), or write to a temp file in dest.parent and os.replace() after all source reads complete.

title: Hotel pchip interpolation (the default) crashes on any channel containing a fill value / NaN
description: _nc_array / _load_csv deliberately map netCDF _FillValue (and CSV NaN) cells to np.nan (documented as the safe choice so -999 fill values don't become real measurements). The pipeline's default interpolation kind is 'pchip' (config.py:46). But _interp_one's pchip branch calls PchipInterpolator(hotel_t, data, extrapolate=False), and scipy's PchipInterpolator raises ValueError('`y` must contain only finite values.') if data contains ANY NaN. So a hotel NetCDF/CSV with a single fill value in a kept channel aborts merge_hotel_into_pfile (and thus the whole campaign run) with a hard crash, rather than skipping/interpolating across the gap.
evidence: `_interp_one(np.array([0,1,2,3.]), np.array([10.,nan,15.,30.]), np.array([0.5,1.5,2.5]), 'pchip')` raises `ValueError: \`y\` must contain only finite values.`. Default kind confirmed: hotel.py:446 `default_kind = hotel_cfg.get('interpolation','pchip')` and config.py:46 sets 'interpolation':'pchip'. _nc_array (hotel.py:150) returns np.ma.filled(arr.astype(float64), np.nan) so fill values arrive as NaN.
suggested_fix: In _interp_one, drop NaN samples from (hotel_t, data) before building the interpolator (mask = np.isfinite(data); if mask.sum()<2 return all-NaN/edge fill), so fill-valued gaps are interpolated across instead of crashing. Apply the same mask to the linear/interp1d branch.
verify_reasoning:
VERIFIED REAL and NOT fixed in current branch code.

Trace (all line numbers in src/odas_tpw/perturb/hotel.py unless noted):
1. Default interpolation kind is "pchip": config.py:46 sets {"hotel": {"interpolation": "pchip"}}; hotel.py:446 reads `default_kind = hotel_cfg.get("interpolation", "pchip")`; hotel.py:459 `kind = opts.get("interp") or default_kind`.
2. Fill values / NaN reach the data array passed to the interpolator. _nc_array (hotel.py:138-151) intentionally maps masked _FillValue cells to np.nan (line 150: `np.ma.filled(arr.astype(np.float64), np.nan)`); the NetCDF loader keeps that NaN (line 322: `ch[v] = _nc_array(var).astype(np.float64)`); the CSV loader also yields NaN for empty/NaN cells (line 282: `df[c].values.astype(np.float64)`). These populate hotel_data.channels.
3. No input-NaN filtering exists anywhere in the call chain. interpolate_hotel iterates channels and at line 470 calls `_interp_one(hotel_t, data, target_t, kind)` with the raw data. grep for isfinite/isnan/dropna shows the only NaN handling is line 421 `mask = np.isnan(out)` which operates on the OUTPUT of pchip — never reached, because the PchipInterpolator constructor (line 418) raises first.
4. scipy behavior reproduced on the installed scipy 1.17.1: `PchipInterpolator(np.array([0,1,2,3.]), np.array([10.,nan,15.,30.]), extrapolate=False)` raises exactly `ValueError: \`y\` must contain only finite values.` — the message claimed in the finding.
5. Crash propagation: _interp_one -> interpolate_hotel (line 470) -> merge_hotel_into_pfile (line 491) with no try/except in the chain, so a single fill value in any kept channel aborts the merge (and the campaign run).

So: a hotel NetCDF/CSV with even one fill value / NaN in a kept channel hard-crashes the default pchip merge path rather than interpolating across or skipping the gap. The suggested fix (mask `np.isfinite(data)` before building the interpolator, and apply the same to the interp1d branch — which today silently propagates NaN into outputs, e.g. interp1d gave [nan, nan, 22.5]) is correct.

Severity major (not blocker): triggers only when the hotel feature is enabled (config default enable=False) AND telemetry actually contains a fill/NaN in a retained channel — but those are common conditions for real hotel telemetry (gaps are normal), and the effect is a hard abort of the whole campaign run.

File: /Users/pat/tpw/turbulence/src/odas_tpw/perturb/hotel.py lines 408-432 (_interp_one), 456-470 (interpolate_hotel loop), 138-151 (_nc_array), 282/322 (NaN sources); /Users/pat/tpw/turbulence/src/odas_tpw/perturb/config.py:46 (pchip default). || Confirmed against current code on branch fix/audit-pr73 and reproduced the crash.

Trace:
- Default interpolation kind is 'pchip': config.py:46 sets hotel.interpolation='pchip', consumed at hotel.py:446 (default_kind = hotel_cfg.get('interpolation','pchip')) and applied at hotel.py:459 (kind = opts.get('interp') or default_kind).
- Fill/NaN values reach the interpolator: _nc_array (hotel.py:138-151) returns np.ma.filled(arr.astype(float64), np.nan), so NetCDF _FillValue/masked cells become np.nan; _load_csv (hotel.py:282) does df[c].values.astype(np.float64), so any blank/NaN CSV cell stays np.nan. Both populate HotelData.channels, which flow unmodified into interpolate_hotel -> _interp_one (hotel.py:470).
- The pchip branch (hotel.py:417-419) calls PchipInterpolator(hotel_t, data, extrapolate=False); scipy 1.17.1 raises ValueError('`y` must contain only finite values.') on any NaN in data. The only isnan guard (hotel.py:421) acts on the OUTPUT edges for boundary fill, not on the input data. No isfinite/dropna masking exists anywhere in the load->interpolate path (grep confirms).

Reproductions (scipy 1.17.1):
1. _interp_one(np.array([0,1,2,3.]), np.array([10.,nan,15.,30.]), np.array([0.5,1.5,2.5]), 'pchip') -> ValueError: `y` must contain only finite values. (linear branch returns [nan nan 22.5] instead.)
2. End-to-end via public interpolate_hotel() with a single-NaN 'pitch' channel and a minimal PFile stand-in -> same ValueError. The hotel_t.size<2 guard does not catch it because the time grid is full; only one data sample is NaN.

Impact: a hotel NetCDF/CSV with a single fill value in any kept channel hard-crashes merge_hotel_into_pfile and the whole campaign run rather than interpolating across the gap. This is exactly the silent-corruption-avoidance design (mapping fills to NaN) turning into a crash on the default path. Severity major: real data files routinely contain telemetry gaps; the default config triggers it; it aborts processing rather than degrading gracefully. Not a blocker only because hotel merge is opt-in (hotel.enable defaults False) and requires a fill value to be present.

Suggested fix matches the finding: in _interp_one, mask = np.isfinite(data); drop NaN samples from (hotel_t, data) before constructing PchipInterpolator/interp1d; if mask.sum()<2 return an all-NaN/edge-filled array. Apply to both branches so gaps interpolate across instead of crashing.

Relevant files: /Users/pat/tpw/turbulence/src/odas_tpw/perturb/hotel.py:138-151, 408-432, 446, 459, 470; /Users/pat/tpw/turbulence/src/odas_tpw/perturb/config.py:46.

title: NaT datetime values decode to a bogus epoch (~ -9.22e9 s) instead of NaN in GPS and hotel time parsing
description: Both _to_epoch_seconds (gps.py:30) and _parse_time's iso/auto-string branches (hotel.py:166,175) convert datetime64 via `.astype(np.int64) / 1e9`. NaT's int64 representation is -2**63 = -9223372036854775808; divided by 1e9 it becomes -9.22e9 (a finite value, not NaN). So missing GPS/hotel timestamps silently become times near 1678 BCE. In GPSFromNetCDF this corrupts self._t_min = float(np.nanmin(t)) to -9.22e9 (nanmin does NOT skip it because it is finite), so _warn_outside_coverage never warns for early queries, and interp1d gets a node at t=-9.22e9 paired with a real lat/lon, producing wildly wrong extrapolation slopes near the gap.
evidence: _to_epoch_seconds(np.array(['2025-01-01','NaT','2025-01-01'],dtype='datetime64[s]')) returns [1.7356896e9, -9.22337204e9, 1.7356896e9]; the int64 of NaT == np.iinfo(np.int64).min. np.nanmin([1.7356896e9, -9.22e9, ...]) returns -9.22e9 (the sentinel is treated as a real coverage bound). The correct path casts to float64 (NaT->NaN) before the /1e9.
suggested_fix: Cast to float64 (not int64) so NaT maps to NaN: `vals = arr.astype('datetime64[ns]').astype('float64'); return vals / 1e9` (NaN stays NaN). Or explicitly set sentinel positions to NaN where int64 == np.iinfo(np.int64).min.
verify_reasoning:
VERIFIED REAL against current code on this branch.

Bug paths (read directly, not from history):
- src/odas_tpw/perturb/gps.py:30 — `_to_epoch_seconds` does `values.astype("datetime64[ns]").astype(np.int64) / 1e9` for datetime64 inputs.
- src/odas_tpw/perturb/hotel.py:166 (iso) and :175 (auto, non-numeric branch) — `pd.to_datetime(raw_time).values.astype("datetime64[ns]").astype(np.int64) / 1e9`.

Reproduced empirically (numpy 1.x semantics, pandas 3.0.1): NaT's int64 representation is np.iinfo(np.int64).min = -9223372036854775808; /1e9 = -9.223e9, a FINITE value (not NaN). So a missing GPS/hotel timestamp decodes to ~1678 BCE rather than NaN.

Downstream consequence confirmed:
- In GPSFromNetCDF.__init__ (gps.py:304) `self._t_min = float(np.nanmin(t))`. np.nanmin does NOT skip the finite -9.22e9 sentinel, so _t_min is corrupted to -9.22e9. _warn_outside_coverage (gps.py:221-241) then never warns for genuinely-early queries, and interp1d gets a spurious node at t=-9.22e9 paired with a real lat/lon, distorting extrapolation slopes (reproduced: extrapolated lat 15.58 from a 15.0-15.2 dataset). Same hazard for hotel _interp_one (hotel.py:408-432): a -9.22e9 node corrupts the interpolation onto the instrument axis. No NaN/NaT filtering exists before either interp1d construction (only isfinite check is on query times in _warn_outside_coverage, not on stored bounds).

Reachability with realistic inputs (verified):
- GPS CSV does NOT hit this: pd.read_csv yields str/object dtype, so _to_epoch_seconds takes the .astype(float64) else-branch (which would raise on strings) — the datetime64/NaT branch is not reached. NOT a CSV-GPS bug.
- GPS NetCDF DOES hit it for an unmasked datetime64 time variable carrying an embedded NaT and lacking CF "<unit> since <ref>" units (then _decode_cf_times_fast returns None, units falsy, falls to _to_epoch_seconds at gps.py:302). (Masked numeric/datetime vars are correctly NaN-filled by _nc_values, so that sub-case is fine.)
- Hotel CSV/NetCDF DOES hit it: a date column with an empty/missing cell parsed via pd.to_datetime produces NaT and the int64 conversion yields the finite sentinel. pd.to_datetime does not raise on None/empty (produces NaT).

Severity major (silent position/telemetry corruption feeding TEOS-10 salinity, geospatial attrs, and interpolated hotel channels) is justified, though the trigger is narrow (requires NaT to survive as an unmasked datetime64 NaT, not a numeric _FillValue).

IMPORTANT CORRECTION to the finding: its suggested fix is WRONG. I verified that `arr.astype("datetime64[ns]").astype("float64") / 1e9` STILL yields -9.22e9 (NaT does NOT become NaN), because the int64 sentinel passes straight through the float cast. A correct fix must explicitly mask NaT, e.g. via np.isnat() (gps) or pd.isna()/NaT detection (hotel), or test int64 == np.iinfo(np.int64).min, then set those positions to np.nan. This error in the remedy does not invalidate the underlying bug, which is real and present. || CONFIRMED REAL and NOT fixed on the current branch (fix/audit-pr73). The three cited code sites all use `.astype(np.int64) / 1e9`:
- gps.py:30 `_to_epoch_seconds`: `values.astype("datetime64[ns]").astype(np.int64) / 1e9`
- hotel.py:166 (`iso` branch): `pd.to_datetime(raw_time).values.astype("datetime64[ns]").astype(np.int64) / 1e9`
- hotel.py:175 (`auto` non-numeric branch): identical pattern.

Reproduced: NaT's int64 representation is `np.iinfo(np.int64).min == -9223372036854775808`; dividing by 1e9 yields the FINITE value `-9.223372037e9`, not NaN. Verified `np.nanmin([1.7356896e9, -9.22337204e9, 1.7356896e9])` returns `-9.223372037e9` (the sentinel is treated as a real coverage bound because it is finite).

Downstream impact in GPSFromNetCDF/GPSFromCSV (gps.py:192-193, 304-305): `self._t_min = float(np.nanmin(t))` becomes `-9.223e9`. `_warn_outside_coverage` (gps.py:221-241) then computes `lo = nanmin(query) - (t_min - max_time_diff)`, which is large positive for any realistic early query, so `lo < 0` is never true and the out-of-coverage warning silently never fires for early queries. I also confirmed interp1d accepts the sentinel as a node paired with a real lat (e.g. node (t=-9.22e9, lat=11.0)), so interpolation/extrapolation slopes near the gap are corrupted. For hotel, the same NaT->sentinel time then feeds onto the instrument axis. Missing GPS fixes (empty CSV cells parsed by pd.to_datetime -> NaT, or masked/fill datetime64 NetCDF time) realistically trigger this.

No upstream guard strips NaT: _load_csv/_load_netcdf/_nc_values do not filter the time array before _parse_time, and GPSFromNetCDF._nc_values only fills masked numeric data with NaN — it does not address datetime64 NaT sentinels, and the time variable goes through _to_epoch_seconds/_parse_time unfiltered.

One correction to the finding: its suggested_fix is WRONG. I verified that `arr.astype('datetime64[ns]').astype('float64')` does NOT convert NaT to NaN — it produces the same -9.223e9 sentinel. The correct fix needs an explicit `np.isnat()` mask (set those positions to NaN) or a pandas isna-aware conversion. The underlying bug, however, is genuine and present.

Severity major is appropriate: silent corruption of GPS positions and disabling of the coverage warning, but only when missing/NaT timestamps are present in a time column.

title: GPS interpolation with a fill-valued lat/lon node contaminates whole spans with NaN, then seawater silently substitutes lat=0/lon=0
description: GPSFromNetCDF reads lat/lon via _nc_values which maps fill values to NaN (gps.py:271-272), then builds interp1d on those arrays. A single NaN lat/lon node makes interp1d return NaN for the entire interval spanning that node (scipy linear interp through a NaN endpoint yields NaN). Those NaN positions then flow into add_seawater_properties, which replaces NaN lat/lon with 0.0 (lat_safe/lon_safe, seawater.py:50-51) for the gsw SA-from-SP conversion. The net effect: a localized missing GPS fix silently degrades absolute-salinity computation to equator/prime-meridian coordinates over a multi-sample span, with no warning.
evidence: interp1d(np.array([0,1,2,3,4.]), np.array([10.0,10.1,nan,10.3,10.4]), fill_value='extrapolate')([1.5,2.5,2.0]) returns [nan,nan,nan] — one NaN node poisons [1,3]. add_seawater_properties then does np.where(np.isnan(lat),0.0,lat), so those NaN positions become lat=0,lon=0 for gsw.SA_from_SP.
suggested_fix: Drop NaN nodes from (t,lat),(t,lon) before constructing the GPS interpolators (mask with np.isfinite on each pair) so gaps are interpolated across rather than NaN-poisoned; and/or warn (not silently substitute 0) when seawater receives NaN positions.
verify_reasoning:
VERIFIED REAL (minor). The full trace holds against current code on this branch.

1. gps.py:34-48 `_nc_values` maps masked/_FillValue cells to NaN (filled with np.nan after float cast). Confirmed.
2. gps.py:266-272 `GPSFromNetCDF.__init__` reads lat/lon via `_nc_values`, then gps.py:307-308 builds `interp1d(t, lat, ...)` / `interp1d(t, lon, ...)` directly on those arrays with NO np.isfinite masking. The CSV path (gps.py:179-206) likewise builds interp1d on raw df columns without NaN filtering. Confirmed — no upstream node filtering exists.
3. scipy reproduction (scipy 1.17.1): `interp1d(np.array([0,1,2,3,4.]), np.array([10.0,10.1,nan,10.3,10.4]), bounds_error=False, fill_value='extrapolate')` queried at [1.5,2.5,2.0,0.5,3.5] returns [nan,nan,nan,10.05,10.35]. A single NaN node poisons BOTH adjacent intervals [1,2] and [2,3]; a NaN endpoint also poisons extrapolation. Confirmed exactly as the finding's evidence states.
4. ctd.py:349-356 calls `gps.lat(bin_epoch)`/`gps.lon(bin_epoch)` and passes the (now NaN-poisoned) lat/lon into `add_seawater_properties`; seawater.py:50-51 does `np.where(np.isnan(lat),0.0,lat)` (and same for lon), silently substituting lat=0/lon=0 into gsw.SA_from_SP and z_from_p with no warning. Confirmed.

So a localized missing GPS fix silently (a) writes NaN into the output binned["lat"]/["lon"] across a multi-sample span, and (b) degrades the TEOS-10 SA/depth computation to equator/prime-meridian coordinates. No warning is emitted in either case.

SEVERITY = minor (matches the finding). The physical magnitude of the lat=0/lon=0 fallback is tiny: at SP=35, P=100 dbar, SA differs by ~0.0009 g/kg (~0.003%) between Saipan (15N,145E) and (0,0), and z_from_p depth differs by ~0.04 m at 100 dbar. The substitution is documented as matching the Matlab default. The genuine defect is the SILENT span-wide NaN poisoning plus the silent zero-substitution, not a large numeric error. The suggested fix (mask non-finite (t,lat)/(t,lon) pairs before building each interpolator so gaps interpolate across, and/or warn instead of silently substituting 0) is correct and appropriate.

This is distinct from the DEFERRED dateline/antimeridian longitude-interpolation issue in gps.py/ctd.py: that concerns wrap-around across +/-180; this is NaN-node poisoning from missing fixes, a separate and more-specific defect, so it qualifies as a new finding. Relevant files: /Users/pat/tpw/turbulence/src/odas_tpw/perturb/gps.py (lines 34-48, 179-206, 270-308), /Users/pat/tpw/turbulence/src/odas_tpw/perturb/ctd.py (lines 349-356), /Users/pat/tpw/turbulence/src/odas_tpw/perturb/seawater.py (lines 49-58). || VERIFIED REAL and NOT fixed on the current branch (fix/audit-pr73).

Code trace (all read from current source, not history):
1. src/odas_tpw/perturb/gps.py:34-48 — `_nc_values` deliberately maps any masked/_FillValue cell to NaN (`np.ma.filled(arr.astype(float64), np.nan)`). So a missing GPS fix becomes a NaN lat/lon node.
2. gps.py:271-272 — lat/lon arrays are read through `_nc_values`, so they can contain NaN nodes.
3. gps.py:307-308 — `interp1d(t, lat, bounds_error=False, fill_value="extrapolate")` (and same for lon) is built DIRECTLY on those arrays. There is NO np.isfinite/NaN filtering of the (t,lat)/(t,lon) pairs before construction. I grepped gps.py: the only isnan/isfinite use is in `_warn_outside_coverage`, which checks the TIME axis coverage only, not NaN-valued position nodes.
4. scipy reproduction confirmed the poisoning: interp1d([0,1,2,3,4],[10.0,10.1,nan,10.3,10.4]) queried at [1.5,2.5,2.0,0.5,3.5] returns [nan,nan,nan,10.05,10.35] — one NaN node poisons BOTH adjacent intervals [1,3], while distant points stay finite.
5. Flow into seawater confirmed: ctd.py:349-356 calls gps.lat(bin_epoch)/gps.lon(bin_epoch) and passes the results straight to add_seawater_properties.
6. seawater.py:50-51 — `lat_safe = np.where(np.isnan(lat),0.0,lat)`, `lon_safe = ...0.0...`. These NaN positions silently become lat=0/lon=0 for gsw.SA_from_SP (regional SA baseline) and gsw.z_from_p (latitude-dependent depth). grep of seawater.py confirms NO warning/log on the substitution.

Net effect exactly as described: a localized interior missing GPS fix silently poisons a multi-sample span to NaN, which is then silently replaced by equator/prime-meridian coordinates in the absolute-salinity and depth computations, with no warning.

Scope/severity notes (consistent with "minor"):
- Conditional: requires the GPS NetCDF to actually contain masked/fill-valued interior lat/lon nodes; if the file has clean lat/lon it never triggers.
- Geographic error is bounded — SA_from_SP regional corrections are typically sub-g/kg and z_from_p latitude dependence is small — so it degrades, not destroys, the salinity/depth estimates. Correctly NOT a blocker.
- Not covered by the deferred dateline/antimeridian note (that is about longitude wrapping in interpolation; this is about NaN nodes poisoning interpolation and silent 0/0 substitution).
- Same NaN-poisoning weakness also exists in GPSFromCSV (gps.py:195-206, no isfinite filter), though the finding cites only the NetCDF path.

Suggested fix is sound: mask with np.isfinite on each (t,lat) and (t,lon) pair before building the interpolators so gaps are interpolated across instead of NaN-poisoned, and/or warn (rather than silently substitute 0) when seawater receives NaN positions. Relevant files: /Users/pat/tpw/turbulence/src/odas_tpw/perturb/gps.py (lines 271-272, 307-308; also 195-206), /Users/pat/tpw/turbulence/src/odas_tpw/perturb/seawater.py (lines 50-51), /Users/pat/tpw/turbulence/src/odas_tpw/perturb/ctd.py (lines 349-356).

title: add_seawater_properties silently strips masked arrays, exposing _FillValue garbage as real T/C/P
description: add_seawater_properties does np.asarray(T,dtype=np.float64) (etc.) on its inputs. np.asarray on a numpy MaskedArray returns the underlying .data and DISCARDS the mask, so any masked cell (typically holding the raw _FillValue, e.g. -999 or 1e20) is treated as a genuine measurement and fed into gsw. Masked-value salinity/density come out as plausible-looking numbers rather than NaN. The current binning caller (ctd.py:356) passes plain arrays so this is latent, but the function is a public API (exported, documented) and the codebase elsewhere is careful to .filled(nan) masked netCDF data precisely to avoid this; seawater.py does not.
evidence: add_seawater_properties with T=np.ma.array([15.,16.],mask=[False,True]), C=np.ma.array([42.9,43.],mask=[False,True]) returns SP=[34.984,34.190] — the masked element produced a real salinity 34.19 (mask silently dropped) instead of NaN; np.ma.is_masked(out['SP']) is False.
suggested_fix: Convert masked inputs to NaN-filled before computing: e.g. `T = np.ma.filled(np.ma.asarray(T).astype(np.float64), np.nan)` for each of T,C,P,lat,lon, so masked/fill cells propagate as NaN through gsw.
verify_reasoning:
VERIFIED REAL (latent). The mechanism is confirmed empirically: at src/odas_tpw/perturb/seawater.py:43-47 each input is coerced with bare `np.asarray(x, dtype=np.float64)`, which on a numpy MaskedArray returns `.data` and silently DROPS the mask. I reproduced the finding's exact case: add_seawater_properties(T=ma.array([15,16],mask=[F,T]), C=ma.array([42.9,43],mask=[F,T]), ...) returns SP=[34.980, 34.186] with np.ma.is_masked(out['SP'])==False — the masked cell becomes a real-looking salinity (34.186) instead of NaN. So masked _FillValue cells would be processed by gsw as genuine measurements (silent data corruption).

The finding's supporting claim about codebase convention is also verified: sibling modules deliberately guard masked netCDF data — gps.py:44-47 and hotel.py:147-150 both do `if np.ma.isMaskedArray(arr): return np.ma.filled(arr.astype(float64), np.nan)`, and binning.py:220 uses `set_auto_mask(False)`. seawater.py has no such guard, so it is genuinely inconsistent with the established pattern.

NOT already fixed: lines 43-47 still use bare np.asarray with zero mask handling anywhere in the function.

Severity = minor (matches finding, and I confirmed the qualifiers): (1) It does NOT trigger on any current code path — the sole caller is ctd.py:356, which passes binned[T_name]/binned[C_name]/binned['P'], all freshly built by _time_bin via np.where/np.full/np.bincount (ctd.py:91,99,117) and therefore always plain ndarrays, never masked; lat/lon come from gps.lat/lon (interp1d, plain ndarrays). The finding correctly concedes this is latent. (2) The 'public API' framing is overstated: add_seawater_properties is NOT re-exported in any __init__.py and there is no __all__ — it is reachable only by full module path, and is 'documented' only in the design plan (PERTURB_PLAN.md). (3) Even if triggered, the prior audit measured the downstream SA-anomaly lon impact as ~0.001 g/kg, so blast radius is small. Net: a real, correctly-diagnosed, codebase-convention-violating robustness gap that would silently corrupt salinity/density IF masked netCDF data were ever routed in, but currently dormant — minor is the right rating. Relevant file: /Users/pat/tpw/turbulence/src/odas_tpw/perturb/seawater.py:43-47. Suggested fix as stated (np.ma.filled(np.ma.asarray(x).astype(float64), np.nan) per input) is correct and matches the gps.py/hotel.py pattern. || VERIFIED REAL but LATENT. The mechanism is confirmed against current code at src/odas_tpw/perturb/seawater.py:43-47, which does `T = np.asarray(T, dtype=np.float64)` (and likewise for C, P, lat, lon) with no mask handling and no `.filled(np.nan)`. I reproduced it: `np.asarray(masked_array, dtype=np.float64)` silently drops the mask and exposes the underlying `.data`. Passing T=np.ma.array([15.,99.],mask=[F,T]), C=np.ma.array([42.9,40.],mask=[F,T]), P=np.ma.array([10.,15.],mask=[F,T]) returns SP=[34.98, 8.96] and rho=[26.0, -33.68] — the masked element produced a real (garbage) salinity/density rather than NaN, and np.ma.is_masked(out['SP']) is False. So the failure can still occur exactly as described; not fixed on this branch.

WHY MINOR (latent, not active): The function is NOT actually reachable with masked input today. The only callers are ctd.py:356 and tests/test_perturb_seawater.py — both pass plain ndarrays. At ctd.py:356, binned[T_name]/[C_name]/['P'] come from _time_bin (ctd.py:29-128), whose outputs are built exclusively via np.full / np.where / np.bincount, all producing plain ndarrays, never MaskedArrays; lat/lon come from gps interpolation (also plain). So no current code path produces corrupted output.

The finding's claim that add_seawater_properties is "exported, documented public API" is overstated: it is NOT in src/odas_tpw/perturb/__init__.py __all__ and is not re-exported anywhere — it is a module-internal helper, importable only by full path. This is a genuine defensive-coding gap / latent silent-corruption risk (the codebase does .filled(nan) on masked netCDF data elsewhere precisely to avoid this), worth the suggested `np.ma.filled(np.ma.asarray(x).astype(float64), np.nan)` hardening, but it cannot currently corrupt any output. Hence minor, not major.

title: Schema hardcodes non-UDUNITS units 'cpm' and 'PSU', defeating the module's CF/UDUNITS compliance goal
description: The module exists to emit CF-1.13/UDUNITS-parseable units (see UNITS_FIXUP / canonicalize_units), yet COMBO_SCHEMA/CHI_SCHEMA directly assign units:'cpm' (wavenumber, K_max_*, kB_*, K_max_T_*) and units:'PSU' (SP). Neither is UDUNITS-parseable, and canonicalize_units/UNITS_FIXUP only normalize RSI raw-config strings — they are not applied to these schema literals. compliance-checker (the module's cited motivation) emits a units error on both.
evidence: cf_units.Unit('cpm') raises '[UT_UNKNOWN] Failed to parse unit "cpm"'; cf_units.Unit('PSU') raises the same. Other schema units (mS/cm, kg/m^3, K^2/s, s-2, m2 s-1, umol L-1, ug L-1, W/kg, g/kg) all parse. UNITS_FIXUP (lines 24-33) does not contain 'cpm' or 'PSU', and canonicalize_units is never applied to schema dict values.
suggested_fix: Use UDUNITS-valid units: wavenumber -> 'm-1' (or 'cycles/m' documented in long_name; 'cpm' here means cycles-per-metre, so 'm-1' is exact), and practical salinity -> '1' (CF convention) with long_name carrying 'PSU'. Keep the human label in long_name.
verify_reasoning:
VERIFIED REAL. The schema literals are present in current branch code: src/odas_tpw/perturb/netcdf_schema.py uses units:'cpm' at lines 248 (K_max_1), 253 (K_max_2), 302 (kB_1), 307 (kB_2), 322 (K_max_T_1), 327 (K_max_T_2), and units:'PSU' at line 169 (SP).

Factual claims confirmed by execution:
- cf_units 3.3.1: Unit('cpm') and Unit('PSU') both raise "[UT_UNKNOWN] Failed to parse unit". All other schema units parse OK (mS/cm, kg/m^3, K^2/s, s-2, m2 s-1, umol L-1, ug L-1, W/kg, g/kg, m-1, m^2/s, K m-1, dbar, degree_Celsius, degrees_north).
- IOOS compliance-checker 5.4.2 (the module's cited motivation): units_known('cpm')=False and units_known('PSU')=False, while units_known('m-1')=True and units_known('1')=True. So the checker the test suite invokes does flag these.

Mechanism confirmed: canonicalize_units / UNITS_FIXUP (netcdf_schema.py:24-38) are applied ONLY to per-channel RSI config unit strings in rsi/profile.py:242 — they are never applied to the schema dict literals. apply_schema (combo.py:122) copies the schema values verbatim into variable attrs. The pipeline (perturb/pipeline.py:2090-2092, cli.py:351-353) writes COMBO_SCHEMA/CHI_SCHEMA/CTD_SCHEMA onto real K_max_*, kB_*, K_max_T_*, and SP variables, so the unparseable units reach real output NetCDF.

The module docstring (lines 1-9) states its purpose is "CF-1.13 / ACDD-1.3 compliant NetCDF output," and UNITS_FIXUP exists precisely to convert UDUNITS-unparseable strings (umol_L-1, FTU, deg_C). Leaving 'cpm'/'PSU' as raw schema literals directly contradicts that goal — a genuine inconsistency, not a deliberate exception.

Why it escapes existing coverage: tests/test_perturb_cf_compliance.py only builds a fixture with T, P, lat, lon, stime, etime. None of SP, K_max_*, kB_*, K_max_T_* appear in the fixture, so the module's own CF/ACDD regression test never exercises these units and does not catch the defect.

The suggested fix is correct: 'cpm' (cycles-per-metre, per dissipation.py:564 long_name "wavenumber (cycles per metre)") is dimensionally m-1, which parses; practical salinity should be units:'1' (CF convention) with the PSU label kept in long_name.

Not data/units corruption (numeric values are correct) and not a crash — it is a metadata/CF-compliance defect producing non-conformant output, correctly rated minor. Not already fixed on this branch.

Note (broader, beyond the cited file): the same non-parseable 'cpm'/'s-2 cpm-1' literals are also written as NetCDF unit attrs in rsi/dissipation.py:441,545,554,563; the finding scopes only to netcdf_schema.py, but a complete fix would address those too. || VERIFIED REAL and NOT already fixed on the current branch.

Code trace:
- /Users/pat/tpw/turbulence/src/odas_tpw/perturb/netcdf_schema.py assigns units:'cpm' at lines 248 (K_max_1), 253 (K_max_2), 302 (kB_1), 307 (kB_2), 322 (K_max_T_1), 327 (K_max_T_2), and units:'PSU' at line 169 (SP).
- apply_schema (lines 459-467) copies every attr verbatim into the dataset (ds[vname].attrs[key]=val) with NO canonicalization.
- combo.py:122 calls apply_schema on the combo output; perturb/cli.py and pipeline.py both feed COMBO_SCHEMA/CHI_SCHEMA/CTD_SCHEMA into that path, so 'cpm'/'PSU' land in the written NetCDF.
- canonicalize_units / UNITS_FIXUP are applied ONLY in rsi/profile.py:242 to RSI raw-config channel strings, never to schema dict values (grep confirms no other callers). UNITS_FIXUP (lines 24-33) contains neither 'cpm' nor 'PSU'.

Reproduction (cf_units installed in this env): cf_units.Unit('cpm') and cf_units.Unit('PSU') both raise '[UT_UNKNOWN] Failed to parse unit'. Every other schema unit I tested parses cleanly: m-1, cycles/m, 1, mS/cm, kg/m^3, K^2/s, s-2, m2 s-1, umol L-1, ug L-1, W/kg, g/kg, K m-1, m^2/s, dbar, degree_Celsius, degree, V, m/s. So 'cpm' and 'PSU' are the sole UDUNITS-invalid strings, defeating the module's stated CF-1.13/UDUNITS goal; compliance-checker emits a units error on both.

Severity minor (not major): no data-value corruption, no crash, no incorrect physics — stored numbers are correct; only the units metadata is non-parseable. Suggested fix is sound: wavenumber 'cpm' (cycles-per-metre) -> 'm-1' (exact: 1 cycle = dimensionless, so cycles/m == m-1) or document 'cycles/m' (which does parse) in long_name; practical salinity 'PSU' -> '1' per CF convention with 'PSU' kept in long_name.

title: Hotel time_format='auto' rejects an entire file when per-channel time grids have ambiguous magnitude, even if the default grid is unambiguous
description: _parse_time('auto') raises ValueError when the median numeric time falls in [1e6,1e9). _parse_time is called independently for the default time column AND for each per-channel time_column (csv:291, netcdf:332, mat:401), all forced through the same 'auto' format. A sparse per-channel grid (e.g. a modem-event time vector whose values happen to land in [1e6,1e9)) will abort load_hotel even when the main time grid parsed fine and the relative/epoch sense is already known. The relative/epoch decision is also redundantly re-derived per grid rather than shared.
evidence: hotel.py:291 `t_arr,_ = _parse_time(np.asarray(df[tc].values), time_format)` (and 332, 401) reuse the same time_format string; with time_format='auto' an extra-time-column whose nanmedian is in [1e6,1e9) hits the `raise ValueError('cannot disambiguate ... in [1e6,1e9)')` branch at hotel.py:190-194, failing the whole file.
suggested_fix: Resolve the relative/epoch sense once from the default time column, then reuse that decision (pass an explicit 'seconds'/'epoch' to the per-channel _parse_time calls) instead of re-running 'auto' disambiguation per grid.
verify_reasoning:
VERIFIED REAL (low severity / nit), currently present on this branch.

Code trace (src/odas_tpw/perturb/hotel.py):
- _parse_time(raw, "auto") raises ValueError ("cannot disambiguate ... in [1e6, 1e9)") at lines 190-194 when np.nanmedian(vals) is in [1e6, 1e9).
- Per-channel time columns are parsed by re-passing the SAME unresolved time_format string:
  - CSV: line 291  `t_arr, _ = _parse_time(np.asarray(df[tc].values), time_format)`
  - NetCDF: line 332 `t_arr, _ = _parse_time(raw, time_format)`
  - MAT: line 401  `t_arr, _ = _parse_time(extra_time_arrays[tc], time_format)`
- The default column is parsed first (csv:276, nc:311, mat:392). So when time_format="auto" and the default grid is unambiguous (median <1e6 or >1e9), is_relative is already determined, yet a per-channel grid whose nanmedian lands in [1e6, 1e9) independently re-runs the auto disambiguation and raises, aborting load_hotel for the whole file.

Why the suggested fix is sound (no new bug introduced): the HotelData contract documented at hotel.py:72-75 and 84-86 explicitly states the relative/epoch sense "Applies to every time array (default and per-channel) -- mixing relative and absolute time arrays in one hotel file is not supported." interpolate_hotel ENFORCES this: it derives a single pf_start_offset solely from hotel_data.time_is_relative (line 453) and subtracts it from time_for(src) for every channel including per-channel grids (line 464). Therefore resolving the sense once from the default column and passing an explicit "seconds"/"epoch" to the per-channel _parse_time calls matches the code's own invariant and removes the redundant, file-aborting per-grid disambiguation. The legitimate "default itself is ambiguous" case still raises, because the default column is still parsed with the original format before any per-channel parsing.

Reachability/severity: the trigger is a specific magnitude coincidence (per-channel time median in [1e6,1e9) = epoch 1970-2001 or relative span 11.6d-31.7y) while the default grid is unambiguous; realistic for sparse per-channel grids (e.g. modem-event vectors) but narrow. Consistent with nit severity.

Not already fixed and not test-pinned: the per-channel time_column tests (test_perturb_hotel.py:627-712) all pass time_format="seconds" explicitly; the "cannot disambiguate" tests (test_perturb_hotel.py:234, test_perturb_hotel_branches.py:36/56) exercise _parse_time directly or the default column, none assert that a per-channel grid SHOULD abort when the default is unambiguous. So current per-channel auto-abort behavior is an unintended side effect, not pinned design. || VERIFIED REAL, NOT FIXED, correctly severity=nit.

Code trace (src/odas_tpw/perturb/hotel.py, current code on branch):
- _parse_time('auto') raises ValueError for numeric times whose nanmedian lands in [1e6, 1e9) (lines 181-194: `if median<1e6 -> relative`, `elif median>1e9 -> epoch`, `else -> raise ValueError('cannot disambiguate ... in [1e6, 1e9)')`).
- All three loaders call _parse_time once for the default time grid (csv:276, netcdf:311, mat:392) capturing both (time, is_relative), then call _parse_time AGAIN per per-channel time_column override forwarding the SAME time_format string and discarding the relative/epoch result: `t_arr, _ = _parse_time(..., time_format)` at csv:291, netcdf:332, mat:401.
- Therefore with time_format='auto' (the default) plus a per-channel `time_column` override whose grid nanmedian lands in [1e6, 1e9), the per-channel call hits the raise branch and aborts the entire load_hotel call even though the default grid parsed cleanly and the relative/epoch sense is already known from line 276/311/392. The resolved sense is never threaded into the per-channel calls.

Not already fixed: no guard reuses the default-grid decision; the `_,` at 291/332/401 explicitly drops it. Existing per-channel tests (tests/test_perturb_hotel.py:627-750) all pass time_format='seconds' explicitly, so none covers this 'auto' path; nothing prevents the failure.

Why nit (not higher): It fails LOUDLY with a clear ValueError — no silent data/units corruption. Triggering requires the conjunction of (1) a non-default per-channel time_column override (uncommon config), (2) time_format left at 'auto', and (3) that specific grid's nanmedian falling in the narrow [1e6, 1e9) ~31.7-yr-vs-1970..2001 window. Robustness-only, low real-world likelihood.

Aside (not the reported finding, deeper/latent): merge uses a single pf_start_offset from one time_is_relative flag (hotel.py:453) and applies it to per-channel times at line 464 (hotel_data.time_for(src) - pf_start_offset). Since the per-channel relative/epoch sense is discarded, a per-channel grid with a different sense than the default would be offset incorrectly even when parsing succeeds. The suggested fix (resolve the sense once and pass an explicit 'seconds'/'epoch' to per-channel _parse_time) would address both this and the reported abort.

title: bin_by_time silently ignores diagnostics flag — time-binned diss/chi lose n_samples and *_std
description: bin_by_time accepts a `diagnostics: bool` parameter (line 518) but its body never references it. It emits only the aggregated value per variable — no `n_samples` counts and no `*_std` per bin. The depth path (bin_by_depth / _bin_snapshot) honors diagnostics and emits both. So with binning.method='time' and binning.diagnostics=true, the diss/chi/profile time combos silently omit the per-bin sample counts and standard deviations the user requested, with no warning. bin_diss/bin_chi forward `diagnostics` into bin_by_time (lines 606, 623), so the loss is reachable from normal config.
evidence: inspect.getsource(bin_by_time) split at the signature shows the body contains no 'diagnostics', no 'n_samples', no '_std'. Confirmed via reflection: `diagnostics used in body: False / n_samples emitted: False / _std emitted: False`. Contrast bin_by_depth lines 471-492 which build `{vname}_std`, `{vname}_n`, and `n_samples` when diagnostics is True.
suggested_fix: Either implement the QC-flag/LnSigma/std/n_samples diagnostics in bin_by_time (mirroring _bin_snapshot), or raise/log a clear NotImplementedError-style warning when diagnostics=True and method='time' so the user knows the diagnostics were not produced.
verify_reasoning:
VERIFIED REAL and still present on the branch.

Core claim confirmed by reading current code. bin_by_time (src/odas_tpw/perturb/binning.py:514-589) declares `diagnostics: bool = False` (line 518) but the function body never references it. grep for "diagnostics" in binning.py shows it appears at lines 471/479/487 (inside bin_by_depth) and 320 (inside _bin_snapshot), but the ONLY occurrence inside bin_by_time's 514-589 span is the parameter declaration at line 518. The body emits only the aggregated value per variable (binned_data dict, lines 552-578) plus the "time" coordinate; it produces no n_samples, no {vname}_std, no {vname}_n.

Contrast confirmed. The depth path honors diagnostics: _bin_snapshot (lines 320-322) builds out["stds"][vname] = _bin_std(...) and out["finite_counts"][vname] = _bin_finite_counts(...) when diagnostics is True, and bin_by_depth (lines 471-492) materializes those into data variables {vname}_std, {vname}_n, and n_samples.

Reachability from normal documented config confirmed. config.py:486-494 is the `binning` block exposing BOTH method: "depth | time" (line 487) AND diagnostics (line 494), whose inline comment explicitly promises "write n_samples, *_std per bin". pipeline.py:1898/1901 read bin_method and diagnostics; pipeline.py forwards both into bin_diss (1991-1999) and bin_chi (2005-2013), and the prof path (1982-1985) calls bin_by_time directly with diagnostics. bin_diss/bin_chi (binning.py:606, 623) forward `diagnostics` into bin_by_time when method=="time". So a user who sets method: time + diagnostics: true silently receives none of the promised per-bin n_samples/std outputs.

Silent confirmed. No warning, log, or error is emitted on this path; the flag is simply discarded.

Severity = minor (correctly rated). The aggregated values themselves are NOT corrupted — bin_by_time applies the same correct dispatch as the depth path (uint QC flags OR-pooled at line 566, LnSigma combined in quadrature via sqrt(nanmean(sigma^2)) at lines 568-575, everything else through agg at line 577). diagnostics also defaults to false. The defect is purely the silent omission of OPTIONAL QC diagnostics (sample counts and standard deviations) that the config documentation promises, with no user warning — feature loss / silent inconsistency, not data corruption or a crash. Not a style preference. Current code does not handle it (already_fixed=false). || CONFIRMED REAL and NOT fixed on the current branch (fix/audit-pr73).

Code trace in /Users/pat/tpw/turbulence/src/odas_tpw/perturb/binning.py:
- bin_by_time signature (line 514-520) declares `diagnostics: bool = False` (line 518), but the function body (lines 521-589) contains ZERO references to `diagnostics`, `n_samples`, `_std`, `stds`, or `finite_counts` (verified by grepping lines 520-589: "NO MATCHES"). It builds `binned_data` with only the aggregated value per variable plus the `time` coordinate, then concatenates (lines 580-589). No per-bin counts and no per-bin std are ever produced.
- Contrast bin_by_depth/_bin_snapshot: _bin_snapshot (lines 320-322) populates `out["stds"]` via `_bin_std` and `out["finite_counts"]` via `_bin_finite_counts` when diagnostics is True; bin_by_depth then emits `{vname}_std` (lines 472-474, 481), `{vname}_n` (lines 475-477, 482-485), and `n_samples` (lines 487-492).
