So the depth path honors the flag; the time path does not.

Reachability from normal config is confirmed:
- cli.py:234 reads `diagnostics = binning_cfg.get("diagnostics", False)`; cli.py:280-281 and 305-306 pass `bin_method` and `diagnostics` into bin_diss/bin_chi.
- binning.py:605-606 (bin_diss) and 622-623 (bin_chi) forward `diagnostics` into bin_by_time when `method == "time"`.
- config template (config.py:487 `method: depth | time`, config.py:494 `diagnostics: false # write n_samples, *_std per bin`) documents both options in the same binning block, so a user setting method=time and diagnostics=true reasonably expects the diagnostics.

Net effect: with binning.method="time" and binning.diagnostics=true, the diss/chi time-binned outputs silently omit per-bin n_samples and *_std, with no warning.

Severity = minor (matches the finding). This is feature-loss / silent inconsistency, not data corruption: the values that ARE emitted are correct (QC-flag uint OR-pooling at line 565-567 and LnSigma quadrature at line 568-576 are properly handled in the time path). No crash, no wrong physics. It only affects users who both opt into time binning (default method is "depth") and request diagnostics (default false), so impact is limited but the silent drop with no warning is a genuine defect.

title: Linear/pchip interpolation of discrete hotel flags over-flags neighboring diss/chi segments
description: The default hotel interpolation is 'pchip' (hotel.py:446); 'linear' is also offered. A drop_from flag channel that is set at a single hotel timestamp is interpolated continuously onto the slow grid, so fractional flag values appear in the samples *between* a flagged and an unflagged hotel time. qc_gate then treats any |sample|>=0.5 as set, so diss/chi segments whose window only touches the interpolated ramp (not the genuinely-flagged time) get dropped. With drop_action='nan' this NaNs otherwise-valid epsilon/chi estimates adjacent in time to a flag.
evidence: Repro with a flag of value 8 set only at t=10s, linearly interpolated to a 1 s slow grid: slow_flag at t=6..10 = [4.8 5.6 6.4 7.2 8.0]; qc_gate's `abs>=0.5` flags ALL of t=6..10, so a segment centered at t=8 (window [6,10]) is dropped even though the flag belonged only to t=10. Only `interp: nearest` (a per-channel opt-in, hotel.py docstring line 17) avoids this; the default pchip/linear does not.
suggested_fix: Force flag/QC drop_from channels to nearest-neighbor interpolation (or step-hold) regardless of the global hotel.interpolation default, or document that any channel used in *_drop_from MUST set interp: nearest to avoid flag bleed.
verify_reasoning:
VERIFIED, but downgraded to nit. The mechanism is real and reproducible. With the default hotel interpolation (pchip; hotel.py:446, falling back via interpolate_hotel hotel.py:459 `kind = opts.get("interp") or default_kind`), a hotel flag channel set at a single timestamp is continuously interpolated to fractional values across the whole inter-sample span. I reproduced the finding's exact numbers: a flag value 8 at hotel t=10 on a 5 s grid, linearly interpolated to a 1 s slow grid, gives slow values >=0.5 across t=6..14 (pchip: same span); qc_gate.py:96-100 then treats every |sample|>=0.5 as set (arr_u = (np.abs(arr) >= 0.5).astype(np.uint8)), and the per-segment OR-reduce (line 104-108) drops every segment whose window touches that ramp. With the default drop_action='nan' (apply_qc_to_dataset lines 162-176) the adjacent epsilon/chi values are NaN'd. `interp: nearest` confines the spread to t=8..12 (reproduced); nothing forces flag channels to nearest, so the default pchip/linear over-flags. The path is genuinely reachable: config.py:477-480 documents naming hotel float channels in epsilon_drop_from/chi_drop_from, and hotel channels are always float64 (hotel.py:418/427/432, merge stores them at hotel.py:500).

Why nit, not minor: (1) The over-flagging direction is FAIL-SAFE for a QC drop gate — it discards some good data near a flag but never keeps bad data; the dangerous under-flagging direction was the one previously fixed. The finding's "corruption" framing is overstated; this is bounded data-retention loss, not wrong physics. (2) Even `nearest` widens a sparse sampled flag to +/-half the hotel interval — a sampled flag is not a delta; linear/pchip only widen it to the full inter-sample span, an incremental effect bounded by hotel flag spacing (typically dense). (3) The `>=0.5` threshold is an intentional, tested round-to-nearest design (tests/test_perturb_qc_gate.py:85-95). (4) The documented production path uses uint8 qc.rules channels (qc_rules.py:351 emits uint8 directly on t_slow, bypassing interpolation entirely), and no example config uses a hotel flag channel in *_drop_from (examples/.../perturb.yaml epsilon_drop_from/chi_drop_from are []). So this is an opt-in, empty-by-default advanced path.

Not already fixed: no code forces flag/QC drop_from channels to nearest regardless of the global default, and no doc warns that channels used in *_drop_from must set `interp: nearest` to avoid flag bleed. The prior audit's fix note (findings.json:158) anticipated this by recommending step interpolation for flag channels, but that recommendation was never implemented as a guard or documented requirement. The suggested fix (force nearest for *_drop_from channels, or document the requirement) is sound. Relevant files: /Users/pat/tpw/turbulence/src/odas_tpw/perturb/qc_gate.py:96-100,162-176; /Users/pat/tpw/turbulence/src/odas_tpw/perturb/hotel.py:418,446,459; /Users/pat/tpw/turbulence/src/odas_tpw/perturb/config.py:477-484. || CONFIRMED REAL and NOT fixed, for the hotel-injected flag-channel path.

End-to-end trace against current code:
1. A flag column in a hotel file is loaded as float64 in _load_netcdf (src/odas_tpw/perturb/hotel.py:322 `ch[v] = _nc_array(var).astype(np.float64)`; same in _load_csv:282 and _load_mat). It is float, never uint.
2. interpolate_hotel picks `kind = opts.get("interp") or default_kind` (hotel.py:459) with `default_kind = hotel_cfg.get("interpolation", "pchip")` (hotel.py:446). There is NO special-casing that forces flag / drop_from channels to nearest/step interpolation — they ride the global pchip/linear default.
3. I reproduced the smear: a flag of value 8 set only at hotel t=10 (zeros elsewhere on a 2 s hotel grid), interpolated to a 1 s target grid, gives linear/pchip values of 4.0 at the neighboring samples t=9 and t=11. Nearest keeps it sharp (only t=10/t=11 nonzero).
4. compute_segment_drop converts the float flag with `arr_u = (np.abs(arr) >= 0.5).astype(np.uint8)` (src/odas_tpw/perturb/qc_gate.py:96-100), so the smeared neighbor value 4.0 is treated as set.
5. It then OR-reduces over each segment window `[t_center - L/2, t_center + L/2]` (qc_gate.py:69-75, 104-108), so a diss/chi segment whose window only overlaps the interpolated ramp (not the genuinely-flagged hotel time) is flagged.
6. With the default drop_action "nan" (src/odas_tpw/perturb/config.py:472), apply_qc_to_dataset NaNs those otherwise-valid epsilon/chi values (qc_gate.py:162-176).

The config docs explicitly describe epsilon_drop_from/chi_drop_from as "hotel channel names ... should be a uint8 CF bitfield (or 0/1)" (config.py:477-481), so hotel-injected flag channels are an intended, documented source — this path is reachable, not hypothetical.

Scope / why minor (matches reported severity):
- The qc_rules-synthesized channels (src/odas_tpw/perturb/qc_rules.py:351 `flag.astype(np.uint8) * np.uint8(bit_val)`, registered at qc_rules.py:366) are already uint8 on the slow grid and are NOT interpolated, so they are immune. Only hotel-file-sourced flag channels are affected.
- Requires the hotel flag time grid to be coarser than the slow grid (otherwise no intermediate samples exist to smear into).
- The failure is conservative: it over-NaNs valid data adjacent to a genuine flag; it never un-flags a bad segment. The over-flag is bounded to segments touching the one interpolation interval on each side of a true flag.

Not already fixed: no guard forces flag/drop_from channels to nearest/step interpolation, and no documentation warns that a hotel flag channel used in *_drop_from must set interp: nearest. The only mention of `interp: nearest` (hotel.py docstring line 17) is a generic example unrelated to flag bleed. The suggested fix (force nearest/step-hold for drop_from channels, or document the requirement) remains applicable.

title: _time_bin diagnostics n_samples counts NaN samples — overstates the effective averaging N
description: In the CTD time binner, the diagnostic `n_samples` is `np.bincount(bin_idx[in_range], ...)` — the count of all in-range samples regardless of finiteness. The mean path (line 110-117) and median path (line 97) only aggregate FINITE samples, so for any bin containing NaN values, n_samples is larger than the actual number of values that entered the mean/median. The depth engine deliberately distinguishes these (binning.py _bin_finite_counts, lines 123-135, '...the in-range sample count overstates how many values actually entered a bin mean; this is the honest N'). The CTD binner does not, so its n_samples cannot be used as a weighting/confidence N.
evidence: Repro: t=[0.1,0.2,0.3,0.4], x=[1,NaN,3,5], bin_width=1 → mean=3.0 (averaged 3 finite), but n_samples=[4]. The 4 includes the NaN sample that did not contribute to the mean.
suggested_fix: Compute n_samples from finite-and-in-range counts (mirror binning._bin_finite_counts), or emit a separate finite-count diagnostic, so the reported N matches the values actually averaged.
verify_reasoning:
VERIFIED REAL. In src/odas_tpw/perturb/ctd.py:78 the diagnostic n_samples is computed once, before the per-channel loop, as `np.bincount(bin_idx[in_range], minlength=n_bins).astype(int)` — counting ALL in-range samples with no finiteness filter. Both aggregation paths exclude NaNs from the actual aggregate: the mean path (lines 110-117) builds counts/sums from `finite = np.isfinite(arr) & in_range`, and the median path (line 97) only assigns when `np.any(np.isfinite(sl))` with np.nanmedian ignoring NaNs. So for any bin containing NaN samples, n_samples > the number of values that entered the mean/median. The finding's reproduction is correct: t=[0.1,0.2,0.3,0.4], x=[1,NaN,3,5], bin_width=1 yields mean=3.0 (3 finite values) but n_samples=[4].

This is corroborated by the project's own depth engine, binning.py:123-135 (_bin_finite_counts), which deliberately uses `finite = in_range & np.isfinite(values)` with the docstring: "the in-range sample count overstates how many values actually entered a bin mean; this is the honest N." The CTD binner does not apply this honest-N logic, so its n_samples is unreliable as a weighting/confidence N. An additional aggravating detail: n_samples is computed once outside the data loop, so it is a single shared array and cannot even represent the per-channel finite N (different channels like N2/dTdz have different NaN footprints per the comments at lines 94-96).

Severity is correctly minor: the binned data values (mean/median) are computed correctly; only the diagnostic n_samples field is inaccurate, affecting downstream weighting/confidence-interval use, not the science values themselves. Not already fixed on this branch — line 78 still uses the in-range-only count. || CONFIRMED, still present, not fixed. At src/odas_tpw/perturb/ctd.py:78 the diagnostic is `result["n_samples"] = np.bincount(bin_idx[in_range], minlength=n_bins)` — it counts every in-range sample regardless of finiteness. Both aggregation paths use only finite values: the mean path masks with `finite = np.isfinite(arr) & in_range` (lines 110-117) and the median path computes nanmedian only over finite slices (line 97, guarded by np.any(np.isfinite(sl))). So when a bin contains NaN samples, n_samples > the number of values that actually entered the mean/median.

Reproduction (run against the actual module): t=[0.1,0.2,0.3,0.4], x=[1.0, NaN, 3.0, 5.0], bin_width=1.0 -> mean=3.0 and median=3.0 (both averaging the 3 finite values), but n_samples=[4] in BOTH the mean and median paths. The NaN sample is counted.

The contrast cited in the finding is accurate: the depth engine's binning.py:_bin_finite_counts (lines 123-135) deliberately computes `finite = in_range & np.isfinite(values)` and documents it as the honest averaging N specifically because the in-range count overstates it. The CTD binner has no equivalent. The mismatch is additionally systematic, not occasional: ctd.py emits a SINGLE per-bin n_samples shared across all channels (one bincount at line 78), so for channels that are NaN outside casts (N2/dTdz, per the line 94-96 comment) the count is wrong for nearly every bin, and it cannot in principle be a per-channel averaging N (unlike the depth engine which emits per-channel {vname}_n finite counts, binning.py:475-485).

Severity minor: this is a diagnostics-only inaccuracy. The binned mean/median data values are themselves correct (they use finite-only data); only the optional n_samples diagnostic is unreliable as a confidence/weighting N. No data-value corruption, crash, or QC-gate breakage. Suggested fix is to mirror binning._bin_finite_counts (count in_range & np.isfinite per channel) or emit a separate finite-count diagnostic.

title: make_combo time-dedup silently drops data at coincident bin-center timestamps
description: For time combos (CTD), after sorting by time the code coalesces exact-duplicate timestamps keeping only the first: `keep[1:] &= tk[1:] != tk[:-1]` then `combo.isel(time=np.flatnonzero(keep))`. When two per-file CTD windows overlap and produce bin centers with identical float epoch-second values, the second (and any further) records — which carry that other file's measurements — are discarded with no warning. CF strict monotonicity is satisfied, but real data from a different source file at that instant is dropped rather than merged.
evidence: combo.py:112-119: duplicates are removed by index filtering, keeping the earliest along the sorted axis; there is no aggregation of the dropped rows. Exact float-equal timestamps across files are rare but possible (e.g. identical bin_width and phase-aligned start_times), and the loss is silent.
suggested_fix: Aggregate (mean/OR) duplicate-timestamp rows instead of keep-first, or at least log a warning counting how many time samples were dropped as duplicates so the user can investigate overlapping windows.
verify_reasoning:
VERIFIED REAL. The code at src/odas_tpw/perturb/combo.py:112-119 (time-combo path) deduplicates by keep-first on exact float-equal timestamps: `keep = np.isfinite(tk); keep[1:] &= tk[1:] != tk[:-1]; combo = combo.isel(time=np.flatnonzero(keep))`. There is no aggregation/merge of the dropped rows and no warning. This is the only duplicate-time handling in the path (make_ctd_combo -> make_combo method="time"); no downstream groupby exists.

Upstream data flow makes coincident timestamps genuinely possible: per-file CTD bin centers are `time_epoch = (bin_edges[:-1]+bin_edges[1:])/2 + epoch_offset` (ctd.py:362-367), with `bin_edges = np.arange(t_lo, t_hi+bin_width, bin_width)` (ctd.py:307) and `epoch_offset = pf.start_time.timestamp()` (ctd.py:336, ms resolution from p_file header). Two files can yield byte-identical float epoch-second centers when their wall-clock windows overlap AND their grids are phase-aligned (epoch offsets differing by an exact integer multiple of bin_width with matching t_lo phase).

I reproduced the exact mechanism: two phase-aligned overlapping windows produce sorted times [...,1001.25,1001.25,1001.75,1001.75,...] where the second file's distinct partial-bin records (data 2.0) are dropped and the first file's (1.0) kept. The dropped rows carry real, distinct data (each file only holds its own samples in that interval), not redundant copies, so keep-first silently loses other-source measurements rather than averaging them.

The code on this branch contains exactly this logic (the comment at lines 109-111 explicitly anticipates overlapping per-file windows and deliberately chose keep-first for CF monotonicity), so it is NOT already fixed.

Severity minor (not higher): the precondition is narrow. For sequential VMP casts, per-file wall-clock windows are normally disjoint, in which case no bin centers can collide. Even when two files overlap, arbitrary millisecond-resolution start-time offsets interleave the grids rather than collide (I confirmed 0 exact duplicates with a realistic 137 ms offset); only exact float collisions trigger the loss, and the comparison is strict equality (near-equal values survive). So real-world impact is low-frequency, matching the finding's own minor/medium-confidence framing. The loss, when it occurs, is silent and concerns distinct data, making it a genuine minor bug rather than a nit. The suggested fix (aggregate duplicate-timestamp rows, or at least log a count of dropped duplicates) is appropriate.

Relevant files (absolute): /Users/pat/tpw/turbulence/src/odas_tpw/perturb/combo.py (lines 102-119), /Users/pat/tpw/turbulence/src/odas_tpw/perturb/ctd.py (lines 300-367). || VERIFIED REAL, NOT already fixed. combo.py:112-119 (current on branch, blame = audit commits c807162/9338855 of 2026-06-19) does exactly what the finding states: after argsort by time, `keep[1:] &= tk[1:] != tk[:-1]` flags any record whose float timestamp equals its predecessor, and `combo.isel(time=np.flatnonzero(keep))` discards them keeping only the first. No aggregation of dropped rows; grep of combo.py shows zero log/warn statements, so the loss is silent. I reproduced it numerically: two CTD files with coincident bin centers (e.g. 1005.0) cause file B's value at that instant to be dropped while file A's is kept.

Severity correctly minor (borderline nit) because the precondition is genuinely rare. Per-file CTD bin centers are `t_lo + bin_width/2 + k*bin_width + epoch_offset` (ctd.py:307,337,362) where epoch_offset = pf.start_time.timestamp() (a real-valued epoch second) and t_lo = nanmin(pf.t_slow). For two independent files to yield a FLOAT-EQUAL coincident center, their (t_lo+epoch_offset) must differ by an exact integer multiple of bin_width in IEEE-754 — which essentially never happens with distinct fractional per-file start timestamps; it requires contrived phase alignment. The finding's evidence slightly overstates this (matching bin_width + phase-aligned start_times is necessary but not sufficient; exact float equality is also required), but the cited line range and core claim are accurate.

The dedup itself is required (CF strict monotonicity forbids duplicate time coords), so the genuine defect is narrowly: keep-first instead of merge/aggregate, plus the absence of any warning counting dropped duplicate samples. A user with truly overlapping CTD windows would get no signal. Real but low-probability silent data loss.

title: eps-chi panels crash on all-NaN data with no manual color limits (LogNorm(None,None))
description: In eps_chi.run, eps_vmin/eps_vmax, chi_vmin/chi_vmax and gam_vmin/gam_vmax come from layout.quantile_limits, which returns (None, None) when a panel has no finite positive data and the user supplied no --*-vmin/--*-vmax override. These are passed straight into LogNorm(vmin=None, vmax=None) and then layout.plot_panel -> pcolormesh, which raises 'ValueError: Invalid vmin or vmax' and aborts the whole figure. Unlike the profiles subcommand (which guards via _make_norm returning None plus a 'not np.any(np.isfinite(z))' check and renders a 'no valid' placeholder), eps_chi has no such guard. This is realistic: if chi fails to align onto the eps profile axis (no stime match within 5 s) chi becomes all-NaN, which makes both the chi panel and gamma=chi/eps all-NaN, crashing the figure even though eps is fine.
evidence: Reproduced: layout.quantile_limits(np.full((3,4),np.nan), None, None) -> (None, None); then layout.plot_panel(ax, fig, cast_x, segments, depth, np.full((3,4),np.nan), cmap, LogNorm(vmin=None, vmax=None), 'g') raises 'ValueError: Invalid vmin or vmax'. The all-NaN chi case arises from the alignment branch at eps_chi.py:300-318 (aligned stays all-NaN when no chi column matches an eps slot within 5 s) and gamma at line 352 (np.where((eps>0)&isfinite(chi), chi/eps, nan) is all-NaN when chi is all-NaN). No guard exists between quantile_limits (354-356) and the three plot_panel calls (358-376).
suggested_fix: Before each plot_panel call, skip/placeholder a panel whose (vmin,vmax) is (None,None) or whose data has no finite positive values (mirror profiles._make_norm returning None and the 'no valid' text path), or pass a valid fallback LogNorm only when finite positive data exists.
verify_reasoning:
CONFIRMED REAL via end-to-end reproduction against the actual current code on this branch.

Trace (src/odas_tpw/perturb/plot/eps_chi.py:354-376 + src/odas_tpw/perturb/plot/layout.py:39-54, 118-150):
- layout.quantile_limits(z, lo, hi) (layout.py:47-54) computes `finite = z[isfinite & >0]`; when `finite.size == 0` it returns the passed-in `(lo, hi)`, i.e. `(None, None)` when the user gave no --*-vmin/--*-vmax override. Verified: quantile_limits(np.full((3,4),nan), None, None) -> (None, None).
- eps_chi.run (lines 354-356) passes those straight into LogNorm(vmin=None, vmax=None) and then layout.plot_panel (lines 358-376). There is NO guard between quantile_limits and the three plot_panel calls.
- Inside plot_panel, the crash actually originates at fig.colorbar(...) (layout.py:149), not pcolormesh: matplotlib colorbar._process_values calls self.norm.inverse(b) which raises 'ValueError: Invalid vmin or vmax' for an unset LogNorm. (Minor inaccuracy in the finding's evidence, which attributed the raise to pcolormesh; the figure still aborts exactly as described.)

Reachability proven two ways:
1. Default --apply-qc path (lines 320-330): I built real diss_combo_01/combo.nc + chi_combo_01/combo.nc fixtures where chi is finite but qc_drop_chi flags every bin. eps_chi.run printed "applied QC: NaN'd 0 eps bins, 20 chi bins" then crashed with ValueError: Invalid vmin or vmax — even though eps was fully valid, losing the whole 3-panel figure. This is the most realistic trigger (a poor FP07 calibration where all chi bins fail QC).
2. The alignment branch (lines 300-318): `aligned` starts all-NaN and is only filled at eps slots matching a chi stime within 5 s; a systematic chi/eps clock offset > 5 s leaves chi all-NaN, and gamma (line 352) follows.

Verified matplotlib is 3.10.8, within the project's pinned "matplotlib>=3.8,<4" (pyproject.toml:23), so this is not a version-specific artifact.

Contrast confirming it's a genuine gap: the sibling profiles subcommand (src/odas_tpw/perturb/plot/profiles.py:104-161, 250-254) guards exactly this case — _make_norm returns None when a LogNorm field has no positive data, and _build_profiles_figure checks `if norm is None or not np.any(np.isfinite(z))` and renders a "no valid" placeholder instead of plotting. eps_chi.py has no equivalent.

Severity major: a realistic, default-path data condition (chi fully QC-dropped, or chi unalignable to eps) crashes the entire figure and discards the valid epsilon panel; not a blocker since it requires a degenerate-but-plausible chi panel, and eps-only runs are unaffected. || CONFIRMED against current code on fix/audit-pr73.

Code trace (all verified, not from git history):
1. layout.quantile_limits (src/odas_tpw/perturb/plot/layout.py:39-54): when `finite = z[np.isfinite(z) & (z>0)]` is empty AND the user passed no override, it returns `(lo, hi)` = `(None, None)`. Reproduced: quantile_limits(np.full((5,4),nan), None, None) -> (None, None).
2. eps_chi.run (src/odas_tpw/perturb/plot/eps_chi.py:354-376) feeds those straight into `LogNorm(vmin=chi_vmin, vmax=chi_vmax)` -> layout.plot_panel -> ax.pcolormesh. Reproduced with matplotlib 3.10.8: this raises `ValueError: Invalid vmin or vmax`, aborting fig.savefig and the whole figure.
3. No guard exists between quantile_limits (354-356) and the three plot_panel calls (358-376); grep for isfinite/return/raise in run() confirms the only finite-checks are inside the alignment loop (311) and the gamma expr (352), neither of which prevents the crash.

Asymmetry with profiles confirmed: profiles._make_norm (profiles.py:153-154) returns None when `vmin is None or vmax is None or vmax <= 0`, and the caller (profiles.py:250) checks `if norm is None or not np.any(np.isfinite(z))` and draws a "no valid {name}" placeholder. eps_chi has no equivalent path.

Realism confirmed end-to-end: simulated eps=valid, chi=all-NaN (and thus gamma=all-NaN via line 352). The eps panel plotted fine; the chi panel raised `ValueError: Invalid vmin or vmax`. All-NaN chi is reachable in practice: (a) the alignment branch at lines 300-318 leaves `aligned` fully NaN when no chi column's nearest eps column is within 5 s (different timestamp rounding/overlap between chi and eps runs); (b) --apply-qc (default True) NaN-ing all chi bins; in either case eps stays valid so the figure is otherwise renderable but the chi/gamma panels crash it.

Severity major (not blocker): it is a visualization/plotting path, not silent corruption of the scientific output — but it crashes the entire figure on a realistic NaN edge case despite eps being fine, with no fallback. Fix: mirror profiles' guard — skip/placeholder any panel whose quantile_limits returned (None,None) or whose data has no finite positive values, and only build a LogNorm when finite positive data exists.

title: profiles --var on a 1D (profile-only) variable crashes with cryptic transpose error
description: _build_profiles_figure selects panel variables with _available(v) = (v in dss.data_vars or is_pseudo_var(v)). Variables like lat, lon, stime, etime are in data_vars but are 1D along 'profile' only. They pass the _available filter, but line 245 does dss[name].transpose('bin', 'profile') which raises ValueError: "Dimensions {'bin'} do not exist" for a profile-only variable. The result is an unhandled crash of the whole run on a request that looked valid (the variable IS present).
evidence: Repro: ds with epsilonMean(bin,profile) and lat(profile). is_pseudo_var('lat') is False but 'lat' in ds.data_vars is True, so _available('lat')==True and it becomes a panel var. ds['lat'].transpose('bin','profile').values raises 'ValueError: Dimensions {'bin'} do not exist. Expected one or more of (\'profile\',)'. There is no try/except or dim check around line 245.
suggested_fix: In _available (or before transpose), require that the variable has both 'bin' and 'profile' dims, e.g. set(dss[v].dims) >= {'bin','profile'}; otherwise treat it as missing (add to the 'missing' list) so the user gets a clear message instead of a traceback.
verify_reasoning:
VERIFIED REAL against current code on this branch.

Trace:
- src/odas_tpw/perturb/binning.py:503 stores lat/lon/stime/etime as (["profile"], ...) — 1D along profile only; data vars are (["bin","profile"]) at line 496. Confirmed these scalars are profile-only in the combo.
- profiles.py run() (line 350-351): when --var is given, variables = list(args.var) with NO dimensionality validation. sections.single_var_limit_guard (sections.py:340) only checks vmin/vmax, not dims. The --var arg (sections.py:313) is a free-form append.
- _build_profiles_figure._available (profiles.py:182-183) returns (v in dss.data_vars) or is_pseudo_var(v). For v='lat', 'lat' in data_vars is True and diagnostics.is_pseudo_var('lat') is False (diagnostics.py:47-49 only matches the 6 *_var names), so _available('lat')==True and 'lat' becomes a panel var — it is NOT routed to the 'missing' list.
- Line 245: np.asarray(dss[name].transpose("bin","profile").values, ...) with no try/except or dim guard.

Reproduced exactly on installed xarray 2026.2.0: ds['lat'].transpose('bin','profile') raises ValueError: "Dimensions {'bin'} do not exist. Expected one or more of ('profile',)". xarray transpose defaults to missing_dims='raise'. The end-to-end repro (mimicking a real depth combo with epsilonMean(bin,profile)+lat/lon/stime/etime(profile)) confirms _available('lat')==True then line 245 crashes. This aborts the whole profiles run with a cryptic traceback instead of the intended clean "variables not on product, skipped" message (profiles.py:191).

Severity minor (not major): the crash is genuine and unhandled, but it is only reachable when a user explicitly passes --var lat/lon/stime/etime — a profile-only coordinate that has no meaningful depth-section rendering. It is not triggered by the default vars, does not corrupt valid output, and represents an invalid/nonsensical request rather than a normal workflow. Net effect is a cryptic traceback instead of a clear "not a (bin,profile) variable" message. The suggested fix (require {'bin','profile'} ⊆ dims in _available, routing profile-only vars to 'missing') is correct. || VERIFIED REAL, NOT FIXED on fix/audit-pr73.

Trace through current code:
1. profiles.py:350-351 — when the user passes `--var lat` (or lon/stime/etime), `variables = list(args.var)` accepts the name verbatim; no dim filtering.
2. profiles.py:182-186 — `_available(v)` returns True if `v in dss.data_vars OR is_pseudo_var(v)`. For `lat`, `is_pseudo_var('lat')` is False (diagnostics.py:47-49 only matches the six `*_var` pseudo-names in `_DIAG_INFO`), but `'lat' in dss.data_vars` is True. Confirmed by binning.py:501-503: lat/lon/stime/etime are emitted as 1-D `data_vars` on the `profile` dim (`data_vars[sname] = (["profile"], sarr, ...)`), and the Dataset is built with only `bin`/`profile` as coords (binning.py:507-510). combo.finalize (combo.py:255-275) never promotes them to coordinates, so they remain profile-only data_vars in combo.nc. Therefore `_available('lat')` is True and `lat` goes into `panel_vars`, NOT `missing`.
3. profiles.py:240 — `is_pseudo` is False for `lat`, so the `else` branch at line 245 runs: `np.asarray(dss[name].transpose("bin","profile").values, dtype=float)[:, col]`.
4. Because `lat` has dims `('profile',)` only, `transpose('bin','profile')` raises `ValueError: Dimensions {'bin'} do not exist. Expected one or more of ('profile',)`. I reproduced this exactly with a minimal xr.Dataset (epsilonMean(bin,profile) + lat(profile)): the transpose raised that precise ValueError.
5. No try/except or dim check surrounds line 245. `single_var_limit_guard` (sections.py:340-346) only checks vmin/vmax vs len(variables); the only dim guard in run() is profiles.py:347 which checks the dataset has `profile`/`bin` dims, not the requested variable. The `finally` at profiles.py:376-377 only closes `ds`; it does not suppress. So the ValueError propagates and crashes the whole run with a cryptic message, on input that passed the package's own `_available` availability check.

The suggested fix (require `{'bin','profile'} <= set(dss[v].dims)` in `_available`, routing profile-only vars into the `missing` list) is sound.

Severity: major — unhandled traceback on input that the code itself reports as available, but it requires the user to explicitly request a profile-only var via `--var` (these are not in `product.default_vars`, so the default path is unaffected), keeping it short of blocker.

Relevant files: /Users/pat/tpw/turbulence/src/odas_tpw/perturb/plot/profiles.py (lines 182-186, 240-245, 350-351); /Users/pat/tpw/turbulence/src/odas_tpw/perturb/binning.py (lines 501-510); /Users/pat/tpw/turbulence/src/odas_tpw/perturb/plot/diagnostics.py (lines 34-49); /Users/pat/tpw/turbulence/src/odas_tpw/perturb/combo.py (lines 255-275).

title: chi/epsilon colorbar units mislabeled with a spurious 's' factor
description: The gamma panel plots chi/eps and labels the colorbar 'K^2 s kg J^-1'. Dimensional analysis: chi has units K^2 s^-1; eps has units W kg^-1 = J s^-1 kg^-1, so eps^-1 = s kg J^-1. chi/eps = (K^2 s^-1)(s kg J^-1) = K^2 kg J^-1 -- the seconds cancel. The label carries an extra 's' factor and is therefore wrong (should be K^2 kg J^-1).
evidence: chi units K^2 s^-1 (see chi panel label line 368: 'K^{2} s^{-1}'). eps units W kg^-1 = J s^-1 kg^-1 (eps panel line 361). chi/eps = K^2 s^-1 / (J s^-1 kg^-1) = K^2 kg J^-1. Line 375 string is r"$\chi / \varepsilon$  (K$^{2}$ s kg J$^{-1}$)" which has an extra 's'.
suggested_fix: Change the label to r"$\chi / \varepsilon$  (K$^{2}$ kg J$^{-1}$)".
verify_reasoning:
VERIFIED REAL. At src/odas_tpw/perturb/plot/eps_chi.py:352 the plotted quantity is gamma = chi / eps (a plain division, no other factors). The colorbar label at line 375 is r"$\chi / \varepsilon$  (K$^{2}$ s kg J$^{-1}$)".

Dimensional analysis, using the units the file itself asserts for the two inputs:
- chi units = K^2 s^-1 (chi panel label, line 368: "K$^{2}$ s$^{-1}$")
- eps units = W kg^-1 = J s^-1 kg^-1 (eps panel label, line 361: "W kg$^{-1}$"), so eps^-1 = s kg J^-1
- chi/eps = (K^2 s^-1) * (s kg J^-1) = K^2 kg J^-1 -- the s^-1 and s cancel.

The label therefore carries a spurious extra 's': it should read K^2 kg J^-1, not K^2 s kg J^-1. The finding's trace is exactly correct against the current code.

Severity: this is a cosmetic plot-label string only. It does not affect any computed value, data, or QC gate -- nothing downstream consumes the label. So it is a labeling nit (the finding said "minor"; I'd call it a nit since there is zero data/physics impact, but it is a genuine, provable defect). Fix: change line 375 to r"$\chi / \varepsilon$  (K$^{2}$ kg J$^{-1}$)".

(Side note, out of scope: this quantity is named "gamma" but is literally chi/eps, not the dimensionless mixing efficiency Gamma; that naming concern is separate from the units-label bug being verified here.) || Verified against current code on the branch (not git history).

src/odas_tpw/perturb/plot/eps_chi.py:
- Line 352: gamma = np.where((eps > 0) & np.isfinite(chi), chi / eps, np.nan) -- the plotted "gamma" quantity is literally chi divided by eps.
- Line 361: eps colorbar label is r"$\varepsilon$  (W kg$^{-1}$)".
- Line 368: chi colorbar label is r"$\chi$  (K$^{2}$ s$^{-1}$)".
- Line 375: gamma colorbar label is r"$\chi / \varepsilon$  (K$^{2}$ s kg J$^{-1}$)".

Dimensional analysis confirms the label is wrong:
- chi has units K^2 s^-1.
- eps has units W kg^-1 = J s^-1 kg^-1, so eps^-1 = s kg J^-1.
- chi/eps = (K^2 s^-1) * (s kg J^-1) = K^2 s^-1 * s * kg J^-1; the s^-1 and s cancel, leaving K^2 kg J^-1.

The label at line 375 contains an extra 's' factor ("K^2 s kg J^-1") and should be "K^2 kg J^-1". This is still present on the current branch; not fixed.

Impact: purely a cosmetic colorbar-label error on a diagnostic plot. No data, units-of-stored-data, physics, QC, or numerical correctness is affected -- only the human-readable axis label text. Hence severity nit (cosmetic), not a functional bug. Suggested fix: change line 375 to r"$\chi / \varepsilon$  (K$^{2}$ kg J$^{-1}$)".

Caveat worth noting: chi/eps is not the standard dimensionless mixing-efficiency/flux-coefficient Gamma (which is N^2*chi/(2*eps*<dT/dz>^2) and dimensionless). The variable is named "gamma" but is the raw chi/eps ratio with units K^2 kg J^-1; that naming is a separate (non-reported) concern and not part of this finding. For this finding specifically, the unit label as written is mathematically incorrect by an extra 's'.

title: eps-chi crashes when chiMean is on the combo but no chi_NN dir exists
description: When chi_combo_dir exists and carries chiMean, the data needed for the chi/gamma panels is fully available in chi_combo/combo.nc. But the branch requires 'has_chi_mean and chi_dir is not None' (line 262). _load_chi_from_combo only opens chi_dir to scrape title attrs (fft/diss/spectrum). If the per-profile chi_NN dir was deleted/archived but the combo remains, has_chi_mean is True yet chi_dir is None, so the code falls through line 275 ('elif chi_dir is not None' -> False) to line 279/280 and raises SystemExit('No chi_NN dir under ...'), refusing to plot data it actually has.
evidence: Lines 262-280: only two productive branches both require chi_dir is not None; the else raises. _load_chi_from_combo (lines 36-55) reads chi/depth/times/qc from the combo and only uses chi_attrs_dir for optional title attrs (sample = glob(...); attrs default {}). So chi_dir is not required for the data, only cosmetics, yet its absence is fatal.
suggested_fix: When has_chi_mean is True but chi_dir is None, still load from the combo (pass attrs={} for the title) instead of raising; only fall back to the legacy per-profile path when chiMean is absent.
verify_reasoning:
Verified against current code at src/odas_tpw/perturb/plot/eps_chi.py:258-286 and reproduced.

Trace: when chi_combo_dir exists with chiMean (has_chi_mean=True) but chi_dir is None, the two productive branches both gate on `chi_dir is not None` (line 262: `has_chi_mean and chi_dir is not None`; line 275: `elif chi_dir is not None`), so both are skipped and line 279-280 raises SystemExit('No chi_NN dir under {root}').

Root cause confirmed: _load_chi_from_combo (lines 36-55) reads ALL plotting data — chiMean, bin, stime, qc_drop_chi — from chi_combo/combo.nc. It uses chi_attrs_dir (=chi_dir) only to glob a per-profile *.nc for title attrs, defaulting to attrs={} (line 50) when none exist. Downstream title code (lines 382-406) only uses chi_attrs via .get()/`in` with '?' fallbacks, so empty attrs is safe.

Reachability confirmed: latest_stage_dir('chi') globs chi_[0-9][0-9] (falls back to chi/); latest_stage_dir('chi_combo') globs chi_combo_[0-9][0-9]. I verified chi_[0-9][0-9] does NOT match chi_combo_01 (the char after chi_ is 'c', not a digit). So a layout with only chi_combo_NN/ and no chi_NN/ yields chi_dir=None, chi_combo_dir set.

Reproduced end-to-end: built diss_combo_01/combo.nc + chi_combo_01/combo.nc (with chiMean) and no chi_NN dir; eps_chi.run raised SystemExit('No chi_NN dir under /tmp/reprotest') despite all panel data being present. Also confirmed _load_chi_from_combo with a nonexistent attrs dir returns full chi data with attrs={}, so the suggested fix (load from combo, attrs={}) works.

Severity minor: requires a non-default directory state (per-profile chi_NN archived/deleted while the combo is kept). It is a hard crash (SystemExit) not silent corruption, and the normal pipeline writes both dirs together so default runs are unaffected. Suggested fix: when has_chi_mean and chi_dir is None, still call _load_chi_from_combo(chi_combo_path, chi_dir_or_"") and only use the legacy per-profile path when chiMean is absent. || Confirmed against current code in /Users/pat/tpw/turbulence/src/odas_tpw/perturb/plot/eps_chi.py.

Trace (lines 258-286):
- chi_combo_dir and chi_dir are resolved independently via layout.latest_stage_dir() (layout.py:16-26), which returns None per-prefix. So chi_combo_dir (prefix "chi_combo") can be non-None while chi_dir (prefix "chi") is None — exactly when the per-profile chi_NN/ dirs are deleted/archived but chi_combo_NN/ remains.
- Line 261 sets has_chi_mean = "chiMean" in peek.data_vars (True here, read from chi_combo/combo.nc).
- Line 262: `if has_chi_mean and chi_dir is not None` -> False (chi_dir is None).
- Line 275: `elif chi_dir is not None` -> False.
- Line 279-280: `else: raise SystemExit("No chi_NN dir under ...")` -> fires.

The chi DATA is fully available without chi_dir: _load_chi_from_combo (lines 36-55) reads chi/depth/times/qc from chi_combo_path (lines 42-49). chi_dir (chi_attrs_dir) is used only at line 51 to scrape title attrs, and attrs defaults to {} (line 50) when no per-profile files are found. So chi_dir is needed only for cosmetic title metadata (fft/diss lengths, spectrum model, fp07 model, chi_method), not for the chi/gamma panels.

Net: with chiMean present in the combo but the per-profile chi dir absent, the script raises SystemExit and refuses to render a plot it has all the data for. Not already guarded — the else branch unconditionally raises.

Severity minor: requires the specific condition that per-profile chi_NN/ was removed while chi_combo_NN/ was kept (e.g., archival/cleanup of intermediate outputs). A normal perturb run produces both directories together, so this does not affect the standard pipeline; it is an edge-case crash with no data corruption. The suggested fix (when has_chi_mean is True but chi_dir is None, call _load_chi_from_combo with an empty-attrs path / pass attrs={}) is correct and low-risk.

title: Iterative-fit FOM mask diverges from Method 1 / MLE despite docstring claiming parity
description: The failed-fit fix added a comment (chi.py:590-593) asserting the iterative-fit FOM is computed on the same band as Method 1 and the MLE path: 'Restrict to above-noise bins (spec_obs > 2*noise), matching Method 1 and the MLE path'. In fact the iterative path builds its FOM mask as a raw strict comparison `valid_fom = (spec_obs > 2 * noise_K) & (K > 0) & (k_u >= K)` (line 595) with NO fallback, whereas Method 1 (line 286, using `valid`) and the MLE path (`_mle_fit_kB` line 441, using `fit_mask`) both reuse `_valid_wavenumber_mask`, which FALLS BACK to `(K>0) & (K<=K_AA)` when fewer than `min_points` bins clear 2*noise. So when few bins are above noise, the iterative FOM is computed on a different (and possibly empty -> NaN) band than the two methods the comment claims it matches. Impact is limited to the diagnostic `fom` value: chi FOM is only stored as an output (l4_chi.py:117, pipeline.py:749) and is never used as a QC gate in the chi combine (processing/chi_combine.py gates only on `chi <= chi_minimum`), so this does not corrupt chiMean/chi_final.
evidence: chi.py:595 `valid_fom = (spec_obs > 2 * noise_K) & (K > 0) & (k_u >= K)` has no min-points fallback. Compare Method 1 chi.py:286 `if np.sum(valid) >= 3 ...` where `valid=_valid_wavenumber_mask(...)` (line 217, which falls back), and `_mle_fit_kB` chi.py:441-445 using `fit_mask` from `_valid_wavenumber_mask` (line 324). grep confirms chi FOM is never a gate: `processing/chi_combine.py` line 89 `chi[chi <= chi_minimum] = np.nan` is the only chi filter; no `fom` reference exists in chi_combine.py.
suggested_fix: Either reword the comment to state the iterative FOM intentionally uses a strict above-noise mask (no fallback), or route it through `_valid_wavenumber_mask` for true parity with Method 1 and MLE.
verify_reasoning:
Verified against the current code on branch fix/audit-pr73. All core technical claims of the finding hold:

1. Comment claims parity: src/odas_tpw/chi/chi.py:590-593 states the iterative FOM is "Restrict[ed] to above-noise bins (spec_obs > 2*noise), matching Method 1 and the MLE path."

2. Iterative mask has NO fallback: chi.py:595 `valid_fom = (spec_obs > 2 * noise_K) & (K > 0) & (k_u >= K)`, guarded only by `if np.sum(valid_fom) >= 3` (line 596) with no widening to a fuller band — it just yields fom=NaN otherwise.

3. Method 1 and the MLE path DO have a fallback. `_valid_wavenumber_mask` (chi.py:121-155) falls back to `(K>0) & (K<=K_AA)` when fewer than `min_points` bins clear 2*noise (lines 153-154). Method 1 FOM uses `valid` from that helper (line 217 -> used at line 286). The MLE FOM (`_mle_fit_kB`) uses `fit_mask` from the same helper (line 397 via `_mle_find_kB` line 324 -> used at line 441). So in the low-signal regime the iterative FOM is computed on a strictly-above-noise (or empty -> NaN) band, while Method 1/MLE compute it on the full anti-aliasing band. The comment's "matching" claim is therefore inaccurate. This is a genuine code-vs-own-comment divergence, not a misread.

One correction to the finding's IMPACT paragraph: it asserts chi FOM is "never used as a QC gate" and that chi_combine.py is the only filter. That is wrong — perturb/pipeline.py:401 `_apply_fom_cut` gates `chi` / `chi_N` on `fom >= fom_max` (invoked at lines 1302-1305 when `chi.fom_max` is configured; config key at perturb/config.py:98/126/404/434, default None=off). So chi FOM CAN drive a QC cut. However: (a) the default is no cut, and (b) in the few-above-noise regime the helper's fallback dilutes Method 1/MLE FOM toward ~1.0 (which passes a fom<max cut), while the iterative path yields NaN there, and `_apply_fom_cut` line 429 only acts on `np.isfinite(fom)` — so a NaN iterative FOM is also not cut. Net QC outcome is thus largely the same, so practical impact remains small.

Severity nit is correct: this is a documentation/parity inconsistency that does not corrupt chiMean/chi_final under default config and produces effectively-equivalent QC decisions even with a configured cut. Not already fixed — the comment (lines 590-593) and the fallback-free mask (line 595) are both present in the current code. Suggested remediation: either reword the comment to state the iterative FOM deliberately uses a strict above-noise band with no fallback, or route it through `_valid_wavenumber_mask` for true parity. || Verified against current code on this branch.

CONFIRMED FACTS:
- chi.py:595 builds the iterative-fit FOM mask as a raw strict comparison `valid_fom = (spec_obs > 2 * noise_K) & (K > 0) & (k_u >= K)` with NO min-points fallback; if <3 bins pass it sets fom=NaN (lines 596,603-604).
- _valid_wavenumber_mask (chi.py:121-155) DOES fall back: line 153-154 replaces the mask with `(K > 0) & (K <= K_AA)` when fewer than min_points bins clear 2*noise.
- Method 1 reuses this helper for its FOM: `valid = _valid_wavenumber_mask(...)` at chi.py:217 (min_points=3), FOM computed on `valid` at lines 286-288.
- The MLE path _mle_fit_kB reuses it too: `fit_mask = _valid_wavenumber_mask(...)` at chi.py:324 (min_points=6, via _mle_find_kB), FOM computed on fit_mask at lines 441-445.
- The docstring comment at chi.py:591-593 asserts the iterative FOM is computed on a band "matching Method 1 and the MLE path," which is false: the iterative path uses a non-fallback strict mask AND additionally bounds by `k_u >= K` (the integration upper limit) rather than `K <= K_AA`. So when few bins clear 2*noise, the iterative FOM is computed on a different (possibly empty -> NaN) band than the two methods it names.

IMPACT (correctly limited): chi FOM is diagnostic-only. chi_combine.py contains no `fom` reference (grep confirms); it gates solely on chi magnitude (line 89: `chi[chi <= chi_minimum] = np.nan`) and inter-probe log-agreement (lines 152-172). Therefore the divergent FOM band cannot corrupt chiMean/chi_final or derived K_T/Gamma. The defect is a documentation overstatement plus a benign inconsistency in a stored diagnostic.

NOT already fixed: both the fallback-free mask (line 595) and the parity-claiming comment (lines 591-593) are present in the current code exactly as described. Severity nit is appropriate: real but cosmetic, no data/output corruption.

title: epsilon/chi multi-probe outlier removal can over-prune 3+ mutually-distant probes down to a single (possibly worst) survivor
description: When >=3 probes disagree by more than the 95% CI AND remain outside the CI after each drop, the `for _ in range(n_probes - 1)` loop keeps removing the furthest-from-ln-mean probe until only one survives. For a log-symmetric triple such as [1e-9, 1e-7, 1e-5] (median = the correct estimate 1e-7), the loop discards the good middle probe and converges on a single extreme (1e-9), 100x off. This is the documented Lueck/Rockland iterative rule, but it has no floor on how many probes it will reject and no preference for the cluster/median when no consensus pair remains.
evidence: Traced with the exact loop internals (reproduced from the source): start epsilon=[[1e-9,1e-7,1e-5]], CF95=~1.63. iter0 dev=[4.605,0,4.605] -> nanargmax returns LAST tie index 2, drops 1e-5 -> [[1e-9,1e-7,nan]]. iter1 ratio=4.605>CF95, dev=[2.303,2.303,nan] -> nanargmax returns index 1, drops the GOOD middle probe 1e-7 -> survivor [[1e-9,nan,nan]]. Final epsilonMean = 1e-9 (geometric mean of all three = 1e-7). Confirmed end-to-end via mk_epsilon_mean: epsilonMean = [1.e-09].
suggested_fix: This degenerate is NOT a regression introduced by the fix (I verified the prior always-drop-max code produced the same 1e-9 survivor on this input). If desired, bound the loop to stop once <=2 probes remain (keep the geometric mean of the surviving pair rather than dropping to one), or pick the survivor closest to the ln-median. Otherwise document that >=3 grossly-disagreeing probes collapse to a single estimate. Report kept at LOW confidence because it is pre-existing behavior and rare in 2-probe VMP data.
verify_reasoning:
VERIFIED REAL against current code at src/odas_tpw/processing/epsilon_combine.py:124-149 (mirror chi_combine.py:152-172).

Mechanism confirmed by direct reproduction of the exact loop and end-to-end via mk_epsilon_mean:
- Input epsilon=[[1e-9, 1e-7, 1e-5]] (log-symmetric triple; correct estimate = geometric mean = 1e-7).
- With realistic params (speed=0.7, nu=1e-6, diss_length=512, fs=512), mk_epsilon_mean produces epsilonLnSigma=0.411 -> CF95_range=1.14. The cross-probe log spread ratio=9.21 >> 1.14, so the outlier loop runs.
- The loop is `for _ in range(n_probes-1)` (=2 iterations) with NO floor on survivors. iter0: dev=[4.605,0,4.605]; np.nanargmax returns the LAST tie index (2), dropping the high extreme 1e-5 -> [1e-9,1e-7,nan]. iter1: ratio=4.605 still > CF95, dev=[2.303,2.303,nan], nanargmax returns index 1, dropping the GOOD middle probe 1e-7 -> survivor 1e-9. Final epsilonMean=1e-9, which is 100x below the correct 1e-7. Confirmed end-to-end (epsilonMean=[1.e-09]).

The loop never stops at the median/cluster: once no consensus pair remains it keeps removing the furthest-from-ln-mean probe until exactly one survives. There is no preference for the central value and no floor of >=2 survivors.

Pre-existing, NOT a regression: I reproduced the prior always-drop-max loop (git show main:...) on the same input and CF95; it also collapses to 1e-9. So the #52 symmetric-rejection fix did not introduce this; both the old and new code over-prune this degenerate triple.

Scope/severity: Only reachable when n_probes >= 3 (data-driven via n_shear = len(shear_names) in dissipation.py:110-111). The documented primary instrument (VMP-250IR_RT SN 479, ARCTERX) carries 2 shear probes, for which the n_probes>=3 branch never executes, so the primary dataset is unaffected. It only bites 3-4 probe configurations where 3+ probes mutually disagree by more than the 95% CI with no consensus pair — a rare condition. Correctly classified minor. Real silent-corruption edge case (collapses a correct median estimate to an extreme outlier), but narrow in practice and pre-existing.

Suggested fix (matches finding): bound the loop to stop once <=2 probes remain (keep geometric mean of the surviving pair) or pick the survivor closest to the ln-median, rather than dropping to a single estimate. Apply symmetrically to chi_combine.py. || VERIFIED REAL but a low-impact, pre-existing edge case; NOT fixed on this branch.

Code trace reproduced exactly (epsilon_combine.py:126-149; chi mirror chi_combine.py:152-172). With epsilon=[[1e-9,1e-7,1e-5]] and CF95_range=1.63: iter0 dev=[4.605,0,4.605], nanargmax returns last-tie index 2, drops 1e-5 -> [1e-9,1e-7,nan]; iter1 ratio=4.605>CF95, dev=[2.303,2.303,nan], nanargmax returns index 1, drops the GOOD middle probe 1e-7 -> survivor 1e-9. Final epsilonMean=1e-9, whereas the geometric mean / median of the triple is the correct 1e-7 (100x off). The loop has no floor preventing collapse to a single extreme when no consensus pair remains, and on a log-symmetric triple the "symmetric outlier rejection" the fix advertises actually discards the median.

Pre-existing, NOT a regression: confirmed by running the old always-drop-max code on the same input -> identical 1e-9 survivor. So the fix in commit 6558dd5 neither introduced nor cured this degenerate.

already_fixed=false: the n_probes>=3 guard (epsilon_combine.py:125 / chi_combine.py:152) does NOT prevent the collapse; it only skips the loop for 2-probe data. When 3+ probes are present the described failure occurs as stated.

Severity downgraded to nit (reporter said minor) because it is UNREACHABLE for this codebase's documented data: the VMP-250IR SN 479 ARCTERX instrument has exactly 2 shear probes (sh1/sh2) and 1-2 thermistors. docs/ARCHITECTURE.md:423-426 and CLAUDE.md confirm the two-probe configuration, where the loop is entirely gated off (n_probes>=3 is false). The defect can only manifest on a generic 3+ probe instrument (e.g. a MicroRider with 3-4 thermistors feeding mk_chi_mean) with three grossly-disagreeing estimates spanning >CF95 even after the first drop. It is also rare even there. Reporter's own low-confidence, "rare in 2-probe VMP data," pre-existing framing is accurate. Reasonable mitigations: stop the loop once <=2 probes remain (keep the geometric mean of the surviving pair) or select the survivor nearest the ln-median; at minimum document the collapse. Relevant files: /Users/pat/tpw/turbulence/src/odas_tpw/processing/epsilon_combine.py and /Users/pat/tpw/turbulence/src/odas_tpw/processing/chi_combine.py.

title: _nan_singular_bins crashes (LinAlgError: SVD did not converge) when AA contains NaN — a regression that aborts L3 processing on corrupted/dropout accelerometer data
description: The fix added `_nan_singular_bins`, which calls `np.linalg.cond(AA)` on the whole batch of accelerometer auto-spectral matrices. `np.linalg.cond` computes the condition number via SVD, and SVD raises `numpy.linalg.LinAlgError: SVD did not converge` whenever ANY matrix in the batch contains NaN or inf. The surrounding `np.errstate(invalid='ignore', divide='ignore')` context only suppresses floating-point WARNINGS; it does NOT catch this exception, so it propagates out of `clean_shear_spec` / `clean_shear_spec_batch`. There is no `try/except LinAlgError` anywhere up the call chain (l3.process_l3 at l3.py:157, rsi/dissipation.py:188, rsi/pipeline.py:337), so a single NaN accelerometer sample aborts processing of the entire .p file. This is a regression: the pre-fix code did `clean_UU = np.real(clean_UU)` with no cond call (np.linalg.solve returns NaN without raising on NaN AA — verified), and the ODAS reference clean_shear_spec.m:140 uses MATLAB mrdivide `UA/AA` which warns but never crashes. The fix thus introduced a hard crash where neither the prior code nor the reference had one. The NaN-in-AA scenario is reachable by design, not exotic: l2._filtfilt_nan / _hp_filter (l2.py:273) deliberately RESTORE NaN at originally-NaN vibration positions, l3._build_window_arrays (l3.py:47-50) copies l2.vib into vib_windows with no NaN filtering, and csd_matrix_batch's _detrend_batch (spectral.py:285) again restores NaN — so any NaN in the L1 vibration channel flows straight into AA. The whole point of that NaN-preservation chain is that l4's bad-bin handling (l4.py:347-355) gracefully drops NaN bins; the new cond() call breaks that contract by crashing first.
evidence: Reproduction (venv python): `clean_shear_spec_batch(accel_w, shear_w, 256, 512.0)` where `accel_w[0,500,0]=np.nan` (one corrupted sample in a (2,1024,2) window batch) raises `LinAlgError: SVD did not converge` — traceback ends in `np.linalg.cond` -> `svd` -> `_raise_linalgerror_svd_nonconvergence`. Direct minimal repro: building AA via `csd_matrix_batch` shows `AA has NaN? True`; calling `np.linalg.cond(AA_with_nan_bin)` raises the same LinAlgError. Confirmed pre-fix behavior is non-crashing: `np.linalg.solve(AA_with_nan, ua_H)` returns NaN with NO exception ('pre-fix solve OK (no crash)'). The single-arg path clean_shear_spec does NOT hit this because non-batch csd_matrix's per-segment scipy.signal.detrend raises ValueError on NaN, which is swallowed by the short-signal `except ValueError` branch (goodman.py:143) returning uncleaned spectra — so only the batched production path (the one l3 actually uses) crashes. Existing tests (tests/test_scor160_goodman.py:44-55) only exercise singular FINITE AA (rank-1 / zero matrices), never NaN-containing AA, so CI does not catch this.
suggested_fix: Mask out non-finite AA matrices before calling np.linalg.cond, treating them as singular (NaN the bin) rather than feeding them to SVD. e.g.: `finite_AA = np.all(np.isfinite(AA), axis=(-2,-1)); cond = np.full(AA.shape[:-2], np.inf); if np.any(finite_AA): with np.errstate(invalid='ignore', divide='ignore'): cond[finite_AA] = np.linalg.cond(AA[finite_AA]); bad = ~np.isfinite(cond) | (cond > 1e13); clean_UU[bad] = np.nan`. Verified this avoids the crash, still NaNs exactly-singular finite bins, and still NaNs the NaN-AA bins. Add a regression test with NaN in AA / a NaN vibration sample driven through clean_shear_spec_batch.
verify_reasoning:
VERIFIED REAL. The crash reproduces exactly as described against the current code on this branch.

Core mechanism (reproduced): `np.linalg.cond(AA)` computes the condition number via SVD, and SVD raises `numpy.linalg.LinAlgError: SVD did not converge` whenever ANY matrix in a batched stack contains NaN/inf. The `with np.errstate(invalid='ignore', divide='ignore')` at goodman.py:58 only suppresses FP warnings, not this exception (confirmed: numpy 2.4.6, errstate does not suppress). Running `clean_shear_spec_batch(accel_w, shear_w, 256, 512.0)` with one corrupted sample `accel_w[0,500,0]=np.nan` raises LinAlgError with the traceback ending at goodman.py:260 -> goodman.py:59 -> np.linalg.cond -> svd -> _raise_linalgerror_svd_nonconvergence (matches the reported repro).

Reachability of NaN-in-AA (verified by code trace): src/odas_tpw/scor160/spectral.py:284-285 (_detrend_batch) deliberately RESTORES NaN at originally-NaN positions, which then flows through the FFT and einsum into Cyy = AA (spectral.py:419). The vibration channel carrying that NaN is preserved by design: l2._filtfilt_nan (l2.py:273) restores NaN, _hp_filter feeds it (l2.py:94), and l3._build_window_arrays (l3.py:47-50) copies l2.vib into vib_windows with no NaN filtering before calling clean_shear_spec_batch(vib_windows, shear_windows, ...) at l3.py:157. The whole point of this NaN-preservation machinery is that l4's bad-bin handler (l4.py:347-355) gracefully interpolates a single NaN bin / rejects multiple; the new cond() call short-circuits that contract with a hard crash.

Regression confirmed (verified): the batched solve+matmul path (goodman.py:244-245) does NOT raise on NaN AA — np.linalg.solve returns NaN for the affected bin and np.matmul propagates it without raising (reproduced). So before _nan_singular_bins was added, the function returned cleanly with NaN bins that l4 handled. The new _nan_singular_bins (goodman.py:45-63, called at :194 and :260) is what converts that graceful path into a crash. Only the batched production path (used by epsilon L3) crashes; the single-arg clean_shear_spec is shielded because non-batch _detrend_segment (spectral.py:52) raises ValueError on NaN, caught at goodman.py:143.

Propagation confirmed (verified): _compute_epsilon (dissipation.py:188) and _process_profile (pipeline.py:337) call process_l3 with no try/except, and the run_pipeline per-file/per-profile loop (pipeline.py:165, :218) has no try/except either, so the exception aborts the whole run. NOTE: np.linalg.LinAlgError IS a subclass of ValueError, but the only (ValueError, RuntimeError) handlers in pipeline.py (lines 388/403/417/527) wrap the CHI spectra/mixing paths, not the epsilon process_l3 — so the epsilon-side crash is genuinely uncaught.

Suggested fix verified: masking non-finite AA before cond (finite_AA = np.all(np.isfinite(AA), axis=(-2,-1)); cond[finite_AA] = np.linalg.cond(...); NaN the rest) avoids the crash, still NaNs exactly-singular finite bins (rank-1), and NaNs the NaN-AA bins (reproduced all three cases).

Severity: major is appropriate. One caveat that does not change the verdict: the NetCDF read path itself (io.py:339, np.asarray of a masked ACC/VIB array) fills masked values with the fill value, not NaN, so the trigger requires NaN already present in l1.vib rather than being injected by the loader. But the codebase intentionally built a NaN-tolerant vibration/shear pipeline for exactly such inputs, the pre-fix code and the ODAS reference (clean_shear_spec.m mrdivide UA/AA) did not crash, and this fix is the one that introduced the crash. Existing tests (tests/test_scor160_goodman.py) only exercise finite singular AA, so CI does not catch it. || VERIFIED REAL and NOT fixed on the current branch (fix/audit-pr73). Reproduced the crash end-to-end through the real production API.

Code trace (current code, not history):
- goodman.py:58-62 `_nan_singular_bins` calls `np.linalg.cond(AA)` inside `with np.errstate(invalid="ignore", divide="ignore")`. errstate only suppresses FP WARNINGS, not exceptions. `np.linalg.cond` computes the condition number via SVD, and SVD raises `numpy.linalg.LinAlgError: SVD did not converge` when ANY matrix in the batch contains NaN/inf — confirmed empirically on numpy 2.4.6 for both a fully-NaN bin and a single-NaN-entry bin.
- It is called at goodman.py:194 (clean_shear_spec) and goodman.py:260 (clean_shear_spec_batch).
- Direct reproduction via the production API: `clean_shear_spec_batch(accel_w, shear_w, 256, 512.0)` with one corrupted sample `accel_w[0,500,0]=np.nan` in a (2,1024,2) batch raises `LinAlgError: SVD did not converge`. Traceback ends exactly at goodman.py:260 -> _nan_singular_bins:59 -> np.linalg.cond -> svd -> _raise_linalgerror_svd_nonconvergence.

Why earlier guards do NOT intercept it (verified):
- The batched `np.linalg.solve(AA, ua_H)` at goodman.py:244 does NOT raise on NaN AA — it returns NaN silently (confirmed). So the per-frequency `except np.linalg.LinAlgError` fallback (lines 246-257) that would NaN bins is never entered; control flows straight to line 259-260 and crashes in `_nan_singular_bins`.
- The single-arg `clean_shear_spec` path is protected only incidentally: non-batch csd_matrix's per-segment scipy detrend raises ValueError on NaN, swallowed at goodman.py:143. The batched `_detrend_batch` instead zero-fills then re-restores NaN (spectral.py:262-287, esp. 284-285), so the batched production path — the one l3 uses — feeds NaN into AA and crashes.

Reachability (real, by design):
- L1 vib loaded from NetCDF ACC/VIB via np.asarray(...,float64) (io.py:339-343,499-503); NetCDF fill/masked values and sensor dropout become NaN with no scrub.
- l2 `_hp_filter`/`_filtfilt_nan` DELIBERATELY restore NaN at originally-NaN positions (l2.py:272-274, documented).
- l3 `_build_window_arrays` copies l2.vib into vib_windows with no NaN filtering (l3.py:45-50).
- l3.process_l3 calls clean_shear_spec_batch(vib_windows, ...) whenever Goodman active and n_vib>0 (l3.py:156-162).
- The NaN-preservation chain exists precisely because l4 gracefully handles NaN spectral bins (l4.py:347-355: interpolate one, invalidate multiple). `_nan_singular_bins` crashes before l4 can apply that contract.

Propagation: no try/except LinAlgError anywhere up the chain — l3.py has no try/except; dissipation.py:188 `l3 = process_l3(...)` is unwrapped; pipeline likewise. A single NaN accelerometer sample therefore aborts processing of the entire .p file.

Regression confirmed: the cond() call was added by the audit fix; pre-fix `np.linalg.solve` returns NaN without raising, and the ODAS reference clean_shear_spec.m mrdivide warns but never crashes — so the fix introduced a hard crash where neither prior code nor the reference had one.

Severity major (not blocker): it is a real uncaught crash that aborts a whole file, but it requires NaN in the accelerometer channel (dropout/corruption/fill values) rather than firing on clean data every run. Existing tests (tests/test_scor160_goodman.py) only exercise FINITE singular AA, so CI does not catch it. Suggested fix is sound: mask non-finite AA matrices before np.linalg.cond (treat them as singular/NaN the bin) instead of feeding them to SVD; add a NaN-AA regression test.

title: Sibling ULP-depth bug: per-profile eps/chi merge splits equal depth bins, causing silent data loss in combine_profiles
description: The combine.py fix added `_depth_key`/`_DEPTH_ATOL` quantization (combine.py:8-17,54,79) specifically because each per-profile `bin_by_depth` builds its own `np.arange(p_min, p_max+bs, bs)` center grid, so two profiles produce mathematically-equal-but-ULP-distinct bin centers (e.g. 0.35 vs 0.35000000000000003). However, the SIBLING merge one step earlier was NOT given the same tolerance. In rsi/pipeline.py:533-556, epsilon is binned over `l4.pres` and chi over `l4_chi_eps.pres` — two independent L4 stages with different `n_spectra`, hence different `[min,max]` pressure ranges and different arange offsets. They are joined with `binned = binned.merge(extra, join='outer')` (line 556), which uses xarray's exact-coordinate alignment with NO ULP tolerance. The two ULP-shifted grids therefore do NOT align: every overlapping physical depth becomes TWO depth_bin rows (one carrying epsilon with chi=NaN, the other carrying chi with epsilon=NaN). When this ULP-split L5_binned dataset later reaches combine_profiles, `canon.setdefault(_depth_key(d), d)` collapses both rows onto ONE key, and the fill loop (combine.py:78-81) maps BOTH ds_depths entries to the SAME column index — the second-processed one (a NaN) overwrites the real value. Net effect: in the eps/chi overlap region, most depth bins end up with one variable NaN'd out. Triggers only when bin_size is not binary-exact (e.g. 0.1, 0.2, 0.3); the default bin_size=1.0 yields x.5 centers and integer p_min, which are binary-exact and unaffected.
evidence: Reproduction (bin_size=0.1): epsilon centers = centers(0.3,10.0,0.1) (97 bins), chi centers = centers(0.0,10.0,0.1) (100 bins). `eps.merge(chi, join='outer')` yields 184 depth_bins with 84 ULP-duplicate pairs (diff<1e-6, e.g. overlap near 1.0 prints as [1.05, 1.05] but values differ by ~2.8e-12); 171/184 rows have exactly one of eps/chi present. Feeding that merged dataset through combine_profiles' _depth_key collapse + fill loop: of 87 overlap depths, 76 end up with exactly one variable present (the other NaN) — i.e. ~75 chi values silently lost. The default bin_size=1.0 case was verified safe (centers exactly equal across profiles).
suggested_fix: Apply the same canonical-depth quantization to the per-profile eps/chi join. E.g. before merging, snap `chi_bin`'s depth_bin coord onto eps_bin's grid within _DEPTH_ATOL (reindex/assign_coords with a quantized key), or build a single shared bin grid for both eps and chi (compute pres_range as the union of l4.pres and l4_chi_eps.pres and pass `pres_range=(p_min,p_max)` to both bin_by_depth calls so both use one arange). Either makes the line-556 outer-merge align exactly and eliminates the split.
verify_reasoning:

The suggested fix (share a single arange grid by passing pres_range=(union_min, union_max) to both bin_by_depth calls, or snap chi_bin's depth_bin coord onto eps_bin's grid within _DEPTH_ATOL before merging) is correct.

(2) The trigger is data-dependent — I verified bin_size=0.1 loses 83 values, but 0.2, 0.25, 0.3, 0.5 happen to produce bit-identical overlapping centers across the two grids (LOST=0) because their np.arange accumulation coincides; only certain non-binary-exact sizes like 0.1 diverge.
