(pipeline.py:113, plumbed through to _process_profile at pipeline.py:258), when a caller passes a triggering value such as 0.1 for fine vertical resolution.

title: QC drop-flag reindex uses np.maximum instead of bitwise-OR, contradicting its 'ORs a drop bitfield' contract
description: In `_reindex_rows_to_depth`, the `reduce="max"` branch is documented (docstring lines 109-110 and call-site comment) as the path that "ORs a drop bitfield so a flagged source bin flags its destination." The qc_drop_chi / qc_drop_epsilon values are genuine uint8 CF bitfields: they are produced by bitwise-OR in `qc_gate.compute_segment_drop` (line 108: `qc[i] |= np.bitwise_or.reduce(...)`) and pooled into combo bins by bitwise-OR in `binning._bin_or` (line 150: `np.bitwise_or.at(...)`). The reindex step, however, combines the bitfields across source bins with `np.maximum` (line 128), which is NOT a bitwise OR: e.g. flags 0b01 and 0b10 OR to 0b11 (=3) but max to 0b10 (=2), silently dropping the bit-0 flag. This is currently harmless because every downstream consumer collapses the flag to a `> 0` boolean (eps_chi.py line 328 `mask = np.isfinite(chi_qc) & (chi_qc > 0)`; profiles.py line 228 same), so the drop/keep decision is identical under max or OR. It is therefore a latent defect / contract violation, not an active data-corruption bug: any future consumer that decodes individual flag bits (via the flag_meanings/flag_masks attrs that the pipeline deliberately preserves through compute_segment_drop) would see corrupted bitfields after this reindex.
evidence: Source flags are real bitfields built by OR: qc_gate.py:108 `qc[i] |= np.bitwise_or.reduce(arr_u[a:b])`; binning.py:150 `np.bitwise_or.at(pooled, idx_v, vals_v)` with module docstring 'preserves flag_meanings / flag_masks'. The reindex combines them with max, proven divergent: `python3 -c 'import numpy as np; print(np.maximum(1,2), 1|2)'` -> `2 3`. Downstream only tests >0 (eps_chi.py:328, profiles.py:228), so the drop decision is unaffected today — confirmed by repro: reindexing qc rows [[0,0],[2,0],[0,1],[0,0]] (src 1.0,1.4,2.0,2.4 -> dst 1.0,2.0) yields [[2,0],[0,1]], whose >0 mask is correct. The defect surfaces only if a bit is decoded after reindex.
suggested_fix: Replace the max accumulation with a true bitwise OR for the flag path, e.g. cast the row to integer and `acc[dst, ok] = np.bitwise_or(acc[dst, ok].astype(np.int64), row[ok].astype(np.int64))` (with acc kept integer for that branch), or document explicitly that the reindexed flag is a presence indicator (>0) only and not a decodable bitfield, so the flag_meanings/flag_masks attrs are dropped after reindexing to avoid implying the bits are intact.
verify_reasoning:
VERIFIED against current code on this branch. Every claim in the finding holds:

1. /Users/pat/tpw/turbulence/src/odas_tpw/perturb/plot/eps_chi.py:128 uses `acc[dst, ok] = np.maximum(acc[dst, ok], row[ok])` in the `reduce == "max"` branch.
2. The docstring (lines 108-110) explicitly contracts that branch: "reduce='max' ORs a drop bitfield so a flagged source bin flags its destination." So the documented intent is bitwise-OR, not max.
3. The qc values are genuine CF bitfields: qc_gate.py:108 `qc[i] |= np.bitwise_or.reduce(arr_u[a:b])`; binning.py:150 `np.bitwise_or.at(pooled, idx_v, vals_v)`. The pipeline deliberately preserves `flag_meanings`/`flag_masks` (qc_gate.py:130-132), so the bits are meant to be individually decodable.
4. Only the chi_qc path uses reduce="max" (eps_chi.py:272-273).
5. max != OR is proven by repro: np.maximum(1,2)=2 vs 1|2=3. When two source bins carrying distinct flag bits (0b01 and 0b10) collapse to the same destination, max yields 2, silently dropping bit-0; a true OR yields 3.

CRITICAL nuance (confirms this is latent, not active): every current downstream consumer collapses the flag to a `> 0` boolean before use — eps_chi.py:324 (`eps_qc > 0`), eps_chi.py:328 (`chi_qc > 0`), profiles.py:228 and :247 (`qc > 0`). My repro confirms the `>0` drop/keep mask is identical under max and OR. So there is NO present data corruption, crash, or broken QC gate; the drop decision is provably unchanged today.

Therefore this is a real contract violation / latent defect (max is not the bitwise-OR the docstring promises), but it has zero behavioral effect on any current code path. It would only surface if future code decodes individual flag bits after reindexing. Severity nit is appropriate, matching the finding's own self-classification. Not already fixed: the np.maximum is present in the working tree. || Verified against current code on branch fix/audit-pr73. In src/odas_tpw/perturb/plot/eps_chi.py the function _reindex_rows_to_depth (lines 91-134) has a reduce="max" branch at line 128: `acc[dst, ok] = np.maximum(acc[dst, ok], row[ok])`. Its own docstring (lines 109-110) and the only call site (lines 272-273, for chi_qc) document this path as "ORs a drop bitfield so a flagged source bin flags its destination." The inputs ARE genuine uint8 CF bitfields: qc_gate.py:108 builds them with `qc[i] |= np.bitwise_or.reduce(arr_u[a:b])` and lifts flag_meanings/flag_masks attrs through (qc_gate.py:110-119); binning._bin_or pools with np.bitwise_or.at. But np.maximum is not a bitwise OR (verified: np.maximum(1,2)=2 vs 1|2=3 — the bit-0 flag is silently dropped). So the reindex violates its stated OR contract and can corrupt multi-bit flag values. This is harmless TODAY because every downstream consumer collapses the flag to a >0 boolean (eps_chi.py:324,328; profiles.py:228,247) and a grep across perturb/plot/ for any bit-decoding (flag_masks/bitwise_and/& mask) finds none — so the drop/keep decision is unaffected. It is a latent contract-violation/correctness bug, not active data corruption; it surfaces only if a future consumer decodes individual bits after the reindex. Note for the fix: acc (eps_chi.py:121) is float and shared with the mean branch, so a correct OR must use an integer accumulator/cast for the flag path, not OR through the float acc. Severity nit is appropriate given no current observable failure.

title: bin_by_time silently ignores `diagnostics`: no *_std / *_n / n_samples emitted for time-binned diss/chi/profiles
description: bin_by_time() accepts a `diagnostics: bool` parameter (line 518) but its body never references it. The depth path (bin_by_depth -> _bin_snapshot, lines 320-322, 471-492) produces per-bin `<var>_std`, `<var>_n`, and `n_samples` when diagnostics=True, but the time path produces none of them. bin_diss/bin_chi route to bin_by_time whenever method=='time' (lines 606, 623), and run_pipeline forwards the config-driven `diagnostics` flag to bin_by_time (line 1983-1985) and bin_diss/bin_chi (lines 1991-1999, 2005-2013). So configuring `binning.method: time` together with `binning.diagnostics: true` yields binned products that silently lack every diagnostic field the user requested, with no warning. Pre-existing (not in the in-range/LnSigma fix scope) but still present.
evidence: grep of `diagnostics` in binning.py shows the symbol in the bin_by_time signature (line 518) and in the bin_diss/bin_chi pass-through calls (lines 606, 623), but NO use of `diagnostics` anywhere in the bin_by_time body (lines 527-589). Contrast _bin_snapshot lines 320-322 (`if diagnostics: out['stds'][vname]=_bin_std(...); out['finite_counts'][vname]=_bin_finite_counts(...)`) and bin_by_depth lines 487-492 which build `n_samples`. pipeline.py:1983 calls `bin_by_time(prof_ncs, bin_width, aggregation, diagnostics, ...)`, so the flag is reachable from config.
suggested_fix: Either implement the diagnostics outputs in bin_by_time (per-bin std via _bin_std, finite counts via _bin_finite_counts, and an n_samples var) mirroring the depth path, or emit a one-time logger.warning when diagnostics=True and method=='time'. If intentionally unsupported, drop the unused parameter and adjust callers.
verify_reasoning:
VERIFIED REAL and NOT already fixed. Traced the actual current code on this branch:

1. src/odas_tpw/perturb/binning.py:514-589 — bin_by_time() declares `diagnostics: bool = False` (line 518) but its entire body (lines 527-589) never references `diagnostics`. It builds only the aggregated value vars (binned_data) plus the `time` coord, and concatenates them. No `<var>_std`, `<var>_n`, or `n_samples` are emitted.

2. The depth path DOES emit them when diagnostics=True: _bin_snapshot lines 320-322 set out["stds"][vname]=_bin_std(...) and out["finite_counts"][vname]=_bin_finite_counts(...); bin_by_depth lines 471-492 materialize result_vars[f"{vname}_std"], result_vars[f"{vname}_n"], and result_vars["n_samples"]. Confirmed by reading both regions.

3. Routing confirmed: bin_diss (line 606) and bin_chi (line 623) call bin_by_time(..., diagnostics, ...) whenever method=='time', forwarding the flag into the function that ignores it.

4. Reachable from config: pipeline.py:1898 bin_method = binning_cfg.get("method", "depth") and pipeline.py:1901 diagnostics = binning_cfg.get("diagnostics", False) are both user-config-driven. They are forwarded to bin_by_time at line 1984 (profiles) and to bin_diss/bin_chi at lines 1996/2010. So configuring binning.method: time + binning.diagnostics: true silently produces binned diss/chi/profile NetCDFs lacking every diagnostic field, with no warning.

This is genuine silent-feature-loss, not a misread. Severity minor: no data/units corruption, no crash, no incorrect physics — the aggregated values themselves are correct; only the user-requested optional diagnostic outputs (std/n/n_samples) are silently dropped under time binning. Suggested remediation: implement the diagnostics outputs in bin_by_time mirroring the depth path, or emit a one-time logger.warning when diagnostics=True under time binning, or drop the unused parameter if intentionally unsupported. || Confirmed against current code on branch fix/audit-pr73.

bin_by_time (src/odas_tpw/perturb/binning.py:514-589) declares `diagnostics: bool = False` in its signature (line 518) but its body (lines 527-589) never references the symbol. It builds binned_data only with the aggregated values per variable (lines 552-578), plus QC-flag OR-pooling and LnSigma quadrature handling, and returns a Dataset with just those vars on the time dim (lines 580-589). No `<var>_std`, `<var>_n`, or `n_samples` field is ever created.

By contrast the depth path honors the flag: _bin_snapshot at lines 320-322 populates out['stds'] (via _bin_std) and out['finite_counts'] (via _bin_finite_counts) when diagnostics is True, and bin_by_depth at lines 471-492 materializes `<vname>_std`, `<vname>_n`, and an `n_samples` var into the output. So the two binning strategies produce structurally different outputs for the same user request.

Reachability from config is confirmed: pipeline.py reads `bin_method = binning_cfg.get("method", "depth")` (line 1898) and `diagnostics = binning_cfg.get("diagnostics", False)` (line 1901) as independent keys of the same `binning` block. With method=='time' it calls bin_by_time(prof_ncs, bin_width, aggregation, diagnostics, log_dir=...) (lines 1983-1985), and bin_diss/bin_chi (lines 1991-1999, 2005-2013) which route to bin_by_time at binning.py:606 and 623 — passing the same diagnostics flag through. Therefore a config with `binning.method: time` and `binning.diagnostics: true` yields binned diss/chi/profile products lacking every requested diagnostic field, with no warning logged.

Not a crash, data-corruption, or units bug — it is silent loss of an explicitly requested diagnostic feature, so severity minor is correct. Not in the in-range/LnSigma fix scope and not addressed elsewhere; already_fixed is false. Fix: either implement the diagnostics outputs in bin_by_time mirroring the depth path, or emit a one-time warning (or drop the unused parameter) so the request is not silently dropped.

title: CTD per-file NCs are never pruned: orphaned casts from dropped .p files silently leak into the CTD combo (sibling of the bug the fix addressed)
description: The orphan-prune fix (_prune_orphan_profile_ncs) is applied only to the ('profiles','diss','chi') stage dirs. The CTD stage writes one NetCDF per source .p file into a config-content-hashed (therefore reused-across-runs) directory, and the CTD combo globs every *.nc in that dir. So the exact failure the fix was written to prevent — a re-run on a reduced/changed input set leaving orphaned per-source NCs that the binning/combo glob folds in — is STILL present on the CTD path. A dropped or renamed .p file leaves its stale {stem}.nc in the ctd dir, and make_ctd_combo concatenates it into the published CTD combo, corrupting the CTD time series with data from files no longer in the input set. Worse: even if 'ctd' were added to the prune loop as-is, it would not help — CTD files are named '{stem}.nc' (ctd.py:408), not '{stem}_prof{NNN}.nc', so neither the prune's glob ('*_prof*.nc', line 66) nor its prefix test ('{stem}_prof', line 64) matches CTD files at all.
evidence: pipeline.py:1916 iterates only ('profiles','diss','chi'). ctd_bin_file writes out_path = output_dir / f'{stem}.nc' (ctd.py:408) into output_dirs['ctd'] (pipeline.py:1061), a dir from resolve_output_dir whose signature is config-only (config_base.py:185, sections+upstream, no input-file set). _run_combo passes output_dirs.get('ctd') as ctd_dir (pipeline.py:2023) to make_ctd_combo -> make_combo -> sorted(binned_dir.glob('*.nc')) (combo.py:302, 72). Reproduction: with live stems {fileA,fileB} and a prior-run orphan fileC.nc, running the exact prune loop from lines 1916-1918 leaves the profiles dir as [fileA_prof000.nc, fileB_prof000.nc] (orphan pruned) but the ctd dir as [fileA.nc, fileB.nc, fileC.nc] (fileC orphan KEPT and would be globbed by the CTD combo).
suggested_fix: Prune the CTD dir too, with CTD-aware matching: e.g. delete any {stem}.nc in output_dirs['ctd'] whose stem (filename without .nc suffix) is not in valid_stems (exact membership, not prefix). Add a separate prune helper for the '{stem}.nc' naming, or extend _prune_orphan_profile_ncs to accept the naming pattern.
verify_reasoning:
VERIFIED REAL against current code on branch fix/audit-pr73. Every link in the trace checks out:

1) Prune scope (pipeline.py:1916-1918): the loop `for _stage in ("profiles","diss","chi")` excludes 'ctd'. _prune_orphan_profile_ncs (pipeline.py:47-77) is the ONLY cleanup mechanism in the perturb pipeline (grep for unlink/rmtree/prune confirms no other CTD cleanup exists).

2) CTD dir is config-only-hashed and reused across runs: resolve_output_dir (config_base.py:199-229) keys the dir on compute_hash(section, params, upstream) which canonicalizes only config sections (config_base.py:169-195). The ctd_chain upstream (pipeline.py:149-158) is purely config sections (files, gps, hotel, speed, qc, ct, profiles, stratification). files_p = merge_config("files", config.get("files")) is config defaults overlaid with config, NOT the resolved list of .p files (the actual file list enters run_pipeline as the p_files arg, never the "files" config section). So dropping/renaming/explicitly-reducing the input .p set does NOT change the CTD dir hash; the dir is reused and orphaned {stem}.nc files persist.

3) CTD naming (ctd.py:407-408): out_path = output_dir / f"{stem}.nc" where stem = output_stem (the same _output_stem used at pipeline.py:1915 to build valid_stems). NOT the {stem}_prof{NNN}.nc pattern. Empirically reproduced: running the real _prune_orphan_profile_ncs on a CTD-style dir prunes 0 files because its glob '*_prof*.nc' (line 66) and prefix test '{stem}_prof' (line 64) never match '{stem}.nc'. So even adding 'ctd' to the loop as-is would not help.

4) CTD combo folds orphans in: pipeline.py:2106-2117 calls make_ctd_combo(ctd_dir, ...) -> make_combo(ctd_dir,..., method="time") (combo.py:302) -> sorted(binned_dir.glob('*.nc')) (combo.py:72). With method="time", make_combo only coalesces EXACT-timestamp duplicates (combo.py:112-114: keep[1:] &= tk[1:] != tk[:-1]). An orphan from a dropped .p file carries distinct cast timestamps, so it is NOT coalesced away — its rows are concatenated into the published CTD combo time series.

Empirical reproduction (executed): with valid_stems={fileA,fileB} and a stale fileC orphan from a prior run, the exact prune loop (lines 1916-1918) leaves the profiles dir as [fileA_prof000.nc, fileB_prof000.nc] (orphan pruned) but the ctd dir as [fileA.nc, fileB.nc, fileC.nc] (orphan KEPT), and the combo glob would fold fileC.nc in.

This is the identical failure mode _prune_orphan_profile_ncs was written to prevent, still present on the CTD path, causing silent corruption of the published CTD time series with data from files no longer in the input set.

Minor inaccuracies in the report that do not affect correctness of the finding: the stem helper is the nested local _output_stem (pipeline.py:1840) wrapping _source_output_stem, not a module-level _output_stem; cited line combo.py:72 is the make_combo glob and combo.py:302 the make_ctd_combo delegation (both correct). The mechanism and impact are exactly as described.

Severity: major rather than blocker because corruption only manifests on the specific workflow of re-running the same config with a reduced/changed input .p set into a pre-existing results tree (not on a clean run or full re-run). When it does occur it is silent data corruption of a published product, matching the severity assigned to the original profiles/diss/chi bug this fix addressed. || Verified against current code on fix/audit-pr73. The finding is real and unaddressed.

CHAIN OF EVIDENCE (all confirmed by reading current code):

1. Orphan-prune covers only profiles/diss/chi. pipeline.py:1916-1918 iterates `for _stage in ("profiles", "diss", "chi")` and calls `_prune_orphan_profile_ncs`. There is no `ctd` in that tuple, and `grep` across the entire perturb package shows the only prune/orphan call sites are these (pipeline.py:47-77 def, 1918 call). No CTD prune exists anywhere.

2. The CTD dir is config-reused, not input-file keyed. `dirs["ctd"] = resolve_output_dir(output_root, "ctd", "ctd", ctd_params, upstream=_upstream_for("ctd", config))` (pipeline.py:1614-1620). `resolve_output_dir` (config_base.py:199-235) hashes only section/params/upstream (`compute_hash(section, params, upstream=upstream)`, line 214) — the resolved input .p-file set is never part of the signature. So the same dir is reused across runs with the same config, and per-file CTD NCs from a prior run on a larger/different input set persist.

3. CTD writes one `{stem}.nc` per source .p into that exact dir. `ctd_bin_file(pf, gps, output_dirs["ctd"], ...)` (pipeline.py:1058-1071) -> `out_path = output_dir / f"{stem}.nc"; ds.to_netcdf(out_path)` (ctd.py:407-409). Naming is `{stem}.nc`, NOT `{stem}_prof{NNN}.nc`.

4. The CTD combo globs every .nc in that dir. `_run_combo` passes `output_dirs.get("ctd")` as `ctd_dir` (pipeline.py:2023); `make_ctd_combo(ctd_dir, ...)` (pipeline.py:2106-2113) -> `make_combo(ctd_dir, ..., method="time")` (combo.py:302) -> `nc_files = sorted(binned_dir.glob("*.nc"))` (combo.py:72), then concatenates all of them lengthwise. So an orphaned `{stem}.nc` from a dropped/renamed .p file is folded into the published CTD combo, corrupting the CTD time series with data from files no longer in the input set.

EMPIRICAL REPRODUCTION (ran the verbatim prune-helper logic): with profiles=[fileA_prof000.nc, fileB_prof000.nc, fileC_prof000.nc] and ctd=[fileA.nc, fileB.nc, fileC.nc], valid_stems={fileA,fileB}: profiles correctly pruned to [fileA_prof000.nc, fileB_prof000.nc]; ctd left as [fileA.nc, fileB.nc, fileC.nc] (orphan kept). Confirmed the secondary claim too: running `_prune_orphan_profile_ncs` on the ctd dir prunes 0 files because it globs `*_prof*.nc` (line 66) and tests prefix `{stem}_prof` (line 64), neither of which matches `{stem}.nc`. So even a naive `("profiles","diss","chi","ctd")` extension would NOT fix it.

This is the exact failure mode the orphan-prune fix was written to prevent, still present on the CTD path. Severity major (not blocker): silent data corruption of a published product, but only triggers on a re-run with a reduced/changed input set into a reused (same-config) output tree — not on a clean first run. Correct fix is a CTD-aware prune keyed on exact `{stem}.nc` membership against valid_stems.

Relevant files: /Users/pat/tpw/turbulence/src/odas_tpw/perturb/pipeline.py (1916-1918, 1610-1620, 1058-1071, 2023, 2106-2113, 47-77), /Users/pat/tpw/turbulence/src/odas_tpw/perturb/ctd.py (407-409), /Users/pat/tpw/turbulence/src/odas_tpw/perturb/combo.py (72, 302), /Users/pat/tpw/turbulence/src/odas_tpw/config_base.py (199-235).

title: Orphan prune uses startswith(prefix) instead of exact stem membership, so an orphan whose stem is '{livestem}_prof...' is wrongly kept and still leaks into the combo
description: _prune_orphan_profile_ncs keeps a file iff its name startswith '{stem}_prof' for some valid stem. Because profile filenames are '{stem}_prof{NNN}.nc' and stems are free-form (derived from arbitrary .p file/dir names via _source_output_stem), a DROPPED stem of the form '{livestem}_prof<...>' produces orphan files like '{livestem}_prof<...>_prof000.nc' that start with the live stem's '{livestem}_prof' prefix and are therefore NOT pruned. The docstring asserts the only error direction is 'keeping a stray orphan, never dropping live data' and frames the kept orphan as harmless, but it is not harmless: the binning step globs '*.nc' (lines 1925/1936/1956), so a kept orphan is folded straight into the binned product and combo — exactly the corruption the fix was meant to eliminate. The fix thus does not actually guarantee orphan removal.
evidence: Line 64: prefixes = tuple(f'{s}_prof' for s in valid_stems); line 67: if not nc.name.startswith(prefixes): unlink. Reproduction: live stem 'cast', dropped stem 'cast_prof' (e.g. from a file named cast_prof.p). Files cast_prof000.nc (live) and cast_prof_prof000.nc (orphan). With valid_stems={'cast'}, the prune leaves BOTH: 'cast_prof_prof000.nc'.startswith('cast_prof') is True, so the orphan is kept and would be globbed by bin_by_depth/bin_diss/bin_chi.
suggested_fix: Match exact stems, not prefixes: parse each filename with a regex like r'^(?P<stem>.+)_prof\d+\.nc$' and keep iff match and match['stem'] in valid_stems; delete files that match the profile-name shape but whose parsed stem is not a current stem. This also tightens the glob ('*_prof*.nc') vs binning glob ('*.nc') asymmetry.
verify_reasoning:
VERIFIED REAL. In /Users/pat/tpw/turbulence/src/odas_tpw/perturb/pipeline.py, _prune_orphan_profile_ncs (lines 47-77) keeps any file whose name startswith one of prefixes = tuple(f"{s}_prof" for s in valid_stems) (line 64) and only unlinks files failing that prefix test (line 67). Profile NCs are named "{stem}_prof{NNN}.nc" (rsi/profile.py:137), and stems are free-form, derived from arbitrary .p file/dir names via _source_output_stem/_safe_stem_part (pipeline.py:28-44), which preserves underscores. Therefore a previously-processed-but-now-dropped file whose stem is "{livestem}_prof<...>" produces orphan files like "{livestem}_prof<...>_prof000.nc" that start with the live stem's "{livestem}_prof" prefix and are NOT pruned.

I reproduced it directly: live stem "cast" (from cast.p), dropped stem "cast_prof" (from cast_prof.p). With valid_stems={"cast"}, _prune_orphan_profile_ncs left BOTH cast_prof000.nc (live) and cast_prof_prof000.nc (orphan); pruned count = 0; orphan still present = True.

The kept orphan is then folded into the binned product: at lines 1915-1918 the prune runs on output_dirs["profiles"/"diss"/"chi"], then lines 1925/1936/1956 glob those same dirs with "*.nc" and pass every path into bin_by_depth/bin_diss/bin_chi (binning.py:367+, 592+, 612+), which consume the passed list with no stem re-filtering. So a surviving orphan is silently merged into the combo — the exact corruption the prune was meant to prevent. This contradicts the docstring claim (lines 57-60) that keeping an orphan is harmless.

Severity minor: real silent-data-corruption path, but the trigger requires an adversarial filename collision where one input stem is a "{otherstem}_prof..." extension of another, plus a changed input set across two runs against the same content-hashed stage dir. Under the project's normal naming (e.g. ARCTERX_..._SN479_0001.p) it does not trigger. The suggested fix (parse with r"^(?P<stem>.+)_prof\d+\.nc$" and keep iff parsed stem is in valid_stems) is correct and also closes the "*_prof*.nc" prune-glob vs "*.nc" bin-glob asymmetry. || Confirmed against current code on branch fix/audit-pr73.

TRACE:
- /Users/pat/tpw/turbulence/src/odas_tpw/perturb/pipeline.py:64-67 — `_prune_orphan_profile_ncs` builds `prefixes = tuple(f"{s}_prof" for s in valid_stems)` and keeps any file where `nc.name.startswith(prefixes)`. This is a PREFIX test, not exact-stem membership.
- Per-profile NCs are named `{stem}_prof{pi:03d}.nc` (rsi/profile.py:137).
- Stems come from `_source_output_stem` (pipeline.py:34-44), which for a flat input dir is just the bare `.p` stem run through `_safe_stem_part`. `_SAFE_STEM_RE = r"[^A-Za-z0-9_.-]+"` keeps underscores, so a file `cast_prof.p` yields the legal stem `cast_prof`.
- Reproduction (verified by running the actual regex/logic): live `cast.p` -> stem `cast` -> file `cast_prof000.nc`; dropped `cast_prof.p` -> stem `cast_prof` -> orphan `cast_prof_prof000.nc`. With `valid_stems={'cast'}`, `prefixes=('cast_prof',)`, BOTH `cast_prof000.nc` and `cast_prof_prof000.nc` pass `startswith('cast_prof')` -> orphan is KEPT.
- The orphan then leaks into the binned/combo product: binning globs `output_dirs[stage].glob("*.nc")` at pipeline.py:1925, 1936, 1956 — exactly the corruption the prune was meant to prevent. So the docstring's claim (pipeline.py:56-60) that the only error direction is "keeping a harmless stray orphan" is wrong; the kept orphan is folded into the binned output.

ALREADY-FIXED CHECK: No. Current branch still has `startswith(prefixes)` at pipeline.py:67 (git log shows the prune commit eaf2d9b introduced this prefix logic and no later commit changed it).

The codebase already contains the correct primitive: binning.py:23,26-28 defines `_PROF_SUFFIX_RE = re.compile(r"_prof\d+$")` and `_source_stem()` which strips exactly one trailing `_prof\d+` to recover the source stem. Using `_source_stem(nc) in valid_stems` (verified: yields `cast` for the live file -> keep, `cast_prof` for the orphan -> prune) fixes it exactly, and also resolves the glob asymmetry (prune globs `*_prof*.nc` while binning globs `*.nc`).

SEVERITY minor: the failure is genuine but needs both a `.p` filename whose stem matches `{livestem}_prof...` and a cross-run input-set reduction. The real ARCTERX stems (ARCTERX_Thompson_2025_SN479_NNNN) never contain `_prof`, so it won't trigger on the shipped dataset, but it defeats the documented guarantee of the prune function for adversarial/free-form input names.

title: Empty-data_vars binning re-run reuses a stale binned.nc, so the combo can publish data from a prior input set
description: The binned dir (config-hashed, reused across runs) holds binned.nc. Each stage writes binned.nc only when ds.data_vars is non-empty (e.g. 'if ds.data_vars: ds.to_netcdf(prof_binned_dir / "binned.nc")'). On a re-run where the per-profile NCs still exist (so prof_ncs is non-empty and the stage runs) but binning yields an empty Dataset — e.g. every value was QC-dropped to NaN or all bins are empty — the new (empty) result is NOT written, so a binned.nc left by a previous run on a different/larger input set survives. _run_combo then passes the (non-None) binned dir to make_combo, which globs '*.nc' and re-publishes the stale binned.nc into the combo. The prune fix only touches the per-profile dirs, not the binned dirs, so it does not cover this path.
evidence: pipeline.py:1986 'if ds.data_vars: ds.to_netcdf(prof_binned_dir / "binned.nc")' (same pattern at 2000 for diss, 2014 for chi) — no else-branch removes a pre-existing binned.nc. prof_binned_dir is only None when prof_ncs is empty (line 1926 'if prof_ncs:'); when prof_ncs is non-empty but binning returns empty data_vars, the dir is non-None and stale binned.nc remains. _run_combo target loop (pipeline.py:2094-2104) calls make_combo on the non-None dir; combo.py:72 sorted(binned_dir.glob('*.nc')) picks up the stale file.
suggested_fix: Before writing each binned.nc, unlink any existing binned.nc in the dir (or always write, even when empty, and have make_combo skip zero-variable inputs). At minimum, when ds.data_vars is empty, delete a pre-existing binned.nc so the combo does not resurrect stale data.
verify_reasoning:
VERIFIED REAL against current code on this branch. Every link in the chain holds:

1. Binned dir is config-hashed and reused across runs. resolve_output_dir (config_base.py:218-229) returns an existing dir whenever a hash-matching signature exists. compute_hash/canonicalize (config_base.py:169-195) hash CONFIG PARAMS ONLY, never the input .p file set. So a re-run with the same binning config but a different/smaller input set resolves to the same profiles_binned_NN/ (and diss_binned_NN/, chi_binned_NN/) dir, where a prior binned.nc may live.

2. Binning can return an empty Dataset while prof_ncs is non-empty. bin_by_depth returns bare xr.Dataset() at binning.py:434-435 when every _bin_scan_worker returns None — which happens for a profile NC with no depth var (binning.py:226-227) or all-NaN depth (binning.py:356-357). bin_by_time returns xr.Dataset() at binning.py:587-588 when no file has a usable finite time coord. bin_diss/bin_chi delegate to these. I confirmed empirically that both a bare xr.Dataset() and a coords-only Dataset have FALSY data_vars.

3. The write guard skips writing but never deletes the stale file. pipeline.py:1986-1987 (profiles), 2000-2001 (diss), 2014-2015 (chi) are all `if ds.data_vars: ds.to_netcdf(<dir>/"binned.nc")` with no else. A pre-existing binned.nc survives.

4. The prune fix does not cover this path. _prune_orphan_profile_ncs (pipeline.py:47-77) is invoked only on output_dirs["profiles"|"diss"|"chi"] — the per-profile *_NN dirs (pipeline.py:1916-1918) — never on the *_binned_NN dirs. It also only globs *_prof*.nc and so would not match binned.nc even if aimed at a binned dir. The function's own docstring describes exactly this orphan-folds-into-combo failure mode, confirming the maintainers treat the same-dir-reuse-across-input-sets scenario as a real bug for the per-profile dirs while leaving the binned dirs uncovered.

5. _run_combo passes the non-None binned dir to make_combo (pipeline.py:2089-2104); make_combo globs sorted(binned_dir.glob('*.nc')) (combo.py:72) and re-publishes whatever it finds. End-to-end reproduction: Run 1 writes binned.nc (4 profiles of epsilonMean); Run 2 (empty binning) leaves it untouched; the combo glob then re-publishes the stale 4-profile data — proving stale data from a prior input set can leak into the combo.

REFUTATION ATTEMPTS that failed to break it: (a) The hash does not include the input file set, so a changed input set does NOT route to a fresh dir — the very gap the per-profile prune was added to plug. (b) The empty-binning path is genuinely reachable (missing/NaN depth or no finite time), not theoretical. (c) The binned dirs are distinct paths (profiles_binned_NN vs profiles_NN) from the pruned dirs, so the prune cannot incidentally clean them.

Severity minor: requires the narrow conjunction of a config-identical re-run on a reduced/changed input set (reusing the binned dir) AND that re-run's binning collapsing to empty data_vars. Genuine silent stale-data corruption when it occurs, but low frequency. Suggested fix is sound: unlink any pre-existing binned.nc before/instead of the guarded write (or always write and have make_combo skip zero-variable inputs).

Relevant files: /Users/pat/tpw/turbulence/src/odas_tpw/perturb/pipeline.py (lines 47-77, 1911-1918, 1986-2015, 2089-2104); /Users/pat/tpw/turbulence/src/odas_tpw/perturb/combo.py (line 72); /Users/pat/tpw/turbulence/src/odas_tpw/perturb/binning.py (lines 226-227, 356-357, 434-435, 587-588, 605-626); /Users/pat/tpw/turbulence/src/odas_tpw/config_base.py (lines 169-195, 218-229). || Confirmed against current code on fix/audit-pr73.

TRACE (all line refs current):
1. Binned dirs are config-hashed and reused across runs. resolve_output_dir (config_base.py:199-235) returns an existing {prefix}_NN dir when a matching .params_sha256_<hash> exists. The hash covers section params + upstream chain (_upstream_for, pipeline.py:95+) but NOT the resolved input-.p-file set. So two runs with identical config but different .p lists resolve to the SAME prof_binned/diss_binned/chi_binned dir.

2. prof_binned_dir is non-None whenever prof_ncs is non-empty (pipeline.py:1925-1934). Same for diss (1936-1946) and chi (1955-1964).

3. Each stage writes binned.nc only under `if ds.data_vars:` with NO else branch (pipeline.py:1986-1987, 2000-2001, 2014-2015). grep confirms the only unlink in pipeline.py is line 69 inside _prune_orphan_profile_ncs.

4. The binning call CAN return an empty-data_vars Dataset while prof_ncs is non-empty: bin_by_depth returns a bare xr.Dataset() when saw_any is False (binning.py:434-435), which happens if every _bin_scan_worker returns None (binning.py:349-358: snapshot None, or all-NaN depth). Even when saw_any is True, if every per-profile res["vars"] is empty and no scalar is finite, data_vars stays empty (binning.py:494-503) and `ds.data_vars` is falsy. So the guard's else path is reachable.

5. In that case the new (empty) result is not written, so a binned.nc left by a prior run on a larger/different input set survives in the reused dir.

6. _run_combo (pipeline.py:2089-2104) iterates targets including prof_binned_dir and only skips when `src is None or not src.exists()`. The dir is non-None and exists, so make_combo is called. combo.py:72 does sorted(binned_dir.glob("*.nc")) and re-publishes the stale binned.nc into the combo.

NOT ALREADY FIXED: The prune fix (_prune_orphan_profile_ncs, pipeline.py:47-77) operates only on per-profile stage dirs (output_dirs["profiles"/"diss"/"chi"], lines 1916-1918) and globs `*_prof*.nc` (line 66), so it can never touch binned.nc. No code clears/overwrites a stale binned.nc when the current run bins to empty.

SEVERITY minor: trigger is narrow (requires a prior run on a different/larger input set followed by a same-config re-run whose binning yields empty data — e.g. all profiles QC-dropped to NaN depth/values), and the result is self-consistent-but-stale published data rather than a crash, units corruption, or silently wrong physics. Suggested fix: when ds.data_vars is empty (or unconditionally before each write), unlink any pre-existing binned.nc in the dir so the combo cannot resurrect stale data.

title: Chi L4 produces NaN (silent window drop) on an all-NaN-temperature window, where epsilon substitutes 10 degC
description: The audit fix changed T_means to np.nanmean so a single NaN temperature sample no longer poisons a whole window, and its comment claims this 'mirrors the epsilon side's NaN tolerance (scor160/l4.py)'. But it only mirrored the single-NaN tolerance, not the epsilon side's actual all-NaN guard. The epsilon path (scor160/l4.py:154-160) explicitly detects non-finite window temperatures and substitutes 10 degC before computing viscosity (with a warning). The chi path has no such fallback: when a window's temperature samples are ALL NaN (e.g. a fully despiked / surface segment of temp_fast[0], which l2_chi.temp can be), np.nanmean returns NaN, which flows into visc()/visc35() and yields nu=NaN, and l3_chi appends nu=NaN for that window. In chi/l4_chi.py nu=NaN makes the Batchelor/Kraichnan fit produce NaN chi, and the window is silently discarded at l4_chi.py:276 (good = np.isfinite(chi[:,j]) & (chi[:,j]>0)). So an all-NaN-temp window yields NO chi estimate on the chi side, but WOULD yield a finite estimate (using 10 degC) on the epsilon side — an inconsistent, silent data loss that the fix's own comment claims to have addressed.
evidence: Proven: visc35(np.nan) -> nan and visc(np.nan, 34.5, 10.0) -> nan (run in repl). l2_chi.temp can be the despiked fast thermistor (l2_chi.py:135-140: temp_slow = temp_fast[0] when l1.temp.size != n_time), which carries NaN from despiking; an all-NaN window then makes np.nanmean(l3_chi.py:156) return NaN. chi/l4_chi.py:100 reads nu=l3_chi.nu[j]=NaN and l4_chi.py:276 drops the NaN result. By contrast scor160/l4.py:154-160 substitutes 10 degC for non-finite temp (this branch's own test_main_l3_l4 emits 'Non-finite window temperature(s); using 10 degC for viscosity in those windows'), so the two pipelines disagree on the same input.
suggested_fix: After computing T_means (and sal_means) in _process_section_chi, apply the same non-finite fallback the epsilon side uses: T_means = np.where(np.isfinite(T_means), T_means, 10.0) (and warn). Optionally guard sal_means the same way. This makes chi mirror scor160/l4.py:152-160 fully, not just the nanmean part.
verify_reasoning:
The core code defect is real and present in current code: chi/l3_chi.py:156 computes T_means = np.nanmean(l2_chi.temp[indices], axis=1) with NO all-NaN fallback, whereas the epsilon side (scor160/l4.py:154-160) explicitly substitutes 10 degC for non-finite window temperatures (with a warning). I verified the NaN propagation mechanically: visc35(nan)=nan and visc(nan,34.5,10)=nan, np.nanmean of an all-NaN slice = nan, so an all-NaN-temperature window yields nu=NaN. That NaN flows into the Batchelor/Kraichnan fit (l4_chi.py:100 reads nu=l3_chi.nu[j]) producing NaN chi, which is then silently dropped at l4_chi.py:276 (good = np.isfinite(chi[:,j]) & (chi[:,j]>0)). Meanwhile the same all-NaN input on the epsilon side produces a finite estimate via the 10 degC fallback. So the asymmetry/silent-data-loss the finding describes is genuine, and the audit comment at l3_chi.py:153-155 ('Mirrors the epsilon side's NaN tolerance') overclaims — it only added single-NaN tolerance (nanmean), not the all-NaN guard. The suggested fix (T_means = np.where(np.isfinite(T_means), T_means, 10.0) with a warning) is correct and is NOT present in current code, so already_fixed=false.

However, I downgrade severity from major to minor because the finding's stated trigger is incorrect and the bug is latent rather than demonstrably reachable on real data:
(1) despike (scor160/despike.py:146-166) does NOT introduce NaN — it replaces spike neighborhoods with a local mean or 0.0, and lines 81-85 only *preserve* pre-existing NaN. So the claim that 'despiked temp_fast[0] carries NaN from despiking' is wrong; despiking cannot create the all-NaN window.
(2) In the production rsi chi pipeline the trigger branch the finding relies on (l2_chi.py:137-138, temp_slow = temp_fast[0]) is essentially unreachable: _build_l1data_from_channels (helpers.py:436) always sets L1Data.temp = T_fast[s_fast:e_fast] with size == n_time, and T_fast = np.interp(t_fast, t_slow, T_slow) (helpers.py:331). Therefore l2_chi.py:135 (if l1.temp.size == l1.n_time) takes the FIRST branch (temp_slow = l1.temp), never the temp_fast[0] fallback. Moreover np.interp produces no NaN (it clamps to endpoints), and data['T'] from the physical-unit channel converters is finite for normal .p files, so the slow temperature feeding l2_chi.temp has no all-NaN segment under realistic input. The all-NaN path is only reachable for hand-constructed or scor160-style L1Data that supply a mismatched/empty temp alongside NaN-bearing temp_fast.

Net: a real, present defensive/consistency defect (missing all-NaN guard, asymmetric with epsilon, comment overstates parity) worth fixing, but it is latent — not the demonstrated data-corruption path the finding's evidence claims, and the cited reproduction mechanism (despike-introduced NaN via temp_fast[0]) does not actually hold in the production pipeline. Relevant files: /Users/pat/tpw/turbulence/src/odas_tpw/chi/l3_chi.py:156, /Users/pat/tpw/turbulence/src/odas_tpw/chi/l4_chi.py:100 and :276, /Users/pat/tpw/turbulence/src/odas_tpw/scor160/l4.py:154-160, /Users/pat/tpw/turbulence/src/odas_tpw/chi/l2_chi.py:135-140, /Users/pat/tpw/turbulence/src/odas_tpw/scor160/despike.py:146-166, /Users/pat/tpw/turbulence/src/odas_tpw/rsi/helpers.py:331,436. || CONFIRMED REAL and NOT fixed on this branch. Code trace: src/odas_tpw/chi/l3_chi.py:156 computes T_means = np.nanmean(l2_chi.temp[indices], axis=1) with NO subsequent finite-guard. For a window whose temperature samples are ALL NaN, np.nanmean returns NaN (reproduced). That NaN flows to l3_chi.py:164-172 visc(T_means[i], sal, P) / visc35(T_means[i]), both of which return NaN for a NaN input (reproduced: visc35(nan)=nan, visc(nan,34.5,5)=nan). The NaN nu is stored unconditionally at l3_chi.py:214 (acc.nu.append(nu_all[w])), read by l4_chi.py:100 (nu = l3_chi.nu[j]), and the resulting NaN chi is silently discarded at l4_chi.py:276 (good = np.isfinite(chi[:,j]) & (chi[:,j]>0)). By contrast the epsilon side at scor160/l4.py:154-160 explicitly detects non-finite window temperatures and substitutes 10 degC (with a warning), yielding a finite viscosity (reproduced: 1.35e-6) and a finite epsilon estimate on the SAME input. So the two pipelines diverge on identical all-NaN-temp windows: chi silently drops them, epsilon keeps them. NaN temperature is a genuinely anticipated condition here — the code itself documents it at scor160/io.py:392-395 and 624-627, and unlike speed (NaN-scrubbed at helpers.py:325-328) the temperature arrays (T_fast at helpers.py:331, temp=T_fast[...] at helpers.py:436) are NOT NaN-scrubbed, so NaN in the slow temp source propagates through np.interp into l2_chi.temp.

The l3_chi.py:153-155 comment ('Mirrors the epsilon side's NaN tolerance (scor160/l4.py)') overstates the parity: it mirrors only the single-NaN nanmean tolerance, not the epsilon side's all-NaN 10-degC fallback.

One detail in the finding's EVIDENCE is inaccurate but non-load-bearing: it claims despike() introduces NaN into temp_fast[0]. I read src/odas_tpw/scor160/despike.py — despike only replaces spikes with local means or 0.0 (line 158-166) and never assigns NaN, so that specific mechanism is wrong. The defect still stands via the slow-temp NaN path described above; the finding's conclusion is correct even though its named trigger is not.

Severity downgraded from the reported 'major' to 'minor': the trigger requires an ENTIRE window of temperature to be NaN (any single finite sample defeats it via nanmean), an edge case; the impact is silent loss of chi for those specific windows plus an inconsistency with epsilon, not corruption of otherwise-valid chi values. Suggested fix is correct and low-risk: after l3_chi.py:156 (and for sal_means), apply T_means = np.where(np.isfinite(T_means), T_means, 10.0) with a warning, fully mirroring scor160/l4.py:152-160.

title: Chi L3 speed window uses nan_to_num (collapses whole window to 0.01 m/s) instead of nanmean, inconsistent with the temperature handling on the same lines
description: On the very same lines, the fix treats temperature with np.nanmean (a single NaN sample tolerated) but treats speed with np.mean(...)+np.nan_to_num(nan=0.01). Because np.mean of a window containing even ONE NaN sample is NaN, nan_to_num then collapses that window's mean speed to the 0.01 m/s floor. A real ~0.6 m/s window becomes 0.01 m/s — a ~60x underestimate — which corrupts that window's wavenumber axis (K_all = F_const/speed_means, l3_chi.py:174), the spectral correction (clean_spectra *= speed_means, l3_chi.py:178-182), fp07_tau_batch(speed_means) (l3_chi.py:186) and gradT_noise_batch(...speed_means...) (l3_chi.py:198-205), silently mis-scaling chi for that window. The fix's own comment for temperature ('a single NaN sample must not poison the whole window') argues for nanmean here too. In the standard rsi chi path prepare_profiles now NaN-scrubs speed upstream so pspd_rel is rarely NaN at L3, which is why this is minor rather than major, but the divergent handling is a latent corruption if any NaN speed reaches L2ChiData (e.g. a caller that builds L2ChiData directly, or l2_chi.py:121 spd_safe which does not nan_to_num).
evidence: Reproduced: spd=[0.6]*10 with spd[3]=NaN, diss_length=5, starts=[0,5] -> current speed_means=[0.01, 0.6]; the consistent nanmean gives [0.6, 0.6]. So one NaN sample drops a 0.6 m/s window to the 0.01 floor while the temperature on the same line would survive via nanmean.
suggested_fix: Use the same nanmean tolerance for speed: speed_means = np.maximum(np.nanmean(np.abs(l2_chi.pspd_rel[indices]), axis=1), 0.01), then handle an all-NaN window with the floor (e.g. np.nan_to_num after nanmean). This keeps speed and temperature window handling consistent.
verify_reasoning:
VERIFIED REAL (latent, minor). At src/odas_tpw/chi/l3_chi.py:148-150 speed uses np.maximum(np.nan_to_num(np.mean(np.abs(pspd_rel[indices]),axis=1),nan=0.01),0.01); the SAME logical block at line 156 uses np.nanmean for temperature. Because np.mean of any window containing one NaN is NaN, nan_to_num then collapses that window's mean speed to the 0.01 m/s floor. Reproduced exactly: spd=[0.6]*10 with spd[3]=NaN, diss_length=5, two windows -> current speed_means=[0.01,0.6] vs nanmean [0.6,0.6] = 60x underestimate for the contaminated window. The l3 code's own comment (lines 153-155) argues a single NaN sample 'must not poison the whole window' for temperature, so the divergent treatment is internally inconsistent. The mis-scaled speed_means propagates into K_all=F_const/speed_means (line 174), the spectral correction clean_spectra*=speed_means (lines 178-182), fp07_tau_batch(speed_means) (line 186), and gradT_noise_batch(...,speed_means,...) (lines 198-205), silently mis-scaling that window's chi. SEVERITY minor / NOT active in default path: rsi chi goes through helpers.prepare_profiles which NaN-scrubs speed at the choke point (helpers.py:325-327, np.nan_to_num(...,nan=speed_cutout)), so pspd_rel reaching L3 is NaN-free there. But l2_chi.py:121 spd_safe=np.maximum(np.abs(pspd_rel),0.01) does NOT scrub NaN and l2_chi.py:149 stores pspd_rel raw, and the scor160 NetCDF readers (io.py:621/660) read PSPD_REL raw with no NaN scrub, so a caller that builds L2ChiData/L2RefData directly can carry a NaN into L3 and trigger the 60x corruption. Genuine, internally-inconsistent NaN handling and a latent data-corruption path; correctly minor because gated behind the upstream scrub in the standard pipeline. Not fixed on this branch. Suggested fix: use np.nanmean for speed too, then floor (e.g. np.nan_to_num after nanmean) so an all-NaN window still falls back to 0.01 while a single-NaN window survives, matching the temperature handling. || CONFIRMED REAL and NOT already fixed on the current branch. At src/odas_tpw/chi/l3_chi.py:148-150 the per-window speed mean is computed as np.maximum(np.nan_to_num(np.mean(np.abs(l2_chi.pspd_rel[indices]), axis=1), nan=0.01), 0.01). Because np.mean over a window containing even ONE NaN sample returns NaN, nan_to_num then collapses that entire window's mean speed to the 0.01 m/s floor. On the very next group of lines (153-156) the temperature mean uses np.nanmean, which tolerates a single NaN sample — exactly the divergent handling the finding describes, with the code's own comment ("a single NaN sample ... must not poison the whole window") arguing for nanmean on speed too.

Reproduction confirmed exactly as reported: spd=[0.6]*10 with spd[3]=NaN, diss_length=5, starts=[0,5] gives current speed_means=[0.01, 0.6] whereas the consistent np.nanmean gives [0.6, 0.6] — a single NaN drops a real ~0.6 m/s window to the 0.01 floor, a ~60x underestimate.

Downstream impact trace verified: speed_means feeds K_all = F_const/speed_means (l3_chi.py:174), the spectral correction clean_spectra *= speed_means (l3_chi.py:178-182), fp07_tau_batch(speed_means) (l3_chi.py:186) and gradT_noise_batch(..., speed_means, ...) (l3_chi.py:198-205). A collapsed window speed therefore silently mis-scales that window's wavenumber axis, FP07 transfer, noise floor, and ultimately chi.

Severity minor is justified: in the standard rsi chi path, prepare_profiles NaN-scrubs speed at a choke point (src/odas_tpw/rsi/helpers.py:325-328, np.nan_to_num(speed_fast, nan=speed_cutout)), so pspd_rel arriving at L3 is normally NaN-free. The exposure is latent — a caller that constructs L2ChiData directly, or the fact that l2_chi.py:121 spd_safe = np.maximum(np.abs(l2.pspd_rel), 0.01) does not nan_to_num and l2_chi.py:149 copies pspd_rel through unmodified, so any NaN in the input speed survives to L3. Not a style preference: it is a genuine, if low-probability, silent data-corruption inconsistency.

Suggested fix is correct: use np.nanmean for speed to match the temperature handling, e.g. speed_means = np.maximum(np.nan_to_num(np.nanmean(np.abs(l2_chi.pspd_rel[indices]), axis=1), nan=0.01), 0.01), which still floors an all-NaN window to 0.01 but no longer lets a single NaN sample poison an otherwise-valid window.

title: Epsilon L3 sibling speed path has the same un-fixed NaN bug: max(W,0.05) does not floor a NaN, propagating NaN into the wavenumber axis
description: The audit fix added a NaN-scrub for the chi-side per-window speed (l3_chi.py:148-150) but did NOT touch the analogous epsilon-side code, which is the sibling with the original bug. At l3.py:182 the window speed is all_speed.append(np.mean(l2.pspd_rel[s:e])) (no nan_to_num). At l3.py:201-202 the guard is W = all_speed[-1]; W = max(W, 0.05). Python's max(nan, 0.05) returns nan (nan>0.05 is False), so the 0.05 floor is INEFFECTIVE against NaN. A NaN window speed then makes kcyc_w = F/W (l3.py:203) all-NaN and scales the wavenumber spectrum by NaN (sh_k = sh_freq_spec[w]*W, l3.py:209), silently NaN-ing that window's epsilon. As with the chi case, prepare_profiles now NaN-scrubs speed upstream so NaN rarely reaches l2.pspd_rel on the standard rsi path, hence minor; but the epsilon sibling was left with a floor that cannot protect against NaN whereas the chi side got a real scrub — asymmetric and the kind of un-fixed sibling the audit was asked to find.
evidence: Verified in repl: max(np.nan, 0.05) == nan (Python builtin max keeps the first arg when the comparison is False). So l3.py:202 W=max(W,0.05) leaves NaN intact, and l3.py:203 kcyc_w=F/W becomes NaN. Contrast l3_chi.py:148-150 which explicitly nan_to_num's the chi-side window speed.
suggested_fix: Mirror the chi-side scrub: all_speed.append(np.nan_to_num(np.mean(l2.pspd_rel[s:e]), nan=0.05)), or replace W=max(W,0.05) with W=max(np.nan_to_num(W, nan=0.05), 0.05) so a NaN window speed falls back to the floor instead of NaN-ing the window's epsilon.
verify_reasoning:
VERIFIED REAL (with two citation corrections). The mechanism is correct and present in current code on this branch.

Epsilon path (scor160/l3.py):
- Line 182: all_speed.append(np.mean(l2.pspd_rel[s:e])) — np.mean returns np.float64; if the window slice contains any NaN the result is NaN (no nanmean, no scrub).
- Line 201-202: W = all_speed[-1]; W = max(W, 0.05). Confirmed in repl that Python builtin max(np.nan, 0.05) == nan (nan>0.05 is False so max keeps the first positional arg), so the 0.05 floor is INEFFECTIVE against NaN.
- Line 203/209: kcyc_w = F/W and sh_k = sh_freq_spec[w]*W therefore become all-NaN, silently NaN-ing that window's wavenumber spectrum and downstream epsilon. End-to-end repl reproduction confirms all three (W=nan, kcyc_w all-nan, sh_k all-nan).

Chi sibling DOES have the scrub (so the asymmetry is genuine): the correct path is src/odas_tpw/chi/l3_chi.py:148-150 (NOT scor160/l3_chi.py as the finding cited — that file does not exist), using np.maximum(np.nan_to_num(np.mean(np.abs(l2_chi.pspd_rel[indices]), axis=1), nan=0.01), 0.01). The floor value is 0.01 (the finding's evidence block mistakenly wrote 0.05 in the chi contrast), but the substantive point — chi uses NumPy maximum wrapped around nan_to_num while epsilon uses bare Python max — is exactly right.

Exposure / severity assessment confirms MINOR:
- The scor160 epsilon speed pipeline has NO floor or NaN-scrub anywhere: io.py reads PSPD_REL via np.asarray(pspd_var[:], dtype=float64) with no scrub; l2.py applies _filtfilt_nan which explicitly RESTORES NaN positions to NaN (l2.py:273 result[nans]=np.nan) and there is no np.maximum(pspd, speed_min) in process_l2; so a NaN in reference/benchmark speed reaches l2.pspd_rel and then l3.py:202. This path is reachable via scor160/cli.py:126 process_l3 on ATOMIX/Epsifish/MSS reference NetCDF.
- The rsi production epsilon path (rsi/dissipation.py:188 process_l3) is protected: prepare_profiles -> helpers.py:326 np.nan_to_num(speed, nan=speed_cutout) floors/scrubs speed before L1Data is built, so no NaN positions exist for _filtfilt_nan to preserve. The finding's own minor rationale (prepare_profiles scrubs on the standard rsi path) is accurate.

Not already fixed: l3.py:182 and 201-202 still use bare np.mean and Python builtin max on this branch. The fix is to mirror the chi side, e.g. W = max(np.nan_to_num(W, nan=0.05), 0.05), or scrub at append time.

Net: genuine un-fixed sibling defect (the exact class the audit was asked to find), low real-world probability because benchmark data is normally clean and the production path is protected upstream — hence minor. || CONFIRMED REAL and NOT already fixed on this branch. The chi-side file path in the finding is wrong (it is src/odas_tpw/chi/l3_chi.py:145-150, not scor160/l3_chi.py — that file does not exist), but the substance is correct and the epsilon-side citation (scor160/l3.py:182,201-202) is accurate.

Verified facts:
1. scor160/l3.py:182 `all_speed.append(np.mean(l2.pspd_rel[s:e]))` has no NaN scrub; np.mean of a window containing NaN returns NaN (repl-verified).
2. scor160/l3.py:201-202 `W = all_speed[-1]; W = max(W, 0.05)`. Python builtin `max(np.nan, 0.05) == nan` (repl-verified: the comparison nan>0.05 is False so max returns the first arg). The 0.05 floor is therefore ineffective against NaN.
3. l3.py:203 `kcyc_w = F / W` and l3.py:209 `sh_k = sh_freq_spec[w] * W` then go all-NaN (repl-verified F/nan -> all nan), silently NaN-ing that window's epsilon. L4 at l4.py:180 repeats the same non-protective `max(W,0.05)` but the window is already dead from L3.
4. The chi sibling at chi/l3_chi.py:148-150 DOES scrub: `np.maximum(np.nan_to_num(np.mean(...), nan=0.01), 0.01)`. (Note: chi/l2_chi.py:121 `np.maximum(np.abs(...), 0.01)` does NOT protect — np.maximum(nan,0.01)=nan, repl-verified — so the chi side's real protection is precisely the nan_to_num the epsilon side lacks.) The asymmetry is genuine.

Reachability (why minor, matching the finding's own rationale): On the standard rsi epsilon path (dissipation.py -> prepare_profiles), speed is NaN-scrubbed upstream at helpers.py:325-328 (`np.nan_to_num(..., nan=speed_cutout)` then `np.maximum`), and re-floored in _build_l1data_from_channels (helpers.py:399), so l1.pspd_rel/l2.pspd_rel are NaN-free; l2.py uses _filtfilt_nan which preserves but never adds NaN. So NaN rarely reaches l2.pspd_rel on the standard path. HOWEVER the failure is still reachable on the scor160 benchmark / direct-NetCDF path (io.py): when PSPD_REL is present on a different time grid it is interpolated with bare `np.interp` (io.py:362,365-369) with no NaN scrub, and `np.interp` propagates NaN (repl-verified); a NaN-bearing PSPD_REL (or NaN pressure feeding _compute_speed's raw filtfilt at l2.py:190) then lands a NaN window mean at l3.py:182, which the l3.py:202 floor cannot catch, silently producing NaN epsilon for that window. Consequence is silent data loss for affected windows, not a crash or a wrong-but-plausible value, hence minor. Suggested fix is correct: mirror the chi scrub, e.g. `W = max(np.nan_to_num(W, nan=0.05), 0.05)` at l3.py:201-202 (and similarly l4.py:180).

title: Chi all-NaN window emits an unhandled RuntimeWarning ('Mean of empty slice') from np.nanmean
description: np.nanmean over a window whose temperature (or salinity) samples are all NaN emits a RuntimeWarning('Mean of empty slice') and returns NaN. The epsilon side avoids this by substituting 10 degC and warning explicitly; the chi side has no warning suppression or fallback around the nanmean calls, so processing a file with any all-NaN-temp/all-NaN-salinity window spews numpy RuntimeWarnings to the user. This is the surface symptom of the same missing all-NaN guard as the first finding.
evidence: Reproduced: np.nanmean(np.array([nan,nan,nan])) raises RuntimeWarning('Mean of empty slice') under warnings.simplefilter('error'). l3_chi.py:156 and :161 call np.nanmean with no surrounding errstate/warnings handling.
suggested_fix: Wrap the nanmean calls in `with warnings.catch_warnings(): warnings.simplefilter('ignore', RuntimeWarning)` (or compute via a count-of-finite guard) and apply the 10 degC fallback from the first finding so all-NaN windows are handled deliberately rather than via numpy's NaN+warning.
verify_reasoning:
VERIFIED REAL (nit). All factual claims hold against the current code on this branch.

Code trace:
- src/odas_tpw/chi/l3_chi.py:156 `T_means = np.nanmean(l2_chi.temp[indices], axis=1)` and :161 `sal_means = np.nanmean(np.asarray(salinity)[indices], axis=1)` are inside `_process_section_chi` (def at line 84). There is no surrounding `warnings.catch_warnings`/`simplefilter` and no finite/all-NaN fallback. grep over the file confirms the only warnings-related construct is the `np.errstate(divide=..., invalid=...)` at line 264, which is in a DIFFERENT function (`process_l3_chi`, def at 222) and out of scope for the nanmean calls.

Reproduction (numpy 2.4.3, the installed version):
- `np.nanmean(np.array([nan,nan,nan]))` emits `RuntimeWarning('Mean of empty slice')` and returns NaN.
- Crucially, I also verified that `np.errstate(divide='ignore', invalid='ignore')` does NOT suppress this warning — it originates from Python's `warnings` module, not numpy's FP-error machinery. So even if the line-264 errstate were in scope it would not help.
- The pytest `filterwarnings` in pyproject.toml:90-93 only ignores the two `get_diss()/get_chi() is deprecated` DeprecationWarnings, so this RuntimeWarning is suppressed neither in production nor in the test gate. No package-level global suppression exists for this code path.

Epsilon-side comparison confirmed: scor160/l4.py:152-160 explicitly substitutes 10 degC and emits a deliberate `warnings.warn(...)` for non-finite window temperatures. The chi side has no equivalent guard, so it is genuinely inconsistent.

Adversarial scrutiny / why only a nit:
- The warning fires ONLY for an *all*-NaN window. A single NaN sample (the scenario the inline comment at 153-155 motivates) is tolerated by nanmean WITHOUT any warning, so this does not occur in routine processing.
- It can occur in practice: if the slow temperature channel (l2_chi.temp, sourced from l1.temp in l2_chi.py:135-140) is entirely NaN over a window-length span (dead/disconnected slow thermistor or a data gap), every window in that span will spew the RuntimeWarning, and `visc(NaN,...)`/`visc35(NaN)` -> NaN nu -> NaN chi for those windows.
- Impact is cosmetic/diagnostic only: NaN chi for all-NaN-temperature windows is the CORRECT result; the defect is merely that it is communicated via an opaque numpy warning rather than a deliberate one, and is inconsistent with the epsilon side. No silent data/units corruption, no crash (visc35(nan) returns nan, does not raise). This matches the proposed "nit" severity.

Suggested fix is reasonable: wrap the two nanmean calls in `with warnings.catch_warnings(): warnings.simplefilter('ignore', RuntimeWarning)` and/or apply the 10 degC fallback used on the epsilon side so all-NaN windows are handled deliberately. || Verified against current code on the branch. src/odas_tpw/chi/l3_chi.py:156 calls np.nanmean(l2_chi.temp[indices], axis=1) and :161 calls np.nanmean(np.asarray(salinity)[indices], axis=1). A grep of the whole function confirms there is NO catch_warnings, simplefilter, or finite-count/all-NaN guard around these calls; the only np.errstate in the file is at line 264 and covers an unrelated fd_correction computation.

I reproduced that np.nanmean over an all-NaN slice emits RuntimeWarning('Mean of empty slice') to stderr under default Python warning filters and returns NaN. I also confirmed that np.errstate(divide/invalid='ignore') does NOT suppress this warning (numpy raises it via warnings.warn, not the FP-error system), so the line-264 errstate gives no protection even if it were in scope. pyproject.toml filterwarnings (lines 90-93) only ignore two specific DeprecationWarnings, so this RuntimeWarning is not suppressed project-wide and would surface in normal runs and tests.

The asymmetry the finding cites is real: the epsilon side (scor160/l4.py:154-160) explicitly warns and substitutes 10 degC for non-finite window temperatures; the chi side has neither suppression nor a deliberate fallback. l2_chi.temp is l1.temp (raw slow temperature) or temp_fast[0]; an all-NaN window (full diss_length window of NaN temp or salinity, e.g. a sensor-dropout gap) is plausible though uncommon, and would trigger the warning.

Severity is nit (not the finding's only debatable point but I agree with 'nit'): the numerical result is already safe — an all-NaN window correctly yields NaN T_means -> NaN viscosity -> NaN chi for that window, which is the right answer for a window with no valid temperature/salinity. The sole defect is cosmetic: unhandled numpy RuntimeWarning spew to the user plus inconsistency with the epsilon side's explicit fallback. Not data corruption, not a crash. Suggested fix (wrap nanmean in catch_warnings ignoring RuntimeWarning and/or apply the epsilon-side 10 degC fallback) is appropriate.
